UnIt ONe reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Pop-Rock "Top 40"is music industry shorthand for the most popular; best-selling and most frequently broadcast pop songs Over the years, it has also come to represent a popular song from a particular era as well This play- along is a steady eighth-note dance-pop chart in the tradition of Maroon 5, Michael Jackson, Lady Gaga,etc:
Structure: Eight-Bar Phrase This chart employs a common structure; which is used in most rock and pop music: the eight-bar phrase: This passage is eight bars (measures) long, and each bar (after the first bar) is marked with time slashes: Furthermore; each phrase can be augmented with rehearsal letters and/or repeat signs as well: Ex 1.1
5
Time Slashes Time slashes not only mark the beats of a measure (i.e: 1,2,3 and 4), but they also indicate that a drummer plays "time" (grooves) as well: Ex 1.2
Once the groove has been notated, the time slashes are used in place of the notation; marking the time of the groove: Ex 13
Rehearsal Letters Within the eight-bar phrase; each additional section (new eight-bar phrase) is marked with a letter such as Aor B. Each rehearsal letter represents a different section of the song: Each letter (section) can also represent key musical attribute such as a different structure: Ex 1.4
B
Drum Performance

Unit Chapter 1
Repeat Signs Ex 1,.5 Start Repeat
End Repeat
Rather than writing two identical eight-bar sections, an additional usage of the same phrase is marked with both a start and end repeat sign.Therefore (upon reaching bar eight), you will immediately "repeat" (travel back) to bar one of that phrase: For example:
Ex 1.6
Tight Hi-Hat
Ensemble Figures Ensemble figures are rhythms that are played by the entire ensemble (band) These figures are written in the middle of the staff and take precedence over the time slashes In this particular chart; there are ensemble figures - on beat four of the eighth measure of Letter A, just prior to entering letter B. Ex 1,.7 Tight Hi-Hat
With this in mind, you will need to accentuate these rhythms along with the band: One solid method to orchestrate this figure is to simply play a cymbal crash in unison with the snare drum (which is already playing) on beat four: Ex 1.8 Tight Hi-Hat

B

B
2
There are also ensemble figures at the end of the chart on beats and 2 of the last measure_ Ex 1.11
Again, you will need to accentuate these rhythms with the band. One solid option would be to simply play the snare drum, floor tom and bass drum together on both beats of the last measure Ex 1.12
2
Tempo Marking A tempo marking indicates the tempo (speed) ofthe music; expressed in beats per minute (BPM): In addition, this is usually related to a specific note value: The example below indicates that a quarter note equals 120 beats per minute: Ex 1.13
=120

Unit Chapter 1
The Count-Off The count-offis the "four most important notes that you will ever play " These four counts (with stick clicks) set up the tempo for the song and next few minutes of your life. With this in mind, it is extremely important that you pay special attention to your count-off,to make sure that it is discernable to each member of the band-~and steady in tempo. To count off a unit's chart; you will begin two bars before the chart: Use the count: 1-2_ 1-2-3-4: Ex 1.14
Verbally Stick Clicks
"One"
"Two"
"One" "Two" "Three Four"
In context; the chart (with your count) will begin like this: Ex 1.15
"One"
"Two"
"One' "Two" "Three' Four"
Count in:
Tight Hi-Hat
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0135.PNG ---
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Just as the chart has a particular groove; phrase length and chord progression; it will also have a particular performance approach and drum sound It is not enough to merely play the correct patterns Rather; you must play the correct patterns with the correct sound and feel Therefore; we will break down the groove by describing the appropriate hi-hat; snare and bass drum articulations Ex 1.16
"The Groove"
This chart features a tight (and consistent) one-handed eighth-note hi-hat groove alongside a very strong bass drum and snare backbeat; which has been played on thousands of recordings. Although it is technically simple to play, it is quite difficult to make this groove feel and sound good You must pay particular attention to make sure that each limb lines up with one another Thus, each time you play a snare (or bass drum) that lands with the hi-hat; they should be in perfect unison, and no "flams" should be present:
Sound Now that you understand the basic approach, let's take a look at how you can achieve these articulations in greater detail:
Bass Drum The bass drum pattern should be played very forcefully__with authority and punch: This will be accomplished by utilizing three important strategies They are:
Ex 1.17
1. Using the plastic side of (or a hard-surfaced) bass drum beater: Many rock and pop drummers make this choice because it gives each pedal strokes impact a detailed punch and attack rather than a warm "emmooph" This is the perfect option for cutting through aggressive vocals, distorted guitars and powerful bass cabinets.
2. Burying the bass drum beater into the bass drum head: After each stroke it is also very important to keep the beater pushed (or buried) into the drum head. Not only does this muffle the drum slightly, but it adds another element of attack to the initial stroke (along with the hard-surfaced bass drum beater). However; you must make sure to firmly press the beater into the head to avoid any unwanted buzzing between the beater and drum head.
Drum Performance
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0136.PNG ---
Unit Chapter 2
Snare Drum The snare drum should be played very consistently;with a crisp and cutting sound: Additionally, each snare hit should be a rimshot as well This stroke is executed by hitting the center of the drumhead and the rim at the same exact time:
Most drummers think that a rimshot is played with a caveman-like arm motion. This could not be farther from the truth In actuality, a rimshot is loudest (and most effective) when the stroke is started by the wrist and finished off by the fingers. Let's take a look at both steps: 1 . While aiming for the center of the snare head, start the stroke with your wrist (Ex. 1.18). 2 Now, just before hitting the rim and center of the drum, grab the stick with your back three fingers This is lends a nice crisp attack to an already powerful stroke and it is very similar to a karate chopl Ex 1.18-Ex.1.20
Ex 1.18
Ex 1.19
Ex 1.20
Hi-Hat This eighth-note pattern should be played evenly and with a medium-loud volume In order to achieve this sound,you will use the shoulder of the stick, just beneath the tip (Ex: 1.18). Next; use this part of the stick to strike the edge (not the top) ofthe hi-hat cymbals (Ex 1.19).This will give you a very thick ~yet dynamically controlled- hi-hat tone: In comparison, by bringing the hi-hat volume lower it also creates a dynamic illusion, automatically making the bass drum and snare drum appear to sound louder within the overall drum mix Important: Do not use the tip ofthe stick on the top ofthe hi-hat This can sound very corny and drum machine-likel
Ex 1.21
Ex 1,.22
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0137.PNG ---
All Together The end result will be a strong; punchy and very authoritative bass drum sound alongside a karate-like rims- shot and thick (yet controlled) hat This approach perfectly compliments the arpeggiated guitar patterns and rhythmically driven bass textures within the song:
And Finally-_Reading the Chart Itself After our discussion, you now know that each section is eight bars long: This means that you do not have to stare at your chart and read every single time slash marking and repeat sign You should be able to casually look to your music every four or five bars to "check in" and keep your place: Not only will this help you concentrate on your "proper sound" as described in the previous pages; but you will also be able to focus on your time groove Have fun:

Unit twO reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Hard Rock "Rock 'n' roll"is often used as a generic term, but its sound is rarely predictable: From the outset; when the early rockers merged country and blues, its energy rebellion and catchy hooks have defined rock As a result; everything from Chuck Berry's pounding; three-chord rockers and the sweet harmonies of the Beatles to the jarring; atonal white noise of Sonic Youth has been categorized as "rock" Today, we will play a chart in the style of the Foo Fighters:
Structure: Sixteen-Bar Phrase This chart employs a common structure; which is used in most rock and pop music: the sixteen-bar phrase: As its title suggests, this passage is sixteen bars (measures) long and each bar (after the first bar) is marked with either time slashes or single-bar repeats Furthermore, each sixteen-bar phrase can be augmented with material from last weeks lesson, such as rehearsal letters, repeats signs and additional eight-bar phrases a5 well: Ex 2.1 Medium Hi-Hat w/Quarter-Note Accents
7
7E
)
13
Single-Bar Repeats: single-bar repeat is a sign indicating that you must repeat a particular bar (or measure): Rather than marking beats (like time slashes), single-bar repeats mark measures (all four beats) There is usually one specific bar of notated music at the beginning of a phrase and then multiple uses of single bar repeats thereafter: For example: Ex 2.2
Medium Hi-Hat w/Quarter-Note Accents
13

--- SOURCE: IMG_0141.PNG ---
Marking a Song's Form A drummer's most important job is to play a great groove with steady time: However; these attributes are not enough to make music Thus; we have another very important duty to perform: marking a songs form: Essentially marking a form is outlining (and drawing attention to) each different section within a song: This is accomplished by employing either one (or both) of the following: Employing sonic changes in the composition__picking either a hi-hat or ride cymbal to play within your groove: 2 Performing different groove altogether:
Marking a Song's Form, Example I: One simple method to marking the form would be to play the hi-hat pattern in letter A and then switch to the ride cymbal in letter B. For example: Ex 2.3
Medium Hi-Hat w/Quarter-Note Accents
B Ride Cymbal Bell Plays ' Quarter-Note Accents
7
This method is very effective and simple: One thing to note is if the bass guitar pattern changes you may need to adjust the bass drum pattern in addition to switching metallic surfaces Lets analyze how that would be notated and employed:

B Ride Cymbal Bell Plays  Quarter-Note Accents

In both ofthe previous examples; the drummer would have successfully drawn attention to the two different sections within the song: This would be considered playing musically by providing both a steady groove and marking the songs form:
Accents An accent is a symbol which indicates that a particular note should be played louder than another note: Thus,it is"accentuated" This performance (and dynamic) detail is marked by a sideways triangle as follows: Ex 2.5

Unit 2 - Chapter 1
Written-Out Fill: Letter B There is a written out drum fill in bar 16 of letter B. If a composer wants a specific fill played at a specific time in a chart; they may decide to actually write the fill out: Bar 16 of letter B features a fill between the snare drum/floor tom and the bass drum There is a crash on beat one of measure 16,which serves as an ending of the ensemble figures and a beginning of the measure 16 fill: Ex 2.10
Fermata A fermata (extended ending; hold or pause) is an element of musical notation indicating that the note should be sustained for longer than its note value would indicate: Exactly how much longer it is held is up to the discretion of the drummer: It is also up to the drummer to "cut-off" the band and end this extended hold: Ex 2.11
Don't forget the last unit's background information: In addition to this new information; you must still focus on (and pay attention to) last week's lesson as well: These items are: Ex 2.12

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Now that you understand these new basic chart notation elements (and recall last week's elements), it is up to you to navigate this form from behind the drum set: Remember; it is not enough to merely play the correct patterns, but rather; you must play the correct patterns with the correct sound and feel, and now mark the form as well With this in mind, will break down each the grooves (of the form) by describing the appropriate hi-hat; snare and bass drum articulations Ex 2.13 Medium Hi-Hat w/Quarter-Note Accents
"The A Groove"
This eighth-note rock groove (within letter A) has been played on thousands of popular recordingsIt is comprised of the same hi-hat and snare elements that were featured in last weeks chart, but now includes different bass drum pattern: In addition, the hi-hat now also employs an accent on every quarter note (beats 1,2 , 3 and 4). For example: Ex 2.14 Medium Hi-Hat w/Quarter-Note Accents
Just as in last week's example, it is quite difficult to make this groove feel and sound good. Please pay particular attention to make sure that each limb lines up with one another Thus; each time you play a snare (or bass drum) that lands with the hi-hat; they should be in unison; and no "flams" should be present With this in mind; Ihope that you will spend a sufficient amount of time in your practice lab refining this approach.
Sound Just as the chart has a particular groove; phrase length and chord progression, it will also have a particular drum sound: The last unit's bass drum and snare sounds will carry over to this chart They are: Ex 2.15

Unit 2 - Chapter 2
Hi-Hat In addition, this A section features a medium-loud (yet consistent) one-handed eighth-note hi-hat with an accent on each quarter-note pulse (beats 1, 2, 3,and 4) as follows: Ex 2.16 Medium Hi-Hat w/Quarter-Note Accents
The accented note: In order to achieve this accented sound, you will use the shoulder of the stick; a third of the way down from the tip: Ex 2.17
The un-accented note: In order to achieve this sound, you will use the shoulder of the stick; just beneath the tip. Next; use this part ofthe stick to strike the edge (not the top) ofthe hi-hat cymbals This will give you very thick, yet dynamically controlled unaccented hi-hat tone: In comparison; by using this part of the stick; it will make the unaccented hi-hat note volume lower: Important: Again, do not use the tip of the stick on the top of the hi-hat: This can sound very corny and drum machine-likel Ex 2.18
AIl Together In order to play these two notes back to back;you must use your wrist for the loud (accented note) andyour fingers (and partial wrist) on the quiet (unaccented note).

--- SOURCE: IMG_0147.PNG ---
"The B Groove" In order to properly mark the song's form (and draw attention to a new section), this eighth-note rock groove (within letter B) now includes a different bass drum pattern, and also utilizes the ride cymbal (rather than the hi-hat) Just as in the letter A hi-hat pattern, this cymbal pattern now also employs an accent on every quarter note (beats 1,2,3 and 4). This accent is played on the bell of the cymbal Therefore; the accents are on the bell and the remaining notes are on the body of the cymbal. Furthermore (and because the ride is marking the pulse), the hi-hat foot is closing on all four-quarter note pulses (i.e: beats 1, 2, 3 and 4). For example: Ex 2.19 Ride Cymbal Bell Plays ' Quarter-Note Accents
Ride Cymbal Again; this B section features a medium loud (yet consistent) one-handed eighth-note ride cymbal pattern with an accent on each quarter-note pulse (beats 1, 2,3,and 4) as follows: Ex 2.20 B Ride Cymbal Bell Plays Quarter-Note Accents
The accented note: In order to achieve this accented sound, you will use the shoulder of the stick, a third of the way down from the tip: Ex 2.21
20 Drum Performance 1
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0148.PNG ---
Unit 2 - Chapter 2
The un-accented note: In order to achieve this sound, you will use the tip of the stick, just beneath the tip. Next; use this part of the stick to strike the body of the cymbal. However (because you are striking the bell on the downbeats)  you will not be able to strike the ride in the normal spot: Your hand will not be able to reposition itself that quickly: Therefore, you will use the tip of the stick on the left-hand center part of the cymbal: (to the left of the bell itself). For example: Ex 2.22
All Together In order to play these two notes back-to-back,you must use your wrist for the loud (accented) note, andyour fingers to drop the stick (with a partial wrist stroke) on the quiet (unaccented) note You will also be making sideways motion from left to right __ for the accented quarter note bell-~and from right to left for the unaccented note. For example: Ex 2.23
Accent on Beat 1
Unaccented Note on the "&s"

Unit three reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to One-Handed 16ths This unit's chart is an R&B/pop chart in the tradition of Maroon 5 and John Mayer: It features two popular grooves that you might play on a contemporary session or live "cover" gig: Structure: The AABA 32-Bar Form This study employs a classic musical structure: the AABA 32-bar form: This form is comprised of two different sections and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A or B). Both letters (sections) represent three key musical attributes, which are: (1) a different rhythm (or groove), (2) an eight-bar phrase and (3) a particular riff and chord structure Let's examine each key element individually:
1 _ A different rhythm (or groove). As stated earlier; there are two letters (sections) within this form and each one now represents a particular rhythm (or groove). As a result; there are two different grooves within this chart:
The A section features a I6th-note hi-hat groove with a specific four-bar syncopated 16th-note bass drum pattern: Ex 3.1
Medium-Thick Hi-Hat
The B section groove features an eighth-note hi-hat pattern and a two-bar bass drum pattern: Ex 3.2
B
2 An eight-bar phrase In addition to a particular groove; each letter also denotes the use of an eight-bar phrase: Therefore; the A section features eight bars of a 6th-note hi-hat (and 16th-note syncopated bass drum) groove, and letter B features eight bars of an eighth-note hi-hat and bass drum groove: For example: Letter A has eight bars: Ex 3.3
Medium-Thick Hi-Hat
Fill

3  A particular guitar riff and chord progression Both letters are also centered on a particular riff and chord progression. In this case, the A section utilizes unique (and sparse) riff and chord progression The B section clearly differs by using another distinctive riff and chord progression:
First and second endings: In an AABA form, letter A occurs twice in a row; for a total of 16 bars However; this form is notated as two eight-bar phrases Rather than writing the section twice; this chart utilizes a first and second ending: Ex 3.5
Fill
Let's take a look at how to properly navigate this repeat structure
The first time you play through letter A,you will take the repeat under the first ending sign and travel back to the beginning of the section (as indicated by the solid arrow). Upon repeating the previous part and playing to bar 7 (or 15), you will skip the first ending and play the second ending (as indicated by the bracket beneath the measures). At this point you would be at letter B. Ex 3.6

Unit 3 - Chapter 1
Ensemble Figures: Letter A In this particular chart; there are ensemble figures on the"&"of 3 and beat 4 of the eighth measure of A (first ending), the downbeat and "&"of beat 4 ofthe seventh measure of B,and the last two bars of the entire tune on beats and 3 of the second-to-last measure and beat 1 of the last measure For example: Ex 3.11 Orchestrated as: Ex 3.12
37
Fill ----+
Fill - ----d
D.C. al Fine: D.C (da capo) is a term that tells you to return to the beginning of the chart. Al Fine tells us stop playing once you reach the term Fine. Together; they tell us to return to the beginning and play through to the ending (which is marked by the word Fine). Let's take a look at how to properly navigate this structure:
Upon reaching bar 8 of the last A section (AABA) you will see the text: DC al Fine: You will immediately return to the beginning of the chart (as indicated by the solid arrow). You will now play through the entire AABA form again (symbolized by the broken arrows). As you reach bar 8 of the last A, you will play through that measure and continue onward to measure 26. You will soon notice the ensemble figure- with the term Fine beneath it: On beat 2,you will play a crash cymbal and stop playing and end the chart The note "repeats are good" lets you know that you will play the first and second endings of the A section: Ex 3.13

Unit 3 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Remember; it is not enough to merely play the correct patterns; but rather; you must play the correct patterns with the correct sound, feel and form: We will break down and demonstrate the appropriate hi- hat; snare and bass drum articulations Ex 3.15
B
In this chart; both sections utilize the same drum sound: an open (yet syncopated) bass drum alongside a dark-sounding snare backbeat and chunky 16th-note hi-hat groove Let's take a look at how you can achieve these articulations:
Bass Drum The bass drum pattern should be played very forcefully,with an open articulation This will be accomplished by utilizing two important strategies They are:
1 Using the felt side of (or a soft-surfaced) bass drum beater: This gives each pedal strokes impact a round sound (with less attack and punch) This is the perfect option for adding a full, low tone to a song:
2. Playing the bass drum with an open "off the head" (non- buried) stroke. After each stroke it is also very important to keep the beater away from (not buried into) the drumhead Not only does this allow the drum to resonate more; but it also allows the pitch of the drum to remain lower as well  Important: you must make sure to control the bass-drum beater and footboard, and avoid any unwanted "felt-flutter" between the beater and drum head You must "rest and release"both your foot and the beater between strokes This is very different from the buried approach, because you cannot keep your leg raised in "attack" position between strokes. For example: Ex 3.16
29
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0157.PNG ---
Snare Drum: Center of the head (non-rimshot) stroke The snare drum should be played very consistently,in the center of the drumhead Each hit should be played without a rimshot; which will make the drum sound deep and open: This stroke is executed by hitting the center of the drumhead with the butt end of the stick
Most drummers think that this is an easier stroke to execute (than a rimshot) In actuality, this motion (and sound) takes a great deal of accuracy and concentration Lets take a look at the steps: 1 _ Locate the center of the head and turn the stick so that the butt end is facing outward. 2 Next, while aiming for this spot on the snare head, start the stroke with your wrist: 3_ Now, just before hitting the lower half of the center dot; grab the stick with your back three fingers This is lends a nice crisp attack to an open snare tone:
Ex 3.17
This location will also let the drum ring a bit; which is perfect for this style
Hi-Hat: Chunky; "shouldered" 16ths You should use the shoulder of the stick (mid-way down from the tip). In addition; the hi-hat should be closed with your foot, but not too tightlyl Rather than an articulation of"chick-chick-chick-chick"think ofyour hi-hat saying; "chunk-chunk-chunk-chunk" Ex 3.18 (left)
Next; use this part ofthe stick to strike the edge (not the top) of thehi-hat cymbals. This will give you the very thick and chunky hi-hat tone: These performance methods create a rhythmically thick (and tempo-based) illusion, which will make the groove feel fat; with more forward motion and syncopation. Ex 3.19 (left)
AIl Together The end result will be a strong; round and open bass drum sound, alongside an open (yet dark toned) snare and thick, and "chunky"hat:

The ABAB 32-Bar Form This study employs a similar structure to last week's AABA 32-bar form: the ABAB 32-bar form. Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Now that you understand this form, it is up to you to simulate (and draw attention to) these sectional differences by employing small sonic and dynamic changes on the drum set: Remember marking a songs form from units 2 and 3. Marking the form will be accomplished by both changing the bass drum pattern in each section, altering the hi-hat tone and adding hi-hat openings have described the appropriate hi- hat; snare and bass drum articulations for your review: They are as follows: Ex 4.14
Letter A
Just as the A section has a particular groove, phrase length and chord progression, it will also have a particular drum sound. Let's take a look at how you can achieve these articulations:
Bass Drum The bass drum pattern should be played very forcefully; with a staccato articulation and a dynamic of forte (loud) This will be accomplished by utilizing three important strategies They are as follows:
1. Using the plastic side of (or a hard surfaced) bass drum beater: 2. Burying the bass drum beater into the bass drum head: 3. Use your entire leg (and foot) to make the stroke:
Snare Drum The snare drum should be played very consistently and at a dynamic of forte. Additionally, each snare hit should be a rimshot as well: Remember; this stroke is executed by hitting the center of the drumhead and rim at the same exact time
1 . Because the A groove is a two-handed 16th-note pattern, your rimshot will be played with your right hand as follows:

Again, you will use the shoulder of the stick; just beneath the tip. Next; use this part of the stick to strike the edge (not the top) ofthe hi-hat cymbals This will give you a very thickand dynamically controlled hi-hat tone: By bringing the hi-hat volume lower it creates a dynamic illusion; automatically making the bass drum and snare drum appear to sound louder within the overall drum mix Ex 4.16

Hi-Hat This two-handed slightly-loose hi-hat part should be played evenly and forcefully,at a dynamic of medium loud (mezzo forte): In order to achieve this sound, you will use the middle/shoulder of the stick: This sound (and technique) gives the illusion that the B section speeds up slightly and therefore it generates a large amount of musical excitement: Important: The hi-hat opening will be achieved by opening (and accenting) the "&" of 4 on the second measure of the phrase: Immediately afterward, close the hi- hat with your foot on beat 1 of the next bar: Ex 4.17

The ABAB Form with a Pre-Chorus This study employs a similar structure to last week's ABAB 32 Bar Form but is more condensed by use of shorter section lengths Differing from last weeks discussion,this form is comprised of four different sections and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A, A9, B, Coda)
Intro An intro of a tune is normally a short (four-eight bars) musical phrase that sets up the feel and vibe of the tune The intro for this chart is a pre-programmed drum loop that runs throughout the entire tune (even through the rests) For this unit your instructor will start and stop the loop (you will be responsible for this in later units). The intro is four bars long: ` fours bars of the loop with the guitar lick entering on bars 3-4. For example: Ex 5.1

Coda The coda of this chart functions as a new section, the bridge. A bridge of a song normally includes a new groove and a new harmonic structure: In this particular bridge the drums play a new groove and the playing surface switches from the hi-hat to the ride cymbal. For example: Ex 5.9

Letter B: Fills and Figures Within the first ending of letter B there is a direction to play a two-beat fill. Remember that this fill is only leading the song back to a repeat of B,so it would be appropriate to keep this fiIll on the subtle side: The second ending of letter B containsan ensemble figure of a half note. This figure should be played on a crash cymbal with a bass drum supporting it. The notated snare drum hit on beat 3 should be played as a rimshot with one stick while your free hand should grab the crash cymbal on beat 3. The technique will allow for an incredibly dramatic stop and dead silence on beat 4 For example: Ex 5.15

Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Slow 12-Bar Blues The blues is one of America's greatest musical treasures. A roots music form that evolved out of African- American work songs, field hollers, spirituals,and country string ballads more than a century ago, the blues is the foundation of virtually every major American music form born in the ZOth century, including jazz, rhythm and blues, rock 'n'roll, and hip-hop. This unit's chart will focus on modern master of blues: Stevie Ray Vaughan:
Structure: 12-Bar Blues (I-IV-V) The term "12-bar blues" refers to the number of measures, or musical bars, used to express the theme of a typical blues song: A 12-bar blues is divided into three four-bar segments A standard blues progression typically features three chords based on the first written as first (I), fourth (IV), and fifth notes ofan eight- note scale: In this case; the (B7) chord dominates the first four bars, the IV (E7) chord typically appears in the second four bars and the V (F#7) chord is played in the third four bars Ex 6.1

Although the 12-bar blues is broken up into four four-bar phrases that outline the |-IV-V chord structure; it is important to realize that you are ultimately dealing with a 12-bar phrase:

"12/8 Feel" within a 4/4 Time Signature: The definition of a triplet is three notes of the same value occupying the same space as two notes of the same value: Basically, each beat; instead of being divided by twos or fours, is divided by thirds Dividing each beat into three eighth note triplets creates 12 eighth notes within one measure of 4/4 time (3X4-12). Rather than writing four groups of three note sub-phrases within 12/8 time signature (6/8 and 12/8 will be addressed in Reading 2) , many composers prefer to write four groups of triplets within 4/4 time. This is called a"12/8 Feel" Maintaining the time signature of 4/4 allows for the downbeats to remain as 1, 2,3,and 4.This weeks drum chart is written in 4/4,with a tempo of a quarter note equaling 60 beats per minute: For example: Ex 6.3
4 60 Slow Blues
Al Ride Cymbal 3
Counting Eighth-Note Triplets: There are a few methods to counting eighth-note triplet subdivisions. One great way is to simply use the word "triplet" preceded by the beat number: For example: Ex 6.4

Multiple-Bar Repeat Signs: A multiple-bar repeat sign functions the same way asa single-bar repeat sign, except it instructs you to repeat a certain amount of previous bars. If you have a two-bar phrase that needs to be repeated, you can simply use a modified bar repeat sign with the exact amount of measures that need to be repeated placed above the symbol: For example: Ex 6.5

Unit 6 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Groove 1 The A1 12-bar form is played on the ride cymbal and utilizes a rimshot articulation on the snare drum, alongside strong standard (either "bury-the-beater" or "off the head") bass drum sound. The ride plays continuous eighth note triplets, the snare drum is on beats 2 and 4,and the bass drum plays a one-bar pattern: For example: Ex 6.11
Al Ride Cymbal 3
While on the ride cymbal, please experiment with different hi-hat foot permutations such as closing onbeats 2and4 (matching the snare) or closing on all eighth note triplets (matching the ride pattern)
This eighth-note triplet ride cymbal groove has been played on thousands of popular recordings Although it is technically simple to play; it is quite difficult to make this groove feel and sound good. You must pay particular attention to make sure that each limb lines up with one another: No "flams" should be present: Please focus on the groups ofthree within the eighth note pulse as well Ifthe time straightens out to groups of 2 or four (eighths or 16ths), you are not playing the correct groove.
Groove 2 The A2 section is played on a tightly closed hi-hat and utilizes a cross-stick articulation on the snare drum, alongside strong standard (either "bury-the-beater" or "off the head") bass drum sound. This section features a two-bar pattern from the bass drum by adding a bar to the pattern from A1. For example: Ex 6.12
A2 Tight Hi-Hat
New pattern
Same groove from section A1
55
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0183.PNG ---
Cross-Stick In order to get a strong; full and warm cross stick sound, turn the stick around and use the butt end. The bead (tip) ofyour stick should be placed just "off-center;" between the eight and nine oclock position. The butt end will be off the drum between the three and four Oclock position as follows: Ex. 6.13 (right):
In order to make the stroke, grip the stick in your normal fashion. Then "throw"it into the drum and take your four fingers off the stick At this point your fingers should flatten and you will apply pressure to keep the stick on the head (and rim). To make the next cross-stick stroke, grab the stick with your fingers again and repeat: For example: Ex 6.14
Although this will get you in the general area and enable you to get a solid cross stick sound, each snare drum is quite unique and has its own "sweet spot" Therefore; it is extremely important to experiment and find the stick position that sounds best
56 Drum Performance
==================================================

Groove 3 The A3 section is played on a thicker (less pressure on the pedal) hi-hat and utilizes a rim shot articulation on the snare drum alongside a strong standard (either"bury-the-beater" or"off the head") bass drum sound: This section features a four-bar pattern from the bass drum that varies in every bar of the four-bar phrase For example: Ex 6.15
A3 Thick Hi-Hat
Groove 4 The groove for the final section, 44, features a two-bar pattern; the first bar of the phrase employs a very syncopated bass drum strike on the third partial of the triplet on beat 2. Special attention must be paid to this particular hit since is does not "land"on a downbeat Make sure to "feel" (on the ride cymbal or perhaps with your hi-hat foot) the downbeat of beat 3 immediately following this bass drum hit The second bar of this two-bar pattern is identical to the second bar of the two-bar pattern in A2. The groove also gets "lifted" by moving from the hi-hat back to the ride as previously played during A1. For example: Ex. 6.16
A4 Ride Cymbal

Unit Seven reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Shuffle 12-Bar Blues Todays chart will focus on a standard blues rhythm: the shuffle: Structure Il: 12 Bar Blues (I-IV-V) Open Form: The 12-bar blues is considered an open form. This means that the song (or chart) is played (and subsequently repeated) in 12-bar increments without a specifically stated ending: Therefore; the 12-bar form (chart) will be performed in 12-bar increments (12-24-36-48-60-72 bars): With this fact in mind; there are two additional terms that modify, add to and accompany this form: They are: (1) the head and (2) a chorus Lets take a look at each in greater detail:
The Head The term 'head" is most often used in jazz or blues-related contexts, and it refers to the thematic melody within a song's form. This melody is usually played at the beginning and at the end of a song's performance: Thus, if a bandleader requests one head (in a 12-bar form), that would mean that the band would play through 12 bars ofthe melody. For example: Ex.7.1
Melody Line
Swing J = 120 F7
3
Bb7
F7
Bb7
F7
C7 €
3
Take Coda on cue
Jazz and blues musicians also often give a signal (or cue) for the "head" or "top" by patting their hand on top of their head, which is meant to make sure everybody "goes back to the melody" the next time the "top of the form" comes around:
61
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0189.PNG ---
A Chorus Unlike a pop music chorus which refers to "the hook" of a song ~a jazz or blues chorus is a term that is slang for "one-time through the form" Thus,if a bandleader says they: want to play one chorus of guitar solo (in a 12-bar blues form), that would be one 12-bar blues form of guitar solo. For example: Ex 7.2
Guitar Solo through the form
IV
IV
V_
10
Head and Chorus Terms Used Together Oftentimes a bandleader will state the form at the beginning ofa song He or she might say something like this: Were going to play one head, one chorus of guitar solo and one head out" Thus (in a 12 bar form), they want the band to play 12 bars ofthe melody; 12 bars (one time through the 12-bar form) of guitar solo and one 12-bar melody to the end. For example: Ex 7.3
Head (12 bars)
One Chorus of Guitar Solo (12 bars)
Head Out (12 bars)

Unit 7 Chapter 1
Cue: As stated within the open form terminology, there is no specific ending within this form. Thus, a cue is employed. A cue is a notice made from one band member to the rest of the band in order to signal a break; rest or ending: Thus, in the chart you will see the text "on cue" This will tell you to get the band's attention by playing a short fill or counting them into the coda ending:
Coda As definied in Unit 4,a coda is a specific ending; which is represented by this symbol (left). Therefore; after your cue; the band will take the coda sign after bar 8, and follow it down to the actual coda (marked by the same symbol) This is where the chart will end. For example:
Ex 7.4
Bb7 A7
Ab7 3
G7
CzsuS4)
C7
C#7 C7 7 Take Coda on cue
F?
Fi7   F7
5
Shuffle Feel: A shuffle feel is a triplet-based groove written in 4/4 time It is very common in blues songs, medium tempo jazz pieces and in rock music The shuffle usually refers to a "broken triplet feel;" where there is no middle triplet present: For example: Ex 7.5 3
It is also usually written (in chart form) as eighth notes, although triplets are implied This is recognized at the beginning of the chart with the following symbol: Ex 7.6 3
Played as eighth note triplets
This lets the reader of the chart know that every time they read an eighth note, they are to perform it as a "broken" eighth-note triplet:
63
==================================================

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Review Sound: 1 . Any bass drum articulation:"bury the beater" or open articulation: 2 Rimshot snare sound: 3. Medium-even hi-hat sound.
A different groove order and form for each student: Now that you understand the elements, will break down the grooves for each part of the 12-bar form performance:
Assigning the grooves: Each student will be assigned a particular groove for the head and the choruses: Ex 7.7 Groove #I:"Four on the Floor
Ex 7.8
Groove #2: "Syncopated"
Assigning a form for each student: Each student will also be assigned particular number of heads and choruses of guitar solo for their performances a5 well:
Marking the 12-bar form: hi-hat for the head and ride cymbal for the choruses: In the last few charts, we marked form by changing either the groove or riding surface for each rehearsal letter: However; the approach will differ slightly within a 12-bar-blues "open" form. As previously discussed, you will be repeating the same 12-bar phrase over and over: However; there will be two main sections within this song: the head and the chorus of solo. Thus,you will play thehi-hat during the head and the ride cymbal (with your hi-hat foot moving) during the guitar solo choruses Upon the completion ofthe guitar solo (and return to the head), you will return to the hi-hat:

Unit 7 Chapter 2
Ensemble Figures: Bar 12 Remember: ensemble figures are rhythms that are played by the entire ensemble (band) These figures are written in the middle of the staff and take precedence over the time slashes In this particular chart; there are ensemble figures on beat 2 and the "&" of 2 within bar 12, just prior to repeating the 12-bar form: For example: Ex 7.9 Bb7 A7 Ab7 G7 C7(US4) F7 3 3
Take Coda on cue
Ensemble figures
With this in mind, you will need to accentuate these rhythms alongside the band. One solid orchestration is to play a snare drum on beat 2 (which is already occurring within the groove) and then cymbal a crash in unison with the bass drum.
Ensemble Figures: Coda There are also ensemble figures present in the coda (at the end of the chart) on the "&" of and the downbeats of 2,3 and 4ofthe first bar; and then on the first four eighth notes of the last measure ("1 & 2 &"). For example: Ex 7.10
F7
F#7
FI
Again, you will need to accentuate these rhythms alongside the band. One good option would be to simply play therhythm on the snare and then play the lasthit on the"&"of 2with a bass drum/crash cymbal combination:
Review: 1 . Again, your teacher will assign you a groove for both the head and choruses of solo. For this discussion; let's assume that you are assigned groove for the head and groove 2 for the choruses The (groove 1) head would beon thehi-hat;and the choruses (groove 2) on the ride cymbal: 2 Next, you will also be given a certain form: For the sake of this example; let's assume that you will be playing one head, two choruses of solo and one head out; taking the coda on the cue. Thus, it would be 12 bars of melody (groove 1 on the hi-hat), 24 bars of the choruses of solo (groove 2 on the ride cymbal) and one 12-bar head out (groove 1-~taking the coda sign to the ending:
And finally-_reading the chart itself: After our initial discussion; you now know that each section is 12 bars long: This means that you do not have to stare at your chart and read every single time slash marking and repeat sign. You should be able to casually look to your music every four or five bars to "check in" and keep your place: Not only will this help you concentrate on your sound, but you will also be more able to focus on your time and groove: Have fun:

Unit eight reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Funk-Rock Though much early rock'n'roll was based in the blues, blues-rock didn't fully develop into a subgenre until the late 1960s. Borrowing the idea of an instrumental combo and loud amplification from rock'n'roll, the hybrid became quite popular and the bands that immediately followed (Cream; Led Zeppelin, ZZ Top) were louder and more riff-oriented With this in mind, this weeks chart is in the modern tradition of the John Mayer Trio and groove master Steve Jordan:
Guitar Introduction Although the drum groove is written in bars and 2 ofthe chart; this week's song features a four-bar guitar introduction: This is stated by the text: Ist Time: Guitar Only. Therefore; the guitar plays by itself in the first three bars of the chart; like this: Ex 8.1 Open Hi-Hat
Ist Time: Guitar Only. Full band in on D. C.)
(Ist Time: Bass & Drums in)
Important: Upon taking the DC.al Coda, the drums (and bass) will perform through the intro the second time This is stated by the text: Full Band in on D. C and the notated drum groove (Ex. 8.1 above).
Snare Drum Pick-Up Fill During the guitar introduction, the drums will lead into letter A with a full measure of broken I6th notes. Please perform these as snare drum rimshots, and make sure to play these notes evenly,in both sound and timing: Ex 8.2
Open Hi-Hat
(Ist Time: Guitar Only. Full band in on D. C.)
(Ist Time: Bass & Drums in)

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter A features a very syncopated eighth- and 16th-note based groove: The bass drum has many I6th notes, which do not always line up with the hi-hat: The snare drum is not your standard backbeat either; it is on beats 2, 3 and the"&"of 4.Therefore, it is very important that you subdivide while practicing and playing this groove. Ex 8.3
Open Hi-Hat
Subdividing Subdividing is counting and/or singing the smallest subdivision possible within a particular musical passage or groove Not only will this help you to maintain a steady tempo, but it will also help you develop an even note placement Since this groove is comprised of both eighth and sixteenth notes, we will subdivide 16th notes within this groove: For example: Ex 8.4
Open Hi-Hat

This will be especially helpful with the snare drum on the "&"of beat 4,and the bass drum on the "e"and"a' of beat 3 and the"e"of beat 4. For example: Ex 8.5

B Medium; Thick Hi-Hat
Remember: In order to properly play the 16th-note bass drum/snare drum patterns, it is very important that you subdivide while practicing and playing this groove: For example: Ex 8.7

Unit Ne reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Modern Rock It is important for all drummers to understand how to play and read in the 3/4 time signature: 3/4 is very common and is used by many bands and artists in practically all genres of music: Most recently; popular bands such as Paramore; Muse and Radiohead have recorded many great tunes in 3/4. Technically; from a drummer's perspective, 3/4is really no different than any other style or feel. One trap you can fall into is to allow the tempo to rush: The downbeat of in every measure is very strong in 3/4 time, and it is common for young players to undervalue to importance of beats 2 and 3. Practicing 3/4 time: Use a metronomel A great way to develop your 3/4 time feel is to use a metronome: Let's look at two simple methods:
Method 1: Quarter-Note Click Set your metronome to 100 BPM with no accented clicks. Treating each click with a value of a quarter note, play the following groove: Ex 9.1
Once you get comfortable and "internalize" this tempo, try this same groove at faster and slower tempos
Method 2: Dotted-Half-Note Note Click Set your metronome to 40 BPM with no accented clicks. Treating each click with a value of adotted half note; play the above groove (Ex 9.1).
The ABAB Form This study employs a common ABAB structure with both the A and B sections being eight bars long; each played three times In total there are 48 bars (24/24), creating the Aand B sections There is alsoa D.S.al Coda with the coda functioning as the seam between the B section and the 12-bar ending that leads to the final measure:
Intro The intro of this tune is a four-bar guitar part that sets up the feel of the tune You should count in the guitar player with a two-bar count in: For example: Ex 9.2 "One" "Two" "Three" "One" "Two" "Three"

Unit 9 - Chapter 1
Multi-Rests Another way to indicate multiple measures of rests is to use multi-rest. A multi-rest is a thick; black horizontal bar with a number above it The black bar indicates a rest and the number indicates the number of measures In this chart; the four bars of rest for the drummer are notated by use of a multi-rest For example: Ex 9.3
Although this is a simple concept; it is easy to get lost in a multi-rest (especially in very long ones). One method to counting through a multi-rest is done by adding the measure number: For example: Ex 9.4
"Two" "Three
"2"
"Two" "Three"
"3"
"Two" "Three"
"4"
"Two" "Three"
Third Ending Another chart form tool is to use a third ending: To navigate a third ending, you make sure you play the first and second endings, then proceed to the third ending after repeating back for the third pass of a particular section (or passage):
In the A section the first and second endings are identical The first and second endings are notated by being housed under the same bracket: The third ending has a written fill. For example: Ex 9.5
A Closed Hi-Hat
2
1.2
13.
Ist ending: Travel back to A
3rd ending: Continue on to the next section
Znd ending: Travel back to A

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound The A section features a two-bar groove with a syncopated bass drum and a snare hit on beat 1 of every other bar The hi-hat should be closed and played consistently without any accented notes. The bass drum should be played strong; but with the beater off the head. The snare drum should be a warm, center snare hit (not a rimshot) The fill in the third ending should be played with alternating single strokes For example: Ex 9.7

The coda can be broken down into two sections. Unit ten reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Rock Shuffle As we have learned; the shuffle can be played and used for many styles of music: One very popular from of shuffle is the rock shuffle: This groove has been used in rock music since the '60s, but many modern rock bands of today (Smashing Pumpkins, Louis XIV, Muse; Radiohead) still get a lot of mileage out of this rumbling groove
The Form: You will essentially be playing the same groove for the entire tune (and harmonically the tune does not change much), so you must be aware of the section lengths and section order The basic structure is as follows:
Intro (8 bars) A (16 bars) B (18 bars) DS.
A (8 bars) B (18 bars) C (10 bars) D (16 bars plus final fermata)
Eight-Bar Intro The tune starts with an eight-bar intro with a one-bar shuffle feel Notice that the drums use the floor tom for the ride surface This helps create a darker and ominous vibe The guitar plays a quarter note-based melody that is easily recognizable Ex 10.1
Letter A Letter A is a 16-bar section (by use of first and second endings) containing a one-bar shuffle groove (Ex: 10.1). The guitar plays a more subtle melody at letter A
Letter B Letter B is an 18-bar section basically made up of two eight-bar sections with an added two-bar break The drums maintain the previous groove (Ex: 10.1). There is a new guitar melody in this section with long half note and whole note phrases:
DS: The D. S.at the end of letter B instructs to go back to letter A, but to take the second ending, creating an eight-bar section for letter A on the D. $. This is indicated by the phrase: D. S. (take Znd ending).
Letter € Letter € is a ten-bar section: an eight-bar phrase with another two bar-break added The guitar goes back to the melody played for the intro:
Letter D After the break at letter C, the chart ends with the D section: The D section is identical to the B section, except the tune ends on the upbeat of 4 on the eighth measure of D
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0209.PNG ---
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound The focal point of the particular rock shuffle is the use of the floor tom for a riding surface: It is very important that you not strike the floor tom too hard: You want to find the sweet spot where the drum rumbles at an even keel without any notes standing out: If this is not achieved, then the groove will sound lopsided and sloppy The challenge with this concept is that while you are maintaining a medium volume on the floor tom you also need to hit the snare drum hard on beats 2 and 4. Ex 10.2 Snare drum loud on 2 and 4
Floor tom medium volume every beat
Flams and Double Stops A recurring fill appears three times (bars 8, 16 and 25). This fill consists ofthe bass drum maintaining the four- on-the-floor and the hands filling in the upbeats on the toms (and/or snare/cymbals) Technically speaking; the fill has flams on the toms on the & of 1,2 and 3. On beat 4 the flams come together and form double stops between the snare and crash cymbal (Note: In measure 25 this part is played between the floor tom and rack tom) The flams should be played as right hand flams (right hand is the primary stroke) and should be very wide and open: Ex 10.3
LR
LR
LR
R L

About the Authors
Donny Gruendler was born and raised in the diverse musical  surroundings of Detroit, Michigan: As result; Gruendler grew up alongside an unusually broad range of influences including soul, funk; pop, hip hop; traditional swing, hard bop; techno, house and blues.
At age 20, Gruendler graduated from Berklee College of Music with Bachelor of Music degree At age 21, he earned his Master of Music degree from Wayne State University in Detroit: Now living in Los Angeles, Gruendler has performed, programmed; toured and recorded behind such artists as Kenny Burrell, John Medeski; DJ Logic the Funk Brothers, DJ A-Ski (Unique 74), Rick Holmstrom,and Kirk Fletcher: He has composed and/or played on jingles for Axe Body Spray; RE/MAX on the Boulevard, and NPR. His select film/TV credits include Last Holiday, Father of Invention, and the Showtime feature Chicago Overcoat: Donny is also a noted producer under the pseudonym Inc, and one half of the funky-soul-electro duo Rhett Frazier Inc: Okayplayer describes their productions as cosmic brilliance delivered via a well-stirred pot of soul, jazz, rock; funk and gospel:
In the education realm, Gruendler is Vice President of Instruction and Curricular Development at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California, and he is also a member of the Modern Drummer education team and a frequent columnist for the magazine: Donny has also released several instructional books, DVDs and other online/digital content under his own name: For more information; please visit WWWdonnygruendlercom
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0215.PNG ---
Stewart Jean was born and raised in Deptford, New Jersey, where he was introduced to music through his father's love of classical music and his friends' Led Zeppelin LPs. Stewart's passion for music grew as he studied drumset; timpani and mallets in high school. Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician:
lyouug
At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree, Stewart became a sought-after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings; tours and shows
After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles; maintaining a steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the-point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists'lists
Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years, Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students. Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves, Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards.

UnIt ONe reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Hard Rock Hard rock music commonly employs fast tempos. Thus, today's hard rock drummers must be comfortable both playing loud and fast; while also avoiding undue tension in the hands (and feet). This week's chart focuses on the hard rock grooves of Dave Grohl, Daru Jones and Josh Freese:
Structure: The ABC 34-Bar Form This form is comprised of three different sections, and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A, B or C). AlI letters (sections) represent three key musical attributes They - are: a different groove, phrasing (four; eight; ten or twelve bar lengths), and a particular riff and chord structure: Ex 1.1 Rock =[65

Unit Chapter 1
Intro The chart begins with an unaccompanied bass guitar riff for four bars Therefore, the first four bars within the drum chart contain a multi-rest: Multi-rests are commonly used to indicate two or more bars of rest (in a neat and clear manner as compared with writing out many individual measures of rest). For example: 1.2a
Rock J = 165
On bar 5,the drums enter with double stops between the snare drum and floor tom (this rhythm mimics the bass riff perfectly) It is important that you subdivide eighth notes during the first four bars of the tune: By subdividing you will ensure that your part does not rush and is locked with the bass part: For example: Ex 1.2b
Bass part
p+X JEE m35155E 5
Drum part
Letter A Letter A is a ten-bar phrase; which also marks the entrance of the guitar riff The drums continue to play the opening bass riff; but with added bass drum eighth notes filling in the remaining eighth notes This action occurs for six bars, as a two-bar phrase played three times The last two bars of letter A (within the box below) feature an aggressive pattern that leads into the "&"of beat 4. For example: Ex 1.3a
HE
1 7

Building the Letter A Pattern The hand/foot pattern between the snare drum/floor tom and bass drum must be executed with precision or it will sound lopsided. A great way to practice this pattern is to break down each grouping of four eighth notes. By isolating each component of this pattern you can focus on subdividing eighth notes and tighten up the space between the double stops and the bass drum: For example: Ex 1.3b
2
3
2
3
2
Now put it all together to create the two-bar pattern: Ex 1,.3c
7 & 2  & 3 & 4
1
& 2   & 3 &
&
&

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter A
Snare Drum For letter A the snare drum should be played in the center of the drum (no rim shots) when paired with the floor tom: As mentioned in Performance 1, Unit 3,this technique will make the drum sound deep and open:
1 _
Please locate the center of the head and the tip of the stick 2 Next; while aiming for this spot on the snare head, start the stroke with your wrist: 3 Now, just before hitting the rim and lower half of the center dot; grab the stick with your back three fingers This is lends a nice crisp attack to an open snare tone Ex 1.8
Bass Drum The bass drum should be played with a buried bass drum sound This will make the drum project loudly and forcefully: Also, be sure that the volume is consistent between the snare/floor tom and the bass drum: Ex 1,.9

Unit Chapter 2
Double Stops The double stops between the snare drum and floor tom must be even and consistent: Be sure to examine where you are striking each drum while playing,as it is common for the right hand to drift off-center on the floor tom. Ex 1.10
Correct placement of the right
Incorrect placement of the right
Letter B
Snare Drum At letter B,the snare drum should be played with a rim shot: This will allow the backbeat to cut through the dense guitar and bass parts. As stated in Performance 1, Unit 1, this creates a nice crisp attack to an already powerful stroke and it is very similar to a karate chopl
1 _
While aiming for the center of the snare head, start the stroke with your wrist: 2 Now, just before hitting the rim and center of the drum, grab the stick with your back three fingers Ex 1.11
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0714.PNG ---
Riding on a Crash Cymbal During letter B, the riding surface is a crash cymbal: When riding on crash cymbal, you must use the shoulder of the stick just under the tip. To produce a washy sound with minimum subdivision (the desired effect), you must strike the edge of the cymbal at a 450angle: Your wrist should be relaxed and each stroke should be consistent; avoiding any accenting: Lastly, make sure that this washy cymbal sound does not overpower the snare and bass drum: Ex 1.12
In general,you must be sure to stay physically relaxed when playing aggressive grooves Ifyou get too tense you will choke the sound, causing your groove and sound production to suffer: Be sure to recognize tension in your body (especially your mouth; toes and hands) when practicing:

Unt twO reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Punk-Pop Punk-pop is a post-grunge sub style of alternative rock that combines popular music melodies, chord changes and grooves with speedy punk tempos and loud, driving guitars Green Day and Weezer were the first bands to popularize this variation of rock in the 1990s, and there are several groups that play this style today: These include Fall Out Boy; Franz Ferdinand and Panic at the Disco.
Structure: The ABAB Form This chart employs a musical structure common to rock; pop and jazz music: the ABAB form: It is comprised of two different sections and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A or B) Both letters (sections) represent three key musical attributes: a different rhythm (or groove), an eight-bar phrase and a particular riff and chord structure Let's examine each key element individually:
1.A different rhythm (or groove). As stated earlier; there are two main sections within this form and B) As a result; there are two grooves present within this chart: For example:
The A section features a two-bar; straight-eighth hi-hat groove alongside a quarter-note (four-on-the-floor) bass drum pattern. There is also an open hi-hat accent on the "&"of each beat Ex 2.1
Medium Hi-hat
The B section features a two bar; double-time feel, which placesthesnare drum on the "&"of each beat (rather_thanbeats Zand 4) The hi-hat is comprised of eighth notes and the bass drum employs 6th notes; which do not always line up with the hi-hat: Ex 2.2
Double Time Feel B Sloshy- 1/2 Open Hi-hat
15
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0718.PNG ---
2.An eight-bar phrase Each letter also represents an eight-bar phrase (or section) Therefore; the A section features eight bars of an eighth-note upbeat accented/open hi-hat (with a four-on-the-floor quarter-note bass drum) groove; and letter B features eight bars of a double-time feel. For example: Ex 2.3
Medium Hi-hat
2
2
Fill
Double Time Feel
B| sloshy- 1/2 Open Hi-hat
2
2
2
Fill
3. A particular guitar riff and chord progression: Once more; both letters are also centered on a particular riff and chord progression In this case; the A section utilizes a unique riff and one chord is used for the entire eight bars. The B section clearly differs by using another distinctive riff and a chord progression with chords that change approximately every two bars
Structure Addition: Letter € Letter C is essentially an additional section that features a new chord structure for a guitar solo. This section also uses a two-bar; double-time feel; but varies from the B section. Also, the playing surface switches from sloshy hi-hat (in letter B) to the ride cymbal bell: Ex 2.4
Ride Cymbal Bell

Unit 2 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound We will break down each section and describe the appropriate hi-hat, snare and bass drum articulations: Letter A Ex 2.5
Medium Hi-hat
Just as the A section has a particular groove, phrase length; and chord progression; it will also have a particular drum sound. This section features a medium-heavy (and consistent) eighth-note hi-hat groove (with open accents on the"&"s of each beat) alongside a very staccato four-on-the-floor bass drum pattern and strong snare backbeat: Lets take a look at howyou can achieve these articulations:
Bass Drum The bass drum pattern should be played very forcefully; with a staccato articulation and a dynamic of forte (loud) This will be accomplished by utilizing three important strategies:
1 _ Using the plastic side of (or a hard-surfaced) bass drum beater: 2 Burying the bass drum beater into the bass drum head. 3. Using your entire leg (and foot) to make the stroke
Snare Drum The snare drum should be played very consistently at a dynamic of forte (loud). Each snare hit should be a rim shot: This stroke is executed by hitting the center of the drumhead and rim at the same exact time:
Hi-Hat This medium-heavy hi-hat pattern should be played evenly and forcefully at a dynamic of mezzo-forte (medium loud) In order to achieve this sound, you will use the upper shoulder of the stick (just below the tip) for the unaccented notes and the shoulder of the stick for each "&"accented hi-hat opening: Ex 2.6
US
SH
US
SH
US
SH
US
SH
US = upper shoulder of the stick SH = shoulder of the stick
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0720.PNG ---
The hi-hat opening will be achieved by opening (and accenting) the"&"of each beat Immediately afterward, close the hi-hat with your foot on the downbeat (as _indicated by CL_ close with foot). For example: Ex 2.7
CL
CL
CL
CL
Notation To indicate a closed hi-hat a plus sign (+) is used To indicate an open hi-hat a small circle (0) is used:
All Together The end result will be a strong; punchy, and very authoritative bass drum sound alongside a karate-chop- like rim shot and thick (yet dynamically controlled) hi-hat This approach perfectly propels the music forward within letter A
Letter B Ex 2.8
Double Time Feel B Sloshy- 1/2 Open Hi-hat
In addition to letter A's bass drum and snare drum sounds, this section features a wide-open sloshy eighth- note hi-hat groove alongside a double-time feel, which gives the illusion that the music is speeding up.This is accomplished by moving the backbeat (snare drum) from beats 2 and 4 to the"&"of each beat: Let's take look at how you can add this new hi-hat articulation to your playing:
Hi-Hat This one-handed wide-open sloshy eighth-note hi-hat pattern should be played evenly and forcefully,at a dynamic of fortissimo (very loud) In order to achieve this sound, you will use the shoulder of the stick to simultaneously play both the top and the bottom hi-hat cymbal. This technique (along with the double- time feel) gives the illusion that the B section speeds up and therefore, it generates a lot of musical excitement: Important: Do not use the shoulder/tip motion on the hi-hat because it will only accent the downbeats!
Notation To indicate a half-open hi-hat; a circle with a slash (0) is notated at the beginning of the section over the first hi-hat note

Introduction This ABAB form is augmented with a fermata introduction During this section the entire ensemble will treat the beginning of the song similar to the ending of a song: This is accomplished by playing an extended "out-of-time"flurry of notes Thereafter; to get into Letter A, the drummer must count in the band during the fermata using a very loud one-bar count in: This count in must be done clearly and with authority: Ex 2.9

Unit three reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: R&B-Pop Ballad R&B pop ballads are generally marked by a romantic narrative element; a noticeable rhythmic motif and a traditional structure The most common ballads showcase simple melodies, catchy hooks and the appeal of the ABC arrangement; with both the B and C sections sharply contrasting the A melodically; rhythmically and harmonically This unit's chart focuses on the 6/8-ballad style of Alicia Keys and John Mayer: Structure: The Verse-Pre-Chorus-Chorus Form The verse-pre-chorus-chorus form is common in popular vocal music and it is comprised of three main sections. The verse consists of the main dialogue "storytelling" narrative vocal, the pre-chorus (sometimes called the "rise" or "climb") is used to build into the chorus, and the chorus itself is a memorable lyrical line (often referred to as thehook) In todays chart; these three sections are assigned to rehearsal letters A, B and Crespectively:
Just like the more traditional ABAB and AABA forms; each section represents the same three key musical attributes: a different rhythm (or groove), a particular phrase length, and a particular chord structure: Ex 3.1
Tight Hi-hat/Center Snare
Tight Hi-hat/Cross-stick Snare
5
B
13
Ride Cymbal/Center Snare

Unit 3 - Chapter 1
Pickup Notes/Fill This units song features a pickup fill on beats 5,6and the"&"of beat 6. Pickup fills are notes played before the actual first measure of a song: Due to the slow tempo,you will only use a one measure count-in: You will verbally count beats 1,2 3 and 4,and then proceed to play the fill over beats 5 and 6 like this: Ex 3.2 Your Count: 1-2-3-4 - 5 6 - &
Swung 16th-Note Feel: A swung I6th feel is a 16th-note-triplet-based groove However; this feel is written (in chart form) as 16th notes (sextuplets are implied) This is indicated at the beginning of the chart with the following symbol: Ex 3.3
&
&
Or in this unit's chart by using the text: "Swung 16ths:"
By using either the symbol or text terminology you will be reading straight 16th notes ~yet - these notes are actually performed as I6th-note triplets  In the chart; bar 2 of letter A will look like this (left): Ex 3.4
However; it will be performed as:
Remember; triplets are three notes of the same value that fit into the space normally occupied by two notes of the same value: For example: Ex 3.5
5
5
5
L+ 3 3 3 3 3 3

--- SOURCE: IMG_0728.PNG ---
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter A Letter A (the verse) features a 16th-note based 6/8 groove, which is preformed as a four-bar phrase: All four bars are played on a medium-light hi-hat and utilize a cross-stick-articulation on the snare drum alongside an "off-the-head" bass drum sound: The four-bar phrase looks like this: Ex 3.6
Tight Hi-hat/Cross-stick Snare
This 6/8-ballad groove has been played on thousands of popular recordings Although it is technically simple to play, it is quite difficult to make this groove feel and sound good. You must pay particular attention to make sure that each limb lines up with one another Thus; each time you play a snare (or bass drum) that lands with the hi-hat; they should be in unison, and no flams should be present Please focus on the swung 16th-note (actually I6th-triplet) bass drum on the"&"of 6 (and "&"of 3) as well Ifthe time straightens out to groups of two (i.e. straight I6th notes), you will lose the proper feel:
Cross-Stick Sound In order to get a strong; full and warm cross-stick sound, turn the stick around and use the butt end The bead (tip) of your stick should be placed just "off-center" between the eight and nine oclock position on the head, and the butt end will be off the drum between the two and three oclock position like this: Ex 3.7 (left):
In order to make the stroke, grip the stick in your normal fashion. Then "throw"it into the drum and take your four fingers off the stick At this point your fingers should flatten and you will keep the stick on the head (and rim) by applying pressure: To make the next cross-stick stroke, grab the stick with your fingers again and repeat (above) Ex 3.8
Although this will get you in the general area and enable you to get a solid cross-stick sound, each snare drum is unique and has its own "sweet spot"Therefore; it is extremely important to experiment and find the correct ratio of"on-" and "off-the-drum" positions:
26 Drum Performance 2
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0729.PNG ---
Unit 3 - Chapter 2
Bass Drum The bass drum pattern should be played with conviction, with an open "off-the-head" articulation and a dynamic of mezzo piano (medium soft). This will be accomplished by using the felt side (or a soft-surfaced) bass drum beater; which will give the drum a round, open tone and help blend the bass drum's sound with the snare drum cross-stick dynamic
Letter B Letter B (the pre-chorus) features a similar I6th-note based 6/8 groove; which is now preformed as a four- bar phrase: All four bars utilize the same hi-hat; snare cross-stick and bass drum articulations as Letter A The hi-hat is on all six beats, the snare drum is on beat 4,and the bass drum has quite a few variations to both observe and perform. Ex 3.9
B
Transition Fill There is also a three-beat fill (with a drag rudiment) that connects Letter B (the pre-chorus) to Letter €C (the chorus). Ex 3.10
LL R   R   R
Letter € Letter € (the chorus) features another similar 16th-note based 6/8 groove; which is also preformed as an eight-bar phrase. All eight bars are played utilizing the same open; "off-the-head" bass drum articulation as letters A and B. However; this groove is performed on the ride cymbal, and the hi-hat foot closes on beats 2,3, 5 and 6. The previous cross-stick is now replaced with a center-snare "no rim-shot" articulation as well: Ex 3.11
Ride Cymbal/Center Snare

Rhythmic Breaks with the Band There are also three noteworthy rhythmic breaks that occur between the drums; guitar and bass. They: occur on bar 4, bar 8 (of the first ending) and in the second ending: These should be played very confidently in both time and articulation: Ex 3.12
1. Bar 4
1   2
3   & 4  5   6 & 53
2. Bar 8/First Ending
1   2 3 12
4 trip let 5

Unit 3 - Chapter 2
Sound Review 2: Snare Drum: Center of the Head (No Rim Shot) The snare drum should be played very consistently,in the center of the drumhead. Additionally, each snare hit should be played without a rim shot; which will make the drum sound deep and open in tonality. This stroke is executed by hitting the center of the drumhead with the butt end of the stick
Most drummers think that this is an easier stroke to execute than a rim shot: However; this could not be farther from the truth: In actuality, this motion (and sound) takes a great deal of accuracy and concentration: Let's take a look both steps:
1 . Strike the drum in the center without playing a rim shot This will place the butt end of the stick in the center dot as in ex 3.13,below: 2 Next; while aiming for this spot on the snare head, start the stroke with your wrist:
Ex. 3.13
Bass Drum:"Off the Head" The bass drum pattern should be played very forcefully,with an open articulation This will be accomplished by utilizing two important strategies They are:
1 Using the felt side of (or a soft-surfaced) bass drum beater: 2. Playing the bass drum with an open "off-the-head" (non-buried) stroke: After each stroke it is also very important to keep the beater away from (not buried into) the drumhead: Not only does this allow the drum to resonate more; but it also allows the pitch of the drum to remain lower as well (as it does not get raised by the beater getting buried into the head)
Important:you must make sure to control the bass drum beater and footboard and avoid any unwanted "felt-flutter" between the beater and drum head: In order to play this stroke properly, you must "rest and release" both your foot and the beater between strokes. This is very different from the buried approach, because you cannot keep` your leg raised in "attack" position between strokes Ex 3.14
29
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0732.PNG ---
Other Items of Interest
Subdivide for Tempo and a Correct Bass Drum Placement: Just as in previous units, it is very important that you subdivide in order to stay playing in time and for the proper bass drum placement: Otherwise, you may slow down and/or straighten out your feel Please practice while counting the swung 16th subdivision of"1-&-2-&-3-&-4-&-5-&-6-&"
Ad-Lib Section After the Second Ending: Upon reaching (and playing) the straight 16th-note fill within letter Cs second ending; there will be an additional eight bars based on the intro section: Now that you have established the feel and vibe of the tune;you can take a few liberties during the last eight bars to add your own ideas (as long as the ideas are appropriate to the style of the chart). Also, the accent over the time slash on beat 4 indicates to maintain a backbeat on beat 4. For example: Ex 3.15 What is notated
Ad Lib
Example groove to be played
The Ending: Slight Ritardando The second-to-last bar of the chart indicates to slightly slow the tempo down and ease into the final bar (fermata) This is indicated with the words "slight rit" It is important to make eye contact with the other band members so you are all slowing down at the same pace. It is your job to lead this gradual slowdown, so make sure you are clear and confident when executing this ritardando. Ex 3.16
77 slight rit

Unit fOUN reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Country-Rock Ballad Country rock ballads have been a perennial staple on the charts for decades These tunes capture the emotions contained within bittersweet romances; hard times and pining love: Musically, these tunes are very sophisticated and require extreme musicality from the drummer:
Structure: AABA Form The AABA form is common in popular country music and it is comprised of two main sections The A sections are generally the verse sections and the B sections are the choruses:
Just like other traditional ABAB and/or AAB forms, each section represents the same three key musical attributes: a different rhythm (or groove), a particular phrase length, and a particular chord structure: Ex 4.1

A crescendo is gradual increase in volume within a musical passage It is notated with the symbol below: Ex 4.9
A decrescendo is a gradual decrease in volume within a musical passage It is notated with the symbol below: Ex. 4.10

Bass Drum Sound The bass drum pattern should be played with an open "off-the-head" articulation and a dynamic of piano (soft) . This will be accomplished by using the felt side (or a soft-surfaced) bass drum beater; which will give the drum a round open tone and help blend the bass drum sound with the soft and warm snare drum tone
Letter B
Snare Drum Sound During sections B, A3 and most of the coda, the snare drum switches from soft center-snare sound to a strong backbeat rim shot Make sure the tip of the stick is just past center so you are getting a lot of wood on the drum: This will produce deep and full sound. If the tip of the stick is too close to the rim; then a thinner; high- pitched sound will be  produced. Also, use special care with the note placement of the ghost notes and keep them quiet: Ex 4.14

Bass Drum Sound The bass drum should be big and warm, So maintain the "off-the-head" sound, but play with force and conviction: Burying the beater will cause the drum to choke up and not produce a "round" sound.
Ride Cymbal The ride cymbal should be played with the tip of the stick; but a few"shouldered" strikes will help to spread the sound: By occasionally shouldering the ride; a large amount sonic space gets filled which allows for a more relaxed feel. For the last note (fermata) you should play a very quiet double-stroke roll a few inches from the bell of the ride or crash cymbal: Ex 4.15

Unit five reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: R&B: Motown and Stax During the 1960s and 70s, Motown and Stax were prominent and successful R&B record labels Because of their huge radio and sales success, these label names have actually become sub-genres of R&B music in their own right: Many successful artists of today such as Adele; Bruno Mars, _ Amy ' Winehouse and Ray LaMontagne have dipped into the classic R&B catalog to create their unique Motwon and Stax sounds
Motown Berry Gordy founded the Motown record label during 1959 in Detroit; Michigan. Motown was named after Detroit's most successful product: motor cars. (i.e: the moniker Mo-Town/Motor Town). Employing R&B and pop qualities, Motown saw its greatest success from 1959-1972,and they used a small group of studio musicians to back each of their featured artists. This small group of musicians eventually became known as The Funk Brothers and helped define an ageless sound. The drummers of Motown were Benny Benjamin, Richard "Pistol" Allen and Uriel Jones:
Stax Jim Stewart and Estelle Axton founded the Stax record label in 1957. Based in Memphis, Tennessee; Stax was pop-influenced R&B label that also (like Motown) used one group of musicians to back their entire artist roster This backing band was well-known under the name Booker T and the MG's Their drummer was the influential Al Jackson, Jr:
The drummers of Motown and Stax understood that they needed to compose drum parts that fit the song and made people dance:
Ex 5.1
Benny Benjamin
Al Jackson; Jr;
Ietoxl
"Pistol" Allen
MOTOWN
