Chapter 2: Basic Notation, Structure and Concepts Structure This chart employs a simple verse; chorus, bridge structure The rehearsal letters are assigned as follows: letter A-verse; letter B-chorus, letter C-bridge; and letter D-final chorus Thus, the actual form of this tune would be B,A,A, B, 4, B, C, Dor:
Intro Chorus (eight bars) Verse 1 (eight bars) Verse 2 (eight bars) Chorus (eight bars)
Verse 3 (eight bars) Chorus 2 (eight bars) Bridge (eight bars) Last Chorus (11 bars)
The Intro, B and D Sections The intro, B and D sections of this chart each function as a chorus (hook) These sections feature a genre- specific groove with the snare drum playing on all downbeats (1,.2,3 and 4). The hi-hat plays even eighth notes and opens on the"&"of beat 4 in every other bar (creating a two-bar pattern). The bass drum plays a two-bar I6th-note pattern as well. All together; it looks like this: Ex 5.2
Medium Hi-hat
Snare drum on every downbeat
The A Section This eight-bar phrase uses a two-bar pattern for the first six bars, with the snare drum side-stick on beats 2 and 4.The hi-hat features a mixture of eighth and 16th notes, opening on the"&"of 4 and the"&"of in every other bar; alongside a sparse, two-bar bass drum pattern: Ex 5.33 Tight Hi-hat
The last two bars ofthe A section borrow from the B section by placing the snare drum back on each ofthe downbeats. Ex 5.4

Letter C: The Bridge Letter € functions as the bridge of the tune: A bridge normally occurs near the middle of a tune and uses different chord changes than the rest of the sections Therefore, the drumset ride surface switches from the hi-hat to the ride cymbal, which helps to match this new section (and chord change departure point) The snare drum maintains the quarter-note feel, and the bass drum plays a two-bar pattern: Ex 5.5

Chapter 3: Performance Notes and Sound This classic R&B sound utilizes the same balance of sound used in rock and pop, but none of the kick; snare and hi-hat voices should ever overpower the groove. In general, the sounds should be warm and blend together evenly Thus, the optimum sounds are as follows:
Snare sound: center snare Bass drum: off the head and open: Hi-hat: light and even
Letter A In letter A the hi-hat should be very tight and quiet: You can use the tip of the stick or the upper part of the shaft just under the tip to obtain this sound: For example: Ex 5.6a Upper shaft (just below the tip) Tip
supca JAZZ
The cross-stick sound (on the snare drum) at letter A should be very clear and bright; which is accomplished by striking the rim between two lugs with the butt end of the stick As stated in Unit 3,this will get you in the general area and enable you to get a solid cross-stick sound: Remember; each snare drum is unique and has its own "sweet spot"Therefore; it is extremely important to experiment and find the correct ratio of "on-"and "off-the-drum" positions Ex 5.6b
Letter B In letter B, the "all downbeats" snare drum part should be a center-snare sound with all notes played with the same velocity (do not accent 2 & 4).The bass drum should be played off the head and should blend with the bass player The hi-hat should be played slightly stronger than the A section: This can be accomplished by taking a tiny bit of pressure off of the hi-hat pedal with your foot:

Pickup Notes and Count In The tune starts with a two-beat pickup phrase played by the guitar and bass The drums also play a strong downbeat on beat 3,which should be short and deliberate: One way to orchestrate this figure is to play the hi-hat and snare drum together: For example: Ex 5.7

Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Jazz Jazz was created at the beginning ofthe ZOth century by blending rich African rhythms with sophisticated European harmony (chord structure): Improvisation and group interaction are two of the key elements that make jazz a great vehicle for creative drumming: This unit's chart will focus on a jazz interpretation of the blues, which allows us to focus on the jazz ride cymbal pattern, kick and snare comping, and a 12-bar drum solo
Structure: 12 Bar Blues (I-IV-V) As mentioned in Performance 1, Unit 6,the most common musical form of blues is the 12-bar blues. In this case (because we are in the key of G), the (G7) chord spans the first four bars; the IV (C7) chord typically appears in the second set of four bars and the V (D7) chord is played in the third set of four bars. For example: Ex 6.1
G
1
D
GI
D. C al Coda
Jazz Terminology Review As mentioned in Performance 1,_ Unit 7,there are a few key - terms and concepts related to jazz and blues music that are essential to understand.
Open Form The term open form means that the song is played (and repeated) in 12-bar increments, without a specifically stated ending:
Chorus Unlike a pop music chorus, which refers to "the hook" of a song,a jazz or blues chorus is a term that is slang for "one-time through the form:" If a bandleader says they want to play one chorus of guitar solo (in a 12- bar blues form) , that would be mean one pass through the 12-bar blues form; but with a guitar solo (in place of the melody).
Cue Jazz and blues musicians often give each other the "head" or"top" cue by patting their hand on top of their head,which is usually meant to make sure everybody goes back to the melody the next time the top e ofthe form comes around;

Swing Eighth Notes As mentioned in Performance 1, Units 6 and 7, jazz and blues are usually written (in chart form) as eighth notes, although triplets are implied. This is recognized at the beginning of the chart with the following symbol:
Ex 6.3
=JeJ
This lets the reader of the chart know that every time they read an eighth note, they are to perform it as & "broken" eighth-note triplet: This. applies to the notated drum solo as well:

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound The Jazz Balance of Sound In previous lessons, we have focused on a pop-rock balance of sound This places the main emphasis (and strong dynamic) on the kick and snare; while having the hi-hat placed quite below these within our "players mix" However; in jazz this balance is reversed. It features the ride cymbal as the main (loudest) voice; thehi- hat a close second, with the snare just below the hi-hat and the bass drum as the softest: For example: Ex 6.4
Loudest

The Ride Cymbal: The Foundation of a Good Jazz Time Feel The foundation of jazz drumming is the ride cymbal: The essence of a good jazz cymbal rhythm is the quarter-note pulse;as shown in example 6.5.As soon as you can make that feel good, move on and add the skip note to complete the full jazz ride pattern in example 6.6. It is important to maintain a strong quarter note pulse when adding the skip note: Ex 6.5 Ex. 6.6

--- SOURCE: IMG_0757.PNG ---
Unit 6 - Chapter 2
The normal playing area will be approximately halfway between the edge of the cymbal and the edge of the bell This playing area will give a nice blend between definition and wash: Closer to the bell equals more definition. Closer to the edge equals more wash: It is also important to note that the cymbals typically used in jazz playing are thinner than those used in rock or pop music With these facts in mind, the ride cymbal pattern should create a large blanket of sound, which covers the entire band and connects them all in sound. This does not mean the cymbal should be loud but it must be strong: Ex 6.7
Crash Accents: "Shouldering" the Ride
In rock music; accents are "crashed" on a dedicated crash cymbal In jazz accents are played on the ride cymbal; but they are "shouldered" with the stick This is accomplished by using the shoulder ofthe stick to strike the top surface ofthe cymbal. Not only does this create a full washy sound; but it also helps to control the overtones of the cymbal, as well as making your playing sound more authentic Ex 6.8
The Hi-Hat: The Jazz Backbeat In straight-ahead jazz drumming; the hi-hat is typically played on counts 2 and 4 of the bar This is very similar to pop music's backbeat function There are at least three techniques that may be employed to play the hi-hat with your foot: 1) the rocking motion 2) bouncing the whole leg 3) flat-footed. Use whichever is most comfortable to you: Ex 6.9 J
55
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0758.PNG ---
Bass Drum: Feathering for the Choruses of Guitar Solo Feathering is playing the bass drum on straight quarter notes atan extremely quiet dynamic This articulation should fall underneath each of the other drumset voices. This technique was developed to add an attack and punch to the jazz upright-bass walking feel. The feathered bass drum should be "felt but not heard" If you can hear the bass drum, it is too loud.
In order to achieve this sound; use the felt side of the beater and choke up on the pedal by placing your toes just behind the chain (or strap) of the footboard: Do not bring the beater back too far as it will make the stroke too loud. The beater should only come back about to 1-1/2 inches from the head. Ex. 6.10 (right)
Important: You will e only feather the bass drum during the choruses of guitar solo.
The bass drum strikes exactly with the ride cymbal on counts 1,2,3 and 4 The bass drum, hi-hat; and ride cymbal strike together on counts 2 and 4. You may want to record yourself playing this pattern to make sure that none of the voices are flamming: Ex 6.11
Comping Comping is short for"accompanying" Comping is how the rhythm section instruments, such as piano, guitar; and drums, support both the head (melody) and the soloist (choruses of solo) In each section of the form, comping takes on a different role. Let's take a look at both variations:
Comping the Head During the head,the drums support the melody line by accompanying and accentuating notes within each phrase. These can be accented by either the snare drum or bass drum. However; the ride cymbal pattern and foot hi-hat must keep their patterns unchanged For this reason, it is very important to develop the proper independence to keep the ride and hi-hat going while playing various accents with the snare and/or bass drum:
Let's take a look at bars 1-4 from both the head and drumset chart You will notice that the bass drum and snare drum comp the written guitar melody, with the ride and hi-hat unchanged: For example: Ex 6.12 GI #
Important: For this unit; the bass drum does not feather during the head (melody)
56 Drum Performance 2
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0759.PNG ---
Unit 6 - Chapter 2
Comping the Choruses of Guitar Solo During the choruses of solo, the snare drum and bass drum accompany and react to the soloist This is not the time to display complicated jazz independence exercises underneath your ride cymbal and hi-hat patterns: However; independence is needed to properly execute many of the most tasteful and musical phrases. Therefore; listed below are 12 examples of basic comping patterns for your review: Play the hi-hat foot on beats 2 and 4.These patterns can be played on both the snare drum and bass drum as well or you can mix them up between the bass drum and snare drum:
Comping Examples Ex 6.13
Jazz Drumming: Marking a Song's Form A drummer's most important job is to play a great groove with steady time However; these attributes are not enough to make music. We have another very important duty to perform: marking a songs form. Marking form is outlining (and drawing attention to) each different section within a song: Here; these sections are the head and chorus ofthe 12-bar blues This is accomplished by employing sonic changes to the drumset by changing ride cymbals for each new section of the song: (In jazz, all cymbals are considered rides ) The head should have a particular cymbal/texture and the choruses of solo should have different cymbal/texture:
In today's chart; the following form, performance instructions and cymbal will be used: Ex 6.14
1_
Head (ride cymbal 1) Comping the head (melody)

Chapter 3: The Drum Solo Jazz drumming has a strong tradition in improvisation. This unit's lesson also features a 12-bar drum solo that clearly demonstrates many of the concepts brought forth by Philly Joe Jones; Max Roach and Art Blakey:
Sound Addition: Stick Shot In bars 5-8 and bar 12 ofthis solo, a stick shot is used. A stick shot is usually performed by placing the left stickata 45-degree angle (to the drumhead) Thereafter; you must immediately press the bead (of the stick) into the drumhead: You should apply enough pressure to avoid a buzz sound and slightly mute the head as well The right stick then strikes the left stick and a wood-tone accent is heard This articulation is very common in jazz solo phrases and transition fills (Both traditional and matched grip can be used for this technique) Ex 6.15
Jazz Drum Solo The hi-hat must play on 2 and 4 Ex 6.16

Unit SevEN reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Bossa Nova Bossa nova is a style of Brazilian music popularized by Antonio Carlos Jobim, Vinicius de Moraes and Joao Gilberto. The bossa nova movement (which is Portuguese for "new trend") lasted six years (1958-63),and it contributed a number of songs to the standard jazz repertoire Today's chart will focus on a classic bossa nova drumming method, which is characterized by continuous eighths on the hi-hat (or ride cymbal), a typical two-bar cross-stick pattern and the bass drum playing eighth notes on "1-83-&1" Ex 7.1
Bossa Nova
Notice that this is a two-bar cross-stick phrase The first bar has the pattern being played on beat 1, the"&" of 2 and beat 4.The second bar has the cross-stick pattern on beats 2 and the"&"of 3.It is also important to keep the bass drum pattern consistent; without any rhythmic variations
AAB Open Form The form of this chart is AAB with each section being eight bars in length: This form is open (similar to the open 12-bar blues form), which means the AAB (24-bar) form circulates after the head (melody over AAB the first time) for solo choruses. With these facts in mind; the normal usages of the head and chorus (from Unit 6) still apply: Head = melody Chorus one time through the form
Form Addition: Four-Bar Intro Vamp Unit 7's chart has a four-bar introduction that augments the AAB form: During the introduction, the drums continue to play the bossa nova ostintato while opening the hi-hat on the "&"of beat 4 and on beat 1_ Ex 7.2
The phrase "vamp until cue"is used to instruct you to continue to play the four-bar intro until you receive a visual cue (usually a confirming head nod). Since the guitar plays the melody at A,the guitar player will be in charge of giving the cue You must make sure you are keeping an eye on the guitar player during the intro. The four-bar intro may be played for varying lengths of time the song is performed, but the duration will always be a multiple of four (8, 12, 16,20,etc ). Ex 7.3 Bossa _ 108 Vamp_ until cue
After Cue
5

Unit 7 Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound As you have seen in the last unit's jazz lesson, we featured the ride cymbal as the main (loudest) voice; the hi-hata close second,with the snare is just below the hi-hat and the bass drum as the softest: The bossa nova balance of sound is quite similar: In this new context; the overall dynamic is quite quiet; at a dynamic of mezzo piano (medium soft) The hi-hat and ride articulation will be the strongest voice; but they . should not be as strong as last week's jazz ride: In addition; the cross-stick sound will be equal to the snare drum volume position. For example: Ex 7.4 Loudest Bossa Nova Balance of Sound

Crash Accents:"Shouldering" Either the Ride or Crash Cymbal Just like in jazz, cymbal accents are "shouldered" However; in this new Brazilian context; these articulations can be played on either the ride or crash cymbals This is accomplished by using the shoulder of the stick to strike the top surface of the ride cymbal (Ex. 7.5) or the outermost edge' ofthe crash cymbal (Ex. 7.6). Not only does this create a full washy sound; but it also helps to control the overtones of the cymbal as well as making a more stylistically authentic sound. Ex 7.5 Ex. 7.6

--- SOURCE: IMG_0766.PNG ---
The Hi-Hat: The Bossa Backbeat Just as in straight-ahead jazz drumming; the bossa nova hi-hat is also played on counts 2 and 4 of the bar: This is very similar to pop music's backbeat function. There are at least three techniques that may be employed: 1) heel up 2) heel down 3) bouncing the whole leg: Please use whichever is most comfortable for you: Ex 7.7
Important: You will play on the ride cymbal and close the hi-hat foot on beats 2 and 4 during the choruses of guitar solo
Bass Drum: Proper Bossa Sound The bossa nova bass drum sound features the dotted-quarter/eighth rhythm at an extremely quiet dynamic (see example 7.4). This articulation should fall beneath all of the other drumset voices Please be careful to make both notes even in volume: Many drummers mistakenly accent the second note of each grouping; which is not authentic or appropriate. Ex 7.8
Correct
Incorrect
In order to achieve this quiet sound, use an open "unburied" stroke and the felt side of the bass drum beater Next; choke up on the pedal by placing your toes just behind the chain (or strap) of the footboard. Do not bring the beater back too far; this will make the stroke too loud The beater should only come back about 2 to 2-1/2 inches from the head. Ex. 7.9
The bass drum strikes exactly with the hi-hat (or ride cymbal) on counts "1-83-&1."You may want to record yourself playing this pattern to make sure that none of the voices are flamming: Also, the bass player plays this rhythm as well, so make sure to listen and lock in with the bass player: Ex 7.10

Comping Part 2: Duration Long Notes In the last unit's jazz example; the drums comped nearly all of the written melody: In this chart; the drums support the melody line by accompanying and accentuating the long notes within each phrase. In this context, the eighth notes and quarter notes would be considered short notes: Any half notes or tied notes (where the tie extends the duration of the note) would be considered a long note. For example: (S= short notes; L = long notes) Ex 7.11
Em7665 A7b9) Dmi A7*9) E S $ S L S L S L
S
S L
Long Notes in Use: Now let's take a look at bars 1-4 from both the head and drumset chart: You will notice that the drums accentuate the long notes within the melody by playing "shouldered" crashes. These articulations mimic the long duration of the selected (tied) guitar notes Note: In this chart these long notes are highlighted with an accent purely to aid visually, and should not actually be hit harder The bossa pattern continues throughout: For example: Ex 7.12

Unit eight reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Reggae Reggae is music genre developed in Jamaica during the late 1960s and popularized internationally by Bob Marley and the Wailers Within this style;, Carlton Barrett (drummer for the Wailers) created the one-drop and four-on-the-floor rhythms, which he used alongside very syncopated cross-stick and hi-hat groove embellishments Both grooves can be performed with a straight or shuffle feel: Later; in the 1970s and early 1980s, DJs in the UK began to play reggae songs during their sets. Soon thereafter; The Clash; Fishbone; UB4O and The Police began to incorporate reggaes one-drop and four-on- the-floor rhythms into their music: In recent years, reggae has been revitalized by many mainstream acts; including Sublime; No Doubt; Audiodub and the B-Foundation With this in mind, this week's chart is in the modern tradition of Sublime: It features both the one-drop and four-on-the-floor grooves (and their embellishments) within a shuffle feel,
One-Drop The one-drop is named after the Wailers' song ofthe same name; and this groove shifts the snare cross-stick backbeat and bass drum to the third beat of the bar: Beat is left empty, which allows for large open rhythmic and melodic improvisations Ex 8.1
3
One-Drop Note: It is very easy to speed up (or slow down) while playing the one-drop. Most drummers are accustomed to centering and solidifying their groove by playing beat of each bar on the bass drum You must subdivide triplets in each bar and imagine a huge beat on the bass drum--without playing itl
Four-on-the-Floor Thefour-on-the-floor bass drum pattern plays four solid beats to the bar; giving the music an insistent drive: An example of this groove can be heard in "Exodus" (by Bob Marley and the Wailers). Oftentimes, the snare drum rim shot is used in place of the one-drops cross-stick articulation as well: Ex 8.2
2
3
70 Drum Performance 2

Repeat Instructions: 3X ("Three Times") At the end of letter B (the first time) you will see a repeat sign instructing you to return to letter A. Since the A and B sections need to be played three times, the instruction "3X"is placed by the repeat sign at the end of letter B. This does not mean to repeat three times but rather to play this sequence (letters A and B) three times in total. Ex 8.9 3X
Letter C: Guitar Solo Vamp Letter C diverts from the AB form of the tune and goes into a two-bar vamp for a guitar solo section: A basic four-on-the-floor reggae groove is to be maintained (as notated), but you must add your own embellishments and interact with the soloist Since letter € is marked with the directive "Guitar Solo: Vamp until cue" you must continue to loop the two bars of Cuntil the soloist (guitar) provides a cue: Remember; once you receive the cue (head nod, big "yes" eyes), you must move to letter D after the second bar of C Do not move to letter D after the first bar of letter C Ex 8.10

Unit 8 Chapter
UnIt Ne reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Cumbia Cumbia began as a courtship dance practiced among the African population: In the 1800s, cumbia found its way to South and Central America and became a popular form of music, most notably in Colombia and Panama. In the modern era, cumbia has splintered into many sub-genres such as cumbia chicha, technocumbia, cumbia villera, tropical movement and new Chilean cumbia. Many popular hybrid-rock bands have also adopted the rootsy sound of cubia, like Ozolomati and Lyrics Born:
The AAB Form This study employs a similar structure to many popular tunes, the AAB form: In this particular tune the section lengths are as follows: A (eight bars) , A2 (ten bars) , B (eight bars) The tune uses a D. S.al Coda and an extended use of a four-bar repeated section in the coda. You will play a different groove for each section and there is also a new groove within the coda.
Intro Fill As in past units, this chart begins with a two-beat drum fill Although a count-off is not required, (and since the fill does not start until the"ah" of 2), you may verbally count "one, two" into the fill, like this: Ex 9.1

After this ensemble figure is played; the next section is a four-bar phrase containing a basic soca beat: Soca beats are easy "island" grooves that can be used in a multitude of styles. Percussion Emulation In traditional cumbia, the grooves are created by a section of percussionists (cowbell, guiro, clave, conga, timale; etc, each played by a separate musician). As a drumset player; you must create a groove that emulates this percussion section:
Cross-Stick Articulation In letter A; quarter-note cross-stick pattern will replace a cowbell, jam block or the side of a timbale. Make sure to play this consistently, avoiding any flamming between the cross-stick and the bass drum: A great way to practice this is to remove the hi-hat part and focus on the snare/bass drum synchronization. Ex 9.9
Hi-Hat In the A and A2 sections the hi-hat should emulate the pattern (and sound) of a merengue guiro. The merengue guiro is a metallic (usually aluminum), cylindrical instrument; which is played with a metal scraper called a guira. Ex 9.10

UnIt ten Chapter 1: Rap-Metal Straight Eighth and Two-Handed 16th-Note Grooves During the past decade, many of the biggest rock acts fused the most aggressive elements of hip-hop and heavy metal into one cohesive sound This style became extremely popular by incorporating syncopated beats and rhythmic textures into an alternative rock and metal setting: This movement spawned many successful bands such as Korn; Limp BizKit and Linkin Park Within this play-along, you will find many grooves and textures that represent the style: Structure: The AB 16-Bar Form This chart employs a classic musical structure common to rock and pop music: the AB 16-bar form: This form is comprised of two different sections, and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A or B). In addition, both letters (sections) represent three key musical attributes. They are: a different rhythm (or groove), an eight-bar phrase, and a particular guitar riff and chord structure:
So how does this information apply to this chart? Well, let's examine each key - element individually:
1.A different rhythm (or groove): As stated earlier; there are two letters within this form, and each one now represents a particular rhythm (or groove): As a result; there are two grooves present within this chart: For example:
The A section has a two-handed 16th-note groove: Ex 10.1
Tight Hi-hat
The B section has a one-handed eighth-note groove: Ex 10.2
BLoose Hi-hat
84 Drum Performance 2

--- SOURCE: IMG_0789.PNG ---
Unit 10 - Chapter 1
2. An eight-bar phrase In addition to a particular groove, each letter also denotes the use of an eight-bar phrase Therefore, the A section features eight bars of a two-handed 16th-note groove and letter B features eight bars of a one- handed eighth-note groove: For example: Ex 10.3
Tight Hi-hat
777777777777
27 2
2
BlLoose Hi-hat
2
2
3.A particular guitar riff and chord progression Once more; both letters are also centered on a particular guitar riff and chord progression: In this case; the A section utilizes a unique guitar riff and a chord progression: The B section clearly differs by using another distinctive guitar riff and a chord progression.
As a result; the A section is comprised of a two-handed 16th-note groove, which is eight bars in length: Conversely, the B section is comprised of a one-handed 8th-note groove, which is also eight bars in length.

==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0790.PNG ---
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Now that you understand this form, it is up to you to simulate (and draw attention to) these sectional differences by employing small sonic and dynamic changes inyour playing: Remember; it is not enough to merely play the correct patterns; you must play the correct patterns with the correct sound, feel, and form: With this in mind, we will break down each section and describe the appropriate hi-hat; snare, and bass drum articulations:
Letter A Ex 10.4
Tight Hi-hat
Just as the A section has a particular groove, phrase length, and chord progression, it will also have a particular drum sound. Therefore, this section features a light (yet consistent) two-handed 16th-note hi-hat groove alongside a very staccato-syncopated bass drum and strong snare backbeat Let's take a lookat how you can achieve these articulations:
Bass Drum The bass drum pattern should be played very forcefully, with a staccato articulation and a dynamic of forte (loud) . As mentioned in Performance 1, Unit 1, this sound is accomplished by burying the beater into the bass drum head.
Snare Drum The snare drum should be played very consistently and ata dynamic of forte (loud). Additionally, each snare hit should be a rim shot: As mentioned in Performance 1, Unit 1,this sound is accomplished by striking the snare drum and rim simultaneously with power and conviction:
Hi-Hat This two-handed alternating I6th pattern should be played evenly and softly at a dynamic of piano (soft). In order to achieve this sound (as mentioned in Performance 1, Unit 4), you will use the shoulder of the stick just beneath the tip. Use this part ofthe stick to strike the edge (not the top) ofthe hi-hat cymbals This will give you a very thick, yet dynamically controlled hi-hat tone. In comparison, by bringing the hi-hat volume lower; it also creates a dynamic illusion, making the bass drum and snare drum appear to sound louder within the overall drum mix
86 Drum Performance 2
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0791.PNG ---
Unit 10 - Chapter 2
All Together The end result will be a strong; punchy and very authoritative bass drum sound alongside a karate-chop- like rim shot and thick (yet dynamically controlled) hi-hat: This approach perfectly compliments the arpeggiated guitar patterns and rhythmically-driven synth textures within letter A
Letter B Ex 10.5
BJLoose Hi-hat
In addition to letter A's bass drum and snare drum sounds, this section features a wide-open sloshy eighth- note hi-hat groove. Let's take a look at how you can add this new hi-hat articulation to your drumming arsenal:
Hi-Hat This one-handed wide-open sloshy eighth-note hi-hat pattern should be played evenly and forcefully,at a dynamic of fortissimo (very loudly). As stated in Performance 1, Unit 4,in order to achieve this sound, you will use the middle-shoulder of the stick: This sound (and technique) gives the illusion that the B section speeds up slightly and therefore it generates a large amount of musical excitement:
Important: Do not use the shoulder/tip motion on the hi-hat because it will only accent the downbeats! This will sound very old-fashioned, and it will not sound exciting!
==================================================

Fully Notated

Letter B The fill on bar 8 of letter B (measure 20) is a hand/foot I6th-note fill that should start with the right hand: You should also mentally prepare to play the cymbal crashes on beat of the following measure For example: Ex 10.10
R L   R  L

About the Authors
Donny Gruendler was born and raised in the diverse musical  surroundings of Detroit, Michigan: As result; Gruendler grew up alongside an unusually broad range of influences including soul, funk; pop, hip hop; traditional swing, hard bop; techno, house and blues.
At age 20, Gruendler graduated from Berklee College of Music with Bachelor of Music degree At age 21, he earned his Master of Music degree from Wayne State University in Detroit: Now living in Los Angeles, Gruendler has performed, programmed; toured and recorded behind such artists as Kenny Burrell, John Medeski; DJ Logic the Funk Brothers, DJ A-Ski (Unique 74), Rick Holmstrom,and Kirk Fletcher: He has composed and/or played on jingles for Axe Body Spray; RE/MAX on the Boulevard, and NPR. His select film/TV credits include Last Holiday, Father of Invention, and the Showtime feature Chicago Overcoat: Donny is also a noted producer under the pseudonym Inc, and one half of the funky-soul-electro duo Rhett Frazier Inc: Okayplayer describes their productions as cosmic brilliance delivered via a well-stirred pot of soul, jazz, rock; funk and gospel:
In the education realm, Gruendler is Vice President of Instruction and Curricular Development at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California, and he is also a member of the Modern Drummer education team and a frequent columnist for the magazine: Donny has also released several instructional books, DVDs and other online/digital content under his own name: For more information; please visit WWWdonnygruendlercom
94 Drum Performance 2
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0799.PNG ---
Stewart Jean was born and raised in Deptford, New Jersey, where he was introduced to music through his father's love of classical music and his friends' Led Zeppelin LPs. Stewart's passion for music grew as he studied drumset; timpani and mallets in high school. Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician:
leuug
At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree; Stewart became a sought- after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings, tours and shows.
After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles, maintaining a steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the-point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists'lists.
Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years; Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves; Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards.

--- SOURCE: IMG_1102.PNG ---
Unit ONE reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Pop-Rock Ghost-Note Grooves Pop-rocks primary focus is on big, catchy, and instantly memorable melodies alongside rock instrumentation; precise grooves and strong; definitive backbeat: In addition, pop-rock also has an emphasis on the professional craft of songwriting and record-making: The songs are tightly constructed in both groove and melody, with no wasted space, extraneous notes or detours from the original theme. The production is clean; polished,and bright; which makes full use of current recording technology Thus; pop-rock is polished, catchy and energetic enough to appeal to younger listeners, but clean and safe enough for adults to enjoy too.With its commercial accessibility; this genre produced some ofthe biggest stars ofthe 1980s, 90s and 2000s,such as Elton John, Sting; Avril Lavigne; Michelle Branch; Gavin Degraw and Sheryl Crow: Todays chart is in the modern tradition of Sheryl Crow and groove masters Shawn Pelton and Steve Jordan.
Structure: The ABC 24-Bar Form This unique 24-bar form is comprised of three different sections, and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A, B and C). Similarly to previous performance books, each letter (section) represents three key musical attributes- They - are (1.) a particular rhythm, (24) a particular chord structure (and melody) and (3.), a specific phrase length The A section is four bars long; the B section is 12 bars long (with the aid of a repeat) and the C section is eight bars long: Altogether; these three sections make up the 24-bar form: For example: Ex 1.1
A: 4bars
8 Closed Hi-hat
B: 12 bars (6-bar phrase repeated)
24 bars total
Medium Hi-hat
C: 8 bars
Fill
D S al Coda (with repeats)
Drum Performance 3
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1103.PNG ---
Unit Chapter 1
Letter A Letter A features a unique four-bar phrase alongside a very syncopated 16th-note based train groove: Not only does the bass drum have quite a bit of movement within the I6th note pulse; but also the snare drum is not your standard train backbeat either: In addition, you should now utilize a rim shot articulation on the snare drum: There are a few 32nd notes within this phrase and they should be played as double strokes as well; Ex 1,.2 A
Snare Drum Train Variations Although letter A is a four-bar phrase, there are snare drum variations within bars 2, 3 and 4. These measures are comprised of various rim shots (and 32nd notes), which add excitement to a normally stagnant train groove: The phrase ends with a five-stroke roll leading into a break on beat 3 (of bar 4): Ex 1,.3
A
3

--- SOURCE: IMG_1104.PNG ---
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter B Letter B features a two-bar; very syncopated eighth and 16th-note based groove; which utilizes the snare drum; bass drum and open and closed hi-hat voices. The snare drum employs a standard backbeat and a typical ghost note pattern that is performed on both the"e"of beats 1 and 3 and on the"ah"of beats 2 and 4.These ghost notes are played extremely softly, which should be "felt rather than heard" Please also notice the hi-hat opening on the"&"of beat 3 in bar 2 ofthe phrase: Ex 1.4
B
Letter B Ghost Note Exercises: The Hands Hi-Hat and Snare Drum Before attempting Letter B's groove, it is extremely important that you understand how the snare drum ghost pattern fits within the eighth-note hi-hat pattern (and resulting pulse). Please make sure to practice this pattern slowly and evenly; while subdividing 16th notes: Once it becomes comfortable, you should gradually work up to this unit's performance tempo: 86 BPM: Ex 1.5
1 e & 2 & ah 3 e & 4 & ah 1 e & 2 & ah   3  e & 4   & ah
Letter B Preliminary Ghost Note Exercises: Isolate Each Bass Drum At 86 BPM,it would be very easy to rush these ghost notes, play them too loudly, flam them with the bass drum and generally make the groove feel uneven: Therefore, we have compiled an exhaustive set of preliminary exercises that will helpyou to isolate each bass drum note against the ghost note pattern: Each exercise adds an additional bass drum; which will help you maintain your grooves consistency and become comfortable with the required coordination as well: In order to play perfect unisons between the snare and bass drum; please make sure to subdivide these exercises within a I6th-note click at a slow tempo; before working up to 86 BPM Ex 1.6 Development of Bar One (of the Two-Bar Pattern)

--- SOURCE: IMG_1105.PNG ---
Unit Chapter 2
Ex 1.7
Development of Bar Two (of the Two-Bar Pattern)
Both Bars Together:
B
Articulations In addition to the normal "bury the beater" bass drum and snare drum rim shot backbeat articulations; Letter B will feature a chunky"shouldered"hi-hat sound and mezzo-piano snare drum ghost note sound. Lets take a look at how to achieve these new articulations: Ex 1.8 (below) Hi-Hat: Chunky"Shouldered" Eighths This one-handed chunky eighth-note hi-hat pattern should be played evenly and forcefully, at a dynamic of mezzo-forte (medium loud): In order to achieve this sound, you will use a loose grip and the shoulder of the stick to simultaneously play both the top and the bottom hi-hat cymbal. This technique (along with the snare ghost notes and syncopated bass drum pattern) creates a thick sound, which makes the groove feel fatter; and also gives the illusion that the song is faster than 86 BPM
Important: Do not use the shoulder/tip motion on the hi-hat because it will only accent the downbeats! This will sound very old-fashionedl Example: Rather than an articulation of "chick-chick-chick-chick" think instead of your hi-hat saying "chunk-chunk-chunk-chunk"
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1106.PNG ---
Important: Do not use the tip of the stick on the top of the hi-hat This can sound very corny and drum machine-likel Ex 1.9 (below) Mezzo-Piano Snare Drum Ghost Notes The snare drum employs a standard backbeat and typical ghost note pattern that is performed on both the"e"of beats 1 and 3,and on the "ah" of beats 2 and 4. These ghost notes are played extremely softly; which should be "felt rather than heard" In order to achieve this ghosted sound,you will use the tip of the stick in the center of the snare drum with a light "drop" stroke. This "drop" stroke is achieved by loosening the fingers (which lets the stick drop and lightly tap the head) rather than a full wrist-motioned down stroke (Ex 1.9). For the next backbeat; re-grip the stick around the fingers, lift your hand (from the previous ghost note position) and strike your rim shot as you would normally (Ex 1.10).
Please keep in mind that this technique will take a bit of effort to achieve. Be diligent and patient and you will be able to play these patterns successfully!
Ex 1.9 (above)
Letter € Letter C features a one-bar phrase alongside a very syncopated eighth and 16th-note based groove; which utilizes the snare drum, bass drum and a medium eighth-note hi-hat voice The snare drum employs the same standard backbeat and an additional typical ghost note pattern: Ex 1.11 Medium Hi-hat
Letter C Preliminary Ghost Note Exercises: Isolate Each Bass Drum Just as in Letter Bs groove, it would be very easy to rush these ghost notes; play them too loudly, flam them with the bass drum and generally make them feel uneven Therefore; we have compiled an additional set of preliminary exercises that will help you to isolate each bass drum note against the ghost note and hi-hat patterns: Ex 1.12

Letter C: Recurring Ensemble Figure Letter € (and C2) has ensemble figures on the "&"of beat 3 and the"ah"of beat 4 within both bars 4 and 8 of the phrases For example: Ex 1.13

Letter C: Setup Fills The ensemble figures within letters C and C2 are preceded by a 1-1/2-beat fill marker: As these ensemble figures are very syncopated, you must be sure to play a very solid downbeat on beat 3, which will help to "set up" (and therefore telegraph) their arrival. Here is a short list of (possible) practical set up fills: Ex 1.14
Filling Between Figures In addition to forecasting these figures, you must also make sure these figures are played properly (and consistently). To ensure proper beat placement of these notes; it is best to fill in the missing I6th notes (between the two figures) with ghost notes on the snare drum: For example: Ex 1.15

--- SOURCE: IMG_1108.PNG ---
Ride Cymbal with Upbeat Eighth-Note Accents at Letter C2 Ex 1.16 Ex 1.17 The un-accented note on the downbeat: In order to achieve this sound,you will use the tip ofthe stick; just beneath the tip. Next; use this part ofthe stick to strike the body of the cymbal. However; because you are striking the bell on the downbeats, you will not be able to strike the ride in the normal spotl Your hand will not be able to reposition itself that quickly. With this in mind,you will use the tip of the stick in the left- hand center part of the cymbal (to the left of the bell itself) See Ex 1.16 at left:
The accented note on the upbeat: In order to achieve this accented sound, you will use the shoulder ofthe stick, a third of the way down from the tip, as shown in Ex 1.17. Ex 1.18
AIl Together In order to play these two notes back to back, you must use your fingers (and partial wrist) on the quiet (unaccented) note on the downbeat,and your wrist for the loud (accented) note on the upbeat: Overall the motion is a small movement; on the side of the cymbal from left to right; as shown in the two photos of Ex. 1.18 above.
The Coda The coda features a short five-bar breakdown section utilizing only the hi-hat (with accents) and bass drum: Notice that the fourth bar of the coda is a bar of 2/4.Be sure to count through the 2/4 bar and re-set your count back to 1 on the next bar of 4/4. There is a guitar lick on bar five followed by a written-out drum fill: For example: Ex 1.19

Unit twO reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Two-Handed Swung 16th-Note Grooves Modern rock is a commonly used term, which describes the rock music format found on commercial radio. This style combines the guitar riffs and melodic song structure of classic rock with the ear-splitting guitar noise and attitude of post-Nirvana alternative rock Modern rock was also responsible for the mainstream return of the traditional guitar; bass and drums trio format that had been lost during the synth-pop 1980s era. With its overblown production, fat-syncopated grooves, catchy melodies and mainstream accessibility, modern rock produced some ofthe biggest stars ofthe 1980s, 90s and 2000s. These include Jesus and Mary Chain, Alice in Chains, 311, Paramore, Muse and Green Day:
Form: ABC Sections This unit's form is comprised of three different sections,and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A, Band C) Just like Unit 1, each letter (section) represents three key musical attributes. They are (1.) a particular rhythm; (2 ) a particular chord structure (and melody), and (3.) a specific phrase length With this in mind; the A (combined with A2) section is 16 bars long (with the use of first and second endings) , the B section is eight bars long; andthe C section is ten bars long: All together;these three sections make up a 34-bar form: For example: Ex.2.1
A &A2: 16 bars
A2
B

(no repeats)

Structure Additions: Four-Bar Intro The form is augmented with a four-bar intro section which ends with an abrupt stop on the "ah" of 2 in bar 4. Ex 2.2 Tight Hi-hat
B2 This form is also supplemented with a 16-bar section, which is simply the B section repeated with an added four-beat fill in bar 8. Sonically (and to give the tune an emotional lift) , the drums switch from the opening/closing hi-hat to the ride cymbal, like this: Ex 23 B2 Ride Cymbal Bell
Fill Z23
This chart is played with a swung I6th-note feel This is stated by the text"swung 16th"sat the top of the chart: Ex 2.4 Swung I6ths 101
Tight Hi-hat
Swung 16th Phrasing Although this chart is notated with straight 16th notes, each note and groove should be phrased as swung I6th notes Swung I6th notes are actually sixteenth-note triplets with the first and fifth note removed. In the example below;you will notice that the proper I6th triplet counting is notated in the top staff,with the swung I6th-note counting applied directly beneath it: In addition, full sixteenth note triplets are notated on the bottom staff for your reference: Ex 2.5
trip let
trip let
trip let
trip let
trip let
trip let
trip let
trip let
Before the Count-In: Swung 16ths Before counting the tune in, be sure to subdivide swung 16ths in your head. Not only will this help you feel the proper placement and feel of the tune; but it will also ensure the correct placement of the bass drum notes on the"ah" of 4 in bar 2 and the"ah"of 2 in bar 4.

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter A Letter A features a two-bar swung I6th phrase alongside a syncopated 16th-note bass drum groove; two- handed 16th-note hi-hat pattern and snare backbeat The backbeat will be played with the right hand while the hi-hat is played with alternating (RLRL) sticking: Ex 2.6

etc_

Practicing with Proper Subdivisions at a Slow Tempo: Insure the Proper Feel It will be quite challenging to keep the snare accent note (and overall two-handed hi-hat groove) from rushing and straightening out to normal (straight) 16th notes With this in mind, while practicing this pattern, it will be very helpful to put your metronome on sixteenth-note-triplets and slowly count the proper sextuplet phrasing: For example: Ex 2.9

This section's groove also features an opening/closing hi-hat The hi-hat opens on every upbeat and must stay consistent throughout the section. When opening the hi-hat with your foot; just open it enough to let some air in: Donot lift_your foot_ completely off_of_the_pedal as this will cause the hi-hat to open too wide, producing a weak and sloppy sound: Ex 2.10

Unit 2 - Chapter 2
Anticipated Figures or "Pushes" During letter B, the figure on the "ah" of 4 occurs within every other bar The band actually plays the next chord on the"ah" of four instead of the downbeat of (of the next bar), producing a "pushed" syncopation: Try' playing these crashes with the left hand while the right hand maintains the hi-hat pattern. For example: Ex 2.11
B
+ 0
Practicing with Proper Subdivisions at a Slow Tempo: Insure the Proper Feel Again, it will be quite challenging to keep the bass drum and snare drum notes (and overall groove hand pattern) from rushing and straightening out to normal (straight) 16th notes. In addition, it will also be challenging to properly place the "ah" of beat 4 in every bar on the bass drum: With this in mind, while practicing this pattern, it will be very helpful to put your metronome on sixteenth-note-triplets and slowly count the proper sextuplet phrasing: For example: Ex 2.12
blp lot & blp lot
blp Iot & trp lot 3 tlp Iot & triplot
trp Iot & tlplot
blp Iot
trp Iot 2 tlp bot & trip Iot 3 ulp Iot

Letter C Letter C is a ten-bar section that features a one-bar pattern alongside a very syncopated eighth and 16th- note based tom groove: The bass drum plays a four-on-the-floor pattern, the right hand rides on the floor tom, and the left hand travels between the snare drum and rack tom like this: Ex 2.13 Left hand on 2,4and the"ah" of 4

Ex 2.14
Riding the Floor Tom on Eighth Notes This eighth-note pattern should be played evenly and medium- forcefully at a dynamic of mezzo-forte: In order to achieve this sound, you will use the tip of the stick, with a loose right-hand grip; to strike the center of the floor tom. This will give you a thick and resonant floor tom tone Important: Please be careful to avoid playing just above or below the center of the head. If this occurs, you will not hear the power and fundamental tone of the floor tom:
Musical note: This technique should make the floor tom sound Iike a"low drone"that surrounds the entire band. Ifyou hit the floor tom too hard, off-center or with a tight/tense grip, it will sound too staccato, choked and stiff:
Letter C: Composed Fills After playing the one-bar tom-based groove for seven bars, this chart employs a fully notated fill within bar 8. This fill begins with 32nd notes on the snare drum on the "ah"of 4 in bar 7. The fiIl ends on the"ah"of 4 in bar 8 with a flam on the snare drum: For your reference, the suggested sticking is as follows: Ex 2.15

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter A: Groove and Sound The groove at letter A features a two-bar pattern with the hi-hat playing 16th notes with a tight sound The snare drum plays on beats 2 and 4; the drum should be tuned to a high pitch and played with a rim shot articulation: The bass drum plays a very sparse I6th note-based pattern played with force, but with the beater coming off of the head (for an"open" bass drum sound)  Before playing this groove at 88 BPM; begin at 70 BPM and work your way up to 88 BPM* Ex 3.9
Tight Hi-hat
The"ah" ofthebeat
*Anote on tension: When you are practicing the continuous I6th notes on the hi-hat, you must be aware of any tension in both your hand and arm. It is imperative that you stay loose and relaxed. You will know that you are tense if there is no space between your thumb and index finger: For example: Ex 3.10
Correct: No tension present
Incorrect: Tension present
Tension: Sound Tension is harder to recognize when you are young and still developing your drumming skills. The desire to learn something (without paying attention to the physicality behind it) can very detrimental to your progress in the long run Not only is it physically very dangerous to play with tension; but the sound produced also suffers To hear this for yourself; play a ride cymbal with the stick held tightly in your hand The sound will be very tight and pingy: Now play the ride with the stick loose in your hand (imagine you are holding an egg). Upon striking the cymbal, you will hear a bright; vibrant and wide sound. Please keep this in mind when practicing and performing:

Unit 3 - Chapter 2
Letter A: Fills There are three written out fills within letter A. These three fills have a common rhythmic theme; or motif; that helps to create a thread of creativity and development through this section. All three fills have variations on beats and 2, but beats 3 and 4 have the same rhythms (on differing voices each time). For example: Ex 3.11 7 ;' Theme (motif)
First ending: Bass drum focus w/hi-hat
Second ending: Snare drum focus
)
Last bar of A: Bass drum focus wlsnare (flam)
Half Open Hi-Hat Sound Over the past two books we have explored closed (+) (with varying degrees of sound) and open (0) hi-hat sounds In this chart we now see a new symbol: 0. This symbol instructs us to play the hi-hat half open and very"sloshy". A common characteristic in R&B/hip-hop drumming is to play open-handed with the left hand on the hi-hat and the right hand travelling around the toms This is a great effect to use when you need to "build" into a bigger fill. The wash of the half-open hi-hat paired with the open toms creates nice sheen with an added rumbling undercurrent:
Drop-Outs Another common technique within this genre is the concept of"drop-outs" Drop-outs are short interruptions ofa groove that last for about a beat or two. This concept developed along with DJs'use of drum machines, MPCs, loops and vinyl records. The producer would push "mute" on the mixing console (while the groove was playing) and upon its return, the groove did not restart; but rather continue (as if it has never stopped) There is one drop-out in this chart; in bar 10 of letter A (bar 15 of the entire chart) For example: Ex 3.12 Regular Groove Drop-out
Omit these notes (circled above) to create the "muted mixing console" drop-out:

Letter B: Groove and Sound At letter B, the playing surface switches to the ride cymbal The ride cymbal pattern is the same as the hi- hat pattern (continuous swung I6ths) and must be played with clear sound, avoiding too much "washiness:" Just like letter A,the groove at letter B is a two-bar pattern with significant syncopation present in the snare drum and bass drum parts However; beat 1 ofthe A and B patterns are different: In letter A the bass drum plays on "1 & "where in letter B the bass drum plays"1 ah"on beat 1. This can be an easy detail to miss, creating confusion most notably between the drummer and bass player: Also, notice the snare drum hit on the "ah" of 2 in each bar: These notes should be played at the same sound level as beats 2 and 4,with a rim shot For example: Ex 3.13 BlRide Cymbal
All rim shots
Note: Although it is not notated (for claritys sake), try to play eighth notes with your foot on the hi-hat while paying the letter B groove. This will help all ofthe parts mesh together
Letter B: Ensemble Figures The ensemble figures in bars 9 and 10 of letter B should be played verbatim with no rhythmic interpretation or set-up fills present This stagnant effect will create dramatic musical tension. Here we have included a few ways to play these figures: Ex 3.14

Coda: Groove and Sound The coda also has a two-bar pattern featuring a swung I6th-note feel The playing surface switches to the snare drum; with the right hand playing the continuous 16th notes on the snare drum (notated with the triangular notehead*) and the left hand playing a side-stick sound on beats 2 and 4. With the left hand resting on the snare drum (for the side-stick), a muted sound will be created When practicing this technique; be sure to explore different sound possibilities within this concept (to personalize your take) The right hand should not be too loud and should be played with the tip of the stick striking the head about two inches in from the hoop For example: Ex 3.15

Unit fOUC reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Swung 16th-Note Grooves wIGhost Notes Soul-funk's primary focus is on memorable melodies alongside funk instrumentation, swinging grooves and strong; definitive backbeat: In addition, soul-funk places an equal emphasis on the professional craft of songwriting, jazz improvisation and funky grooves With its commercial accessibility, soul-funk produced some of the biggest stars of the 1960s, 70sand 2000s such as Stevie Wonder; Sly and the Family Stone, Amy Winehouse; The Roots, and Sharon Jones and The Dap Kings This week's chart is in the tradition of Stevie Wonder's classic recording period of the albums Innervisions and Songs in the Key € of Life.
Structure: The AB 32-Bar Form This 32-bar form is comprised of two different sections, and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter and B). Just as in previous weeks' units, each letter (section) represents three key musical attributes They - are (1.) particular rhythm; (2) a particular chord structure (and melody), and (3.) a specific phrase length: In this unit each section has the same phrase length: The A section is 16 bars long (with the aid of a repeat/first and second endings) , and the B section is also 16 bars long (with the aid of a repeat/ first and second endings) All together; these two sections make up the 32-bar form: For example: Ex 4.1

Unit 4- Chapter 1
The Jazz Ride Pattern as Swung I6th Notes In this week's lesson, you will be applying the jazz ride pattern to the hi-hat voice as a swung I6th-note groove: In order to fully understand this concept; let's take a lookat the jazz cymbal pattern and how it can be applied and translated to a modern soul-funk groove:
Here is the jazz ride pattern, traditionally notated as eighth-note triplets: Ex 4.2
Now; let's add snare comping ghost notes to the"&"of beat and beat 4,and a backbeat on beat 3: Ex 4.3
Next; lets phrase (and notate) this pattern as I6th-note triplets: Ex 4.4
Finally; let's"translate"this I6th-note triplet groove into swung I6th notes for this weeks new hi-hat and snare ghost note pattern: Ex 4.5
Now that you understand this concept, let's take a look at each section of the chart in detail:

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter A Ex 4.6
Sound:"Bury" the bass drum Beater; snare rim shot and a light, even hi-hat:
The intro and letter A feature a two-bar phrase alongside a very syncopated eighth and swung 16th-note based groove; which utilizes the snare drum, bass drum and closed hi-hat voices The hi-hat employs the jazz ride pattern as swung 16th notes, and the snare drum "swings"the same ghost-note pattern from Unit 2's B section: Again, these ghost notes are played extremely softly; which should be "felt rather than heard"
Letter A Ghost Note Exercises: The Hands__Hi-Hat and Snare Drum Before attempting the groove at Letter A,it is extremely important that you understand how the snare drum ghost pattern fits within the swung I6th-note hi-hat pattern and pulse: Please make sure to practice this pattern slowly and evenly while subdividing both 16th-note triplets and swung I6th notes: Once it becomes comfortable, you should gradually work up to this weeks tempo of 91 BPM: Ex 4.7
Letter A Preliminary Hi-Hat and Ghost Note Exercises: Isolate Each Bass Drum At 91 BPM, it is very easy to accidentally rush these ghost notes; play them too loudly; flam them with the bass drum, and generally make the groove feel uneven: Therefore; we have compiled a set of preliminary exercises that will help you to isolate each bass drum note against the ghost note pattern: In order to play perfect unisons between the snare and bass drum; please make sure to subdivide these exercises within a 16th-note triplet click at a slow tempo before working up to 91 BPM: Ex 4.8

Unit 4 - Chapter 2
Articulations In addition to the normal "bury the beater" bass drum and snare drum rim shot backbeat articulations; Letter will feature a light and even hi-hat sound and mezzo-piano snare drum ghost note sound. Let's review how to achieve these articulations:
Hi-Hat This pattern should be played evenly; with a medium-loud volume: In order to achieve this sound, you will use the shoulder of the stick; just beneath the tip. Next; use this part of the stick to strike the edge (not the top) of the hi-hat cymbals This will give you a very thick, yet dynamically controlled hi-hat tone: In addition, by bringing the hi-hat volume lower; it also creates a dynamic illusion, automatically making the bass drum and snare drum appear to sound louder within the overall drum mix Ex 4.9 Ex. 4.10
Mezzo-Piano Snare Drum Ghost Notes The snare drum employs a standard backbeat and a typical ghost note pattern (from Unit 2's A section) that Ex.4.11 is performed on both the "e"of beats and 3 and on the "ah"of beats 2 and 4. These ghost notes are played extremely softly, and should be "felt rather than heard" In order to achieve this ghosted sound,you will use the tip of the stick in the center of the snare drum with a light "drop" stroke This"drop" stroke is achieved by loosening the fingers (which lets the stick drop and lightly tap the head) ~rather than a full wrist-motioned down stroke: For the next backbeat; re-grip the stick with the fingers, lift your hand from the previous ghost note position, and strike your rim shot as you normally would. Please keep in mind this technique will take a bit of effort to achieve Be diligent and patient and you will be able to play these patterns successfullyl
37
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1136.PNG ---
Hand Position Many drummers play the same hi-hat and snare drum hand position for every groove. However; this cannot possibly work for every rhythmic pattern or sonic articulation. In order to effectively play a strong backbeat within this week's jazz ride hi-hat pattern, you may need to alter your normal "grooving" hand position:
Rather than crossing your hands over each other in the normal position (Ex 4.13, Fig.3), move your hi-hat hand slightly off- center while keeping your elbows in. Next, strike the hi-hatat a slight angle (Fig.4). This will allow you to stay relaxed and still allow for your left hand to lift up and play a strong backbeat (Fig.5). Important: Do not move your right elbow outward away from your body. This will cause a great amount of tension and fatigue within your groove: (Fig.6) Ex 4.12

Fig: " 4
Fig: 5
Fig: 6

UNIt five reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: World Beat and Pad Triggering This week's chart is in the style of a basic world beat groove: This groove can apply to many Middle Eastern; Asian and African styles This lesson will focus on maintaining a groove while managing your way through starting/stopping loops and triggering sounds from an electronic multi-pad
Instructors: Set DTX module to Kit 031 "Indy Melody"
Structure: ABA with an Eight-Bar Intro. You will start this ABA chart by triggering an electronic table loop like this:
Intro:` Trigger loop and start groove: Ex 5.1
In addition to the upside-down solid triangle notehead indicating the multi-pad, the specific numbered pad to strike is indicated by the number in a box below the staff This pad number relates specifically to the Yamaha Multi1 2. However; ifyou have another model of multi-pad, please feel free to insert any appropriate pad number (and resulting sound). You must be careful to strike the pad perfectly in time with the metronome (100 BPM). Also, after striking the pad and starting the table loop; you must get in position to execute the hi-hat groove with clear upbeat accents:
Next four bars: Loop continues. Syncopated rhythm on pads 7 & 8 is added to the groove: Ex 5.2
8

Unit 5 Chapter 3
Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Slow Swing Grooves and Subdividing Swing blues is a jazz-tinged style of blues that came to prominence in the mid-to-late 1940s. Usually featuring a vocalist in front of a large; horn-driven orchestra, the style is earmarked by intensely shouted vocals,honking tenor saxophone solos and a driving rhythm section. The lyrics are celebratory in nature,and the music was the bridge between the older styles of small band blues and the big band jazz sound of the 1940s.
Structure: 12-Bar Blues Form This 12-bar blues form is comprised of three different sections and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter: A, A2, A3. The head (and main riffs) appears within A and A2, and A3 is a 12-bar chorus of guitar solo. For example: Ex 6.1

Unit 6 - Chapter 2
Splashing the Hi-Hat Splashing the hi-hat with the left foot helps create a metallic pad of sound (or sheen) underneath fills and grooves This is notated by a small "open" circle below the notehead; like this: Ex 6.10
To splash the hi-hat; simply give the hi-hat pedal a terse kick with the heel. This will allow the cymbals to clang together in a gentle but stern fashion. After the initial kick, you then gently place your foot back on the pedal leaving just enough space between the cymbals to maintain the sound:
2. Groove: Bars 5-8 Bars 5-8 are comprised ofthe following groove: Ex 6.11
3. Ensemble Figures within the Groove: Bars 9-12 Bars 9-12 have ensemble figures that are played within the notated swing groove. For example: Ex 6.12
In the graphic above (Ex. 6.12),A shows what the drums would actually play; while B shows what you would typically see in a chart (the chartjust shows the ensemble figures) Notice that in the last bar; the short note on the"&"of 2 is voiced on the hi-hat:
Practice Note: Subdividing at Slow Tempos At an extremely slow tempo; many (ifnot all) professional drummers subdivide a smaller subdivision/note value than the main rhythms notated in the chart This allows them to stay in time and not rush the quarter note pulse; while maintaining a consistent sound (and, of course, accurately reading the chart) Therefore, it is extremely important that you practice this section (and entire chart) while subdividing eighth-note triplets Please spend equal amounts of time playing the chart with your click track on eighth-note triplets and on quarter notes while counting eighth-note triplets to yourself Together; these practice techniques will help you to master this tempo, swing feel and chart.

Usually featuring a small, jazz-driven combo, this style is earmarked by uptempo, yet relaxed, winding melodies; sophisticated solos and a driving rhythm section This units chart will focus on combining (and tastefully transitioning between) a triplet-based jazz feel and a straight eighth-note bossa nova feel.
Structure: AABA Form This 32-bar AABA form is comprised of three distinct choruses There are two choruses of the head, which feature a triplet jazz brush feel and a chorus of solo placed over a straight-eighth bossa nova feel The chorus of solo is also shared between the guitar and drums, with each alternating eight-bar solos Lets take a look at the entire form: Ex 7.a

Structure Additions: DC al Coda: Return to the Head This form is also augmented with a D.C al Coda. After completing the last eight-bar drum solo, you must return to the top of the chart and play through the head one more time (including both endings). Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound The Head As mentioned in earlier units, the "head" (in a jazz setting) refers to the melody of the tune The drums play standard jazz brush pattern* (Ex. 7.9, page XX) while comping the long/tied notes played by the guitar (which is playing the melody):
Letter A Within the A sections (eight bars each), there are a few hits on the"&" of 4, plus a dotted quarter-note figure in bars 5 and 6. For example: Ex 7.3 Swing/Brushes 5 FF43F
ZEE
Recurring Dotted Quarter Figure The figure within bars 5-6are a very typical within jazz phrasing: This pattern is based on the dotted quarter notes (one and a half beats) and goes "over the bar line" superimposing a grouping of three over four: Try playing this figure as written, with the left hand staying on the snare and the right hand staying on the ride cymbal: For example: Ex 7.4

Unit 7 Chapter 2
Transition from Jazz to Bossa As we have previously studied, the jazz feel is based on the eighth-note triplet: In the last two bars of the head, there is a two-bar hold (notated with an extended whole note on bar 7) which gives you time to (1) switch from brushes to sticks and (2) set up the straight-eighth bossa feel for the solo sections: Ex 7.6
Set up Bossa feel
Let's now examine how to manage all of this within two bars: Ex 7.7
Set up Bossa feel
Set brushes down and pick up sticks. Play a very clear and precise straight eighth- Stay relaxed and do not rush this activity: note fill setting up the bossa.
Musical Note: Setting up a New Feel and Subdivision Many (if not all) professional drummers properly set up the transition between swung and straight feels They accomplish this by playing a fill one bar before the new section, using the upcoming section's feel and subdivision This allows the drummer to properly"cue"the band that a new feel is about to occur: Ifyou fail to do this, the new section will appear"out of nowhere" and not sound musical: Here are a few setup ideas for the last bar of the head: Ex 7.8
Set up Bossa feel

Brushes: The Conventional Stroke The conventional stroke has the same rhythmic structure as the conventional jazz ride cymbal pattern, and the same timekeeping feel. It can be used for medium and uptempo jazz timekeeping:
The Right Hand You should begin at the tail of the arrow, using the broken triplet as your pickup. Then move to the point (beat 1). Lift and return to the tail (beat 2). Repeat this pattern for beats 3 and 4.
The Left Hand In a half-note rhythm and a clockwise direction; circle continuously by starting at the tail and moving towards the point (the first beat). Complete this circle at the tail by gently sweeping at the second beat: Ex 7.9
START
StaAT
Right Hand
Slide Half-Circles on Snare
Left Hand
Note: As the left hand is executing the circular pattern, be careful not to "dig in" or press too hard on the drumhead: You can also give the brush a slight accent to help convey the time feel on beats 2 and 4. Also, the hi-hat closes on 2 and 4, matching the hands, which come together on the same counts
The Solos The AABA chorus of solo is broken up into smaller increments as follows: The bossa nova solo section is one chorus long and the solos are subdivided as follows: First A section: Bass and drums play a bossa to accompany an eight-bar guitar solo. Second A section: Guitar and bass stop for eight-bar drum solo: B section: Bass and drums play a bossa to accompany an eight-bar guitar solo. Last A section: Guitar and bass stop for eight-bar drum solo. Drums set up jazz feel in final bars of solo:

Unit eight reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Top 40 Pop with Samba Vamp "Top forty" is music industry shorthand for the most popular; best-selling and most frequently broadcast pop songs within a given week or month Over the years, it has also come to represent a popular song from particular era as well Thus, this play-along; called "Timber Grooves" is a dance-pop chart in the tradition Justin Timberlake and Michael Jackson. It features two popular grooves that you will definitely play on a contemporary"cover" gig: Structure: The AABA 32-Bar Form This study employs a classic musical structure common to rock; pop and jazz music: the AABA 32-bar form, which you have already seen in Drum Performance and 2. To review; this form is comprised of two different sections, and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A or B). In addition, both letters (sections) represent three key musical attributes: They are: a different rhythm (or groove) , an eight-bar phrase and a particular riff and chord structure
So how does this information apply to this chart here in Drum Performance 32 Well; let's examine each key element individually: 1. A different rhythm (or groove). As stated earlier; there are two letters within this form, and each one now represents a particular rhythm (or groove). As a result; there are two grooves present within this chart: For example:
The A section features a straight-eighth hi-hat groove alongside a syncopated 16th-note bass drum pattern: Not only is this groove a two-bar phrase; but it also features a tightly closed hi-hat (in both bars) , with an opening on the"&"of beat 3_ Ex 8.1
Light; Tight Closed Hi-Hat
The B section groove is a one-bar phrase, which features a quarter-note hi-hat and bass drum pattern The hi-hat has an opening on the "&" of beat 4. Ex 8.2
B Louder Tight Closed Hi-Hat

Unit 8 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound It is not enough to merely play the correct patterns; you must play the correct patterns with the correct sound; feel, and form: With this in mind, we will break down each section, demonstrating the appropriate hi-hat; snare; and bass drum articulations: Ex 8.6 Letter A
Light; Tight Closed Hi-Hat
Just as the A section has a sonic treatment; phrase length, and chord progression,it will also have a particular drum sound. Therefore, this section features a light; two-bar eighth-note hi-hat groove alongside very staccato/syncopated bass drum and strong snare backbeat Let's take a look at howyou can achieve these articulations:
Bass Drum The bass drum pattern should be played very forcefully;with a staccato articulation and a dynamic of mezzo- forte (medium-loud) This will be accomplished by utilizing three important strategies They are as follows: 1 Using the felt side of (or a soft-surfaced) bass drum beater: Many pop drummers make this choice because it gives each pedal strokes impact a round sound This is the perfect option for adding a full and low tone to a song; while still providing the rhythmic foundation: 2_ Burying the bass drum beater into the bass drum head: After each stroke it is also very important to keep the beater pushed (or buried) into the drum head: Not only does this muffle the drum slightly, but it adds a slight element of attack to the initial stroke (and compliments the soft-surfaced bass-drum beater). However; you must make sure to firmly press the beater into the head to avoid any unwanted "felt flutter" between the beater and drum head. 3. Use a combination of your leg and ankle: Now that you have correct attack articulations, it is also important to get a loud and powerful tone from the drum: Most drummers use their ankle for each stroke; which results in a fast and accurate tone. However; this technique is not appropriate for loud rock and metal styles because it lacks projection; force, and power: In order to remedy this, it is suggested that you use your entire leg (and foot) to make each stroke:
73
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1172.PNG ---
Snare Drum The snare drum should be played very consistently and at a dynamic of forte (loud). Additionally; each snare hit should be a rim shot: This stroke is executed by hitting the center of the drumhead and rim at the same exact time. A rim shot is loudest (and most effective) when the stroke is started by the wrist and finished off by the fingers Let's take a look at both steps:
1 _
In order to keep the dynamic in the mezzo-forte realm, strike the drum just off center This will place the stick tip in the lower half of the beater dot as in Ex. 8.7 below: Ex. 8.7 2 Next; while aiming for this spot on the snare head, start the stroke with your wrist: 3 Now, just before hitting the rim and lower half of the center dot; grab the stick with your back three fingers: This is lends nice   crisp attack to an already authoritative stroke: It is very similar to a high-five hand slapl
Hi-Hat: Chunky;"Shouldered" Eighths Ex 8.8 This eighth-note pattern should be played evenly and aggressively, at a dynamic of mezzo-forte. In order to achieve this sound, you will use the shoulder ofthe stick mid-way down from the tip. In addition, the hi-hat should be closed with your foot, but not too tightlyl Rather than an articulation of "chick-chick-chick-chick" think ofyour hi-hat saying "chunk-chunk-chunk-chunk"
Next; use this part of the stick to strike the edge (not the top) of the hi-hat cymbals (Ex 8.8). This will give you a very thick and chunky hi-hat tone These performance methods create a rhythmic (and tempo-based) illusion; which make the groove feel faster than 108 BPM, with more forward motion and syncopation:
Finally, the hi-hat opening on the"&"of beat 3 should be played as an accented note, immediately followed by a closed hi-hat foot note Ex 8.9
Light, Tight Closed Hi-Hat

Unit 8 - Chapter 2
All Together The end result will be a strong, round, and present bass drum sound, alongside an open rim shot and thick; "chunky" hat This approach perfectly compliments the spacious and rhythmic synth textures of letter A Ex 8.10 Letter B
B Louder Tight Closed Hi-Hat
In addition to letter As bass drum and hi-hat sounds; this section features a quarter-note hi-hat and bass drum groove, enhanced by a slightly wider and louder snare drum sound. Let's take a look at how you can add this snare-drum articulation to your drumming arsenal: Ex 8.11 Snare Drum: Rim Shot "Just Past the Center" of the Drumhead The snare drum should be played very consistently and at a dynamic of forte (loud). Again, each snare hit will also be a rim shot: However; in this section you will aim for"just past the center" of the drumhead (Ex. 8.11). This will make the snare drum sound a bit drier; with fewer overtones, and it will project more tone. This snare drum sound (and quarter- noted based groove) builds the B section's intensity and differentiates it from the A section:
Important: Be careful not to speed up or slow down when making the switch between the eighth and quarter-note grooves (the A and B sections) This can happen because there is more space (and therefore margin for error) within a quarter-note hi-hat pattern:
Talk-Down First and Second Endings: As you already know,letter A occurs twice; for a total of 16 bars However; this form is notated as an eight-bar phrase, which utilizesa first and second ending: Lets take a lookathow to properly navigate this repeat structure:
The first time through letter A you will take the repeat under the first ending and travel back to the beginning of the section (as indicated by the dotted arrow) Upon repeating the previous part and playing to bar 7 (or 15),you will skip the first ending and play the second ending (as indicated by the solid arrow). At this point you would be at letter B. Ex 8.12
Light; Tight Closed Hi-Hat

Samba Pattern A samba isa dance and type of music from Brazil originating from Africa. Samba drum set grooves are two- bar patterns with the bass drum and hi-hat (or ride cymbal) playing a consistent one-bar pattern and the side-stick playing a two-bar pattern: A samba pattern has many variations and may be notated a number of ways. A few basic samba patterns are as follows: 1. Samba with eighths on the ride cymbal:
2. Samba with added sixteenths on the ride cymbal:
3. Easy samba between hi-hat and bass drum only:
4. Samba between the snare drum and bass drum:
few hi-hat (with foot) options:
2
Any " of these samba patterns will work for this chart: Please pick one that you are most comfortable with:
The samba pattern occurs at the Coda. In this chart the time signature switches to 2/4 for authenticity. The Coda is an open section for a guitar solo with the final bar on cue (cued by the guitarist).

Structure Additions: D.S al Coda (Take Second Ending): Return to the Head and Play Through to the Coda This form is augmented with a modified D.S.al Coda which tells you to use only the second ending (omitting the first ending/repeat) on the D.S. After completing the eight-bar bridge and four-bar second line refrain, you must return to the top of the 12-bar head. Following the "take second ending" instruction, you would then skip the first ending repeat; take the second ending and revisit the bridge; thereafter heading to the coda. For example: Ex 9.2

Letter A Letter A (the 12-bar head) features the same swung 16th-note New Orleans groove from the introduction: The accented notes should be played as light rim shots and the roll on the "&"of 4 should be crushed (not open): For example: Ex 9.5
Fills within the First and Second Endings The fill within the first ending of Letter A is a four-beat fill: Since this fill is leading us back to letter A, this fill should be "groove oriented" and not too complex The second ending has an ensemble figure on the "&"of beat 2, with a specified fill (play as written) leading into and out of the ensemble figure: For example: Ex 9.6
1 Fill
