--- SOURCE: IMG_0894.PNG ---
Unit twO reading | techNIque pertOrMance Chapter 1: Phrase Interpretation (Phrasing) The general rules of setting up ensemble figures are not set in stone; they are merely functional ideas to help you conceptualize set-up fill application: A common mistake that young readers make is to set up every ensemble figure they encounter: While technically being able to set up every figure with flashy fills might merit some praise, you must be able to detect places where little to no set-up is needed. Keep in mind that musicality (and common sense) must supersede the desire to cram all available space with set-up fills Thus, phrasing relates to the manner in which the individual notes of a particular group of consecutive notes are actually played:.
The phrase below (Ex 2.2) is comprised of consecutive eighth notes that occur on upbeats: In a musical setting, each note does not merit its own set-up. For example: Ex 2.2
However; if we were to follow the general rules from Unit 1,each note would be set up like this: Ex 2.3
In the example above (Ex 2.3), by following the general rules; there is a snare drum on every downbeat between the upbeat eighth notes (voiced on an open-to-closed hi-hat) While this is getting into a matter of subjectivity, there is basically too much filling clouding the phrasing of the figure This type of figure is generally written to create a staggered effect and needs the downbeats to be actual rests (as written): A seasoned player will realize that the first three eighth notes are merely leading into the larger figure on the "&"of beat 3:an eighth note tied to a quarter note. Here is a more appropriate way to voice this passage: Ex 2.4
19
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0895.PNG ---
However; this does not mean you couldn't add a few fills to help shape this figure: For example: Ex 2.5
In the example above (Ex. 2,5), the drums are basically grooving through the first two eighth notes, using the snare drum backbeat to set up the"&"of beat 2 and then setting up the"&"of beat 3 with a double stop between the snare drum and floor tom.
Sometimes, to create a dramatic effect; no set-up fill is warranted: A composer may want the band to kick in together with no hint of something coming: For example: Ex 2.6
In the example above (Ex 2.6) there are countless fill ideas that come to mind; but leaving the figure alone may be the best idea. Of course your decision may be made for you if there are fill indicators or directives written on the chart: For example: Ex 2.7 Fill CCCCC__-
No filll
Drum charts really do run the gamut; and in the end it is usually up to the drummer to decide what needs to be played: Here is a list of some possible types of charts you could be presented with in your career:
Fully notated: Contains detailed drum notation with specific drum fills, orchestration performance directives and dynamics Skeletal: Provides the form and basic groove; possibly with some ensemble figures: Lead sheet: A chart that provides the form, chords, melody and basic feel (example: 3/4 jazz waltz) but no specific drum parts Piano, bass or trumpet chart: Many times the drum chart goes missing, and you'll be handed piano or bass chart (lead trumpet if in a big band situation) that at least provides the form and essential rhythms: Shorthand: Many times you may have to chart tune out yourself: This method has endless possibilities

Unit three reading | techNIQUe perfOrmance Chapter 1: Articulations While standard notation can sufficiently provide where and when a note occurs (and its duration), oftentimes more information is needed to help the performer play what the composer (or arranger) intended With this in mind, articulations are used to provide additional performance information (i.e: when placement and length are not enough): Depending on its position on the staff;,an articulation can affect a note in many different ways. Let's take a look at the various drum-specific articulations used within chart reading:
Staccato (Short) Staccato means detached in Italian: In musical terms, a staccato marking instructs the reader to play the note a short as possible; without further sustain. This articulation is written above the note with a dot as follows: Ex 3.1
In the example above (Ex. 3.1), there are four-quarter notes to be played,as very short notes without further sustain: As previously discussed in Drum Reading 1 and 2, clearer passages aid a performer Thus, articulations are used ~ i.e. rather than our standard four short notes on every downbeat: Ex 3.2
While the example above (Ex 3.2) is technically correct; the performer could get lost within the multitude of rests
32 Drum Reading 3
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0908.PNG ---
Unit 3 - Chapter 1
Tenuto (Long or Legato) Tenuto means to hold in Italian. In musical terms,a tenuto marking instructs the reader to play a note as long as possible This articulation is also often referred to as legato, although legato usually affects an entire phrase. Placing a line above the note head or stem marks a tenuto articulation For example: Ex 3.3
Sometimes these articulations are redundant to the rhythmic notation and other times these articulations may be difficult to actually play: In the example above (Ex. 3.3) you may play the phrase the same way _ i.e. regardless of the articulation; but having the articulation present reinforces that each note must be played to its fullest duration (in this case one beat).
Martellato (Roof Top Accent) Martellato means hammered in Italian: In musical terms, a Martellato marking instructs the reader to accent a note in a wider fashion (i.e: than a traditional accent) This articulation is also often referred to as "roof top" accent: If a regularly accented note sounds like "dat"then a rooftop accented note sounds like "daht" Ex 3.4
To summarize: Staccato = short, Tenuto = long and Martellato = fat:
33
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0909.PNG ---

--- SOURCE: IMG_0918.PNG ---
Unit {OUN reading | techNIQue performance Chapter 1: Basic Concepts In this unit; swung eighth-note ensemble figures (paired with larger subdivisions: quarter notes; half notes and whole notes) will be presented in a jazz (triplet-based) setting: Ifyou are doing a trio or quartet-style gig you will most likely be given a"lead sheet" chart which provides the basic form and feel but little more: If you are doing a big band gig then you are more likely to see a specifically notated drum chart with ensemble figures, articulations and notated fills. Ex 4.1 Eighth-Note Triplet Placement Because a triplet contains three eighth notes wedged into the space normally One occupied by two eighth notes, the distance between these notes changes: Straight eighth notes divide one beat symmetrically in half putting the Ex 4.2 upbeat equidistant from the downbeat:
When there are eighth-note triplets, the third partial of the triplet is now your upbeat. This note will be further from the preceding downbeat but closer to the following downbeat:
One
Trip
let
Two
Trip
let
In eighth-note triplet-based music; when seeing a freestanding eighth note on an upbeat; be sure to place the eighth note on the third partial= of the triplet: For example: Ex 4.3 S= 3 S= 3
One
Two
Three
Four

The "Eighth-Note Rule" In jazz (and other eighth-note triplet ~based contemporary music), notation cannot always convey the actual phrasing or "feel" of a figure Therefore, the"eighth note rule"is a simple guideline that can assist you with ensemble figure interpretation: Basically,if you see a freestanding eighth note with nothing following it,then it will be accented. For example: Ex 4.4
When you see a string of eighth notes, the last one ofthe group is accented, with the others functioning a5 lead-in notes. For example: Ex 4.5
Lead in tones
Accented last note of phrase
If we take a guitar line and convert it into ensemble figures, the accents will naturally fall into place: For example: Ex 4.6 Swing
1
In the example above (Ex. 4.6), the end ofeach short phrase is accented along with the freestanding eighth notes You should catch the accents, but can avoid playing every written figure: Here is an example of how this would be voiced on the drum set within a jazz setting: Ex 4.7

Unit Six reading | techNIque | per{OrMaNce Chapter 1: Timekeeping Figures Timekeeping figures display what the other instrumentalists in your band are playing These figures are very useful in helping you to play a "musical" and complimentary time feel Timekeeping figures are written above the staff,with time slashes below them: Unlike ensemble figures; timekeeping figures must not interrupt the general drum pattern: For example: Ex 6.1
In a jazz setting; the phrase above could be voiced on the kit like this: Ex 6.2
Timekeeping figures are most commonly used in jazz-style charts, but can also be used in rock/pop settings For example: Ex. 6.3
In the example above (Ex. 6.3) the bass drum is "catching"the timekeeping figures, while the snare drum stays on beats 2 and 4. Here is another example (Ex. 6.4) that employs more syncopation between the bass drum and snare drum: Ex 6.4

Unit 6 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Shorthand Indicators Oftentimes, shorthand symbols can be used in addition to (or in lieu of) traditional notation. Using shorthand indicators may help to keep a chart concise; especially for "open" solo sections For example: Ex 6.7
77E
7
B Guitar Solo
Shorthand indicator
Open
Pay8 ~tttt
On Cue:
474
13
7E
17
In this example (Ex. 6.7), at letter B, there is a shorthand indicator that instructs to reader to play eight bars ("Play 8 ). Since this is a solo section; the drummer is given the freedom to play an appropriate part and interact accordingly with the soloist: No bar lines, time slashes or rhythms are used:
This also allows the drummer to relax from all the terminology and notation traditionally used in a chart: A common mistake young drummers make (when developing their reading skills) is to get fixated on the chart and stop listening to what is going on around them: A skilled reader can glance at a chart; retain the information and continue to interact with the band.

Unit SeveN reading | techNIQUe perfOrmance Chapter 1: Basic Concepts Ensemble figures based on 16th-note subdivisions are used in many styles of music; namely funk; R&B and rock When a tune is based on a template of 16th-note subdivisions, syncopated and complex rhythms are usually prevalent (namely on the "e" and the "ah" of all beats) When reading 16th note-based charts, you must be comfortable with beat placement on all partials of 16th notes:
Beat Placement: The"e" and"ah" Before you begin to develop a 16th note-based ensemble figure set-up fill repertoire, you must first solidify your ability to properly place every partial within 16th-note subdivision: Once you subdivide (and internalize) the 16th-note subdivision, you should practice 16th-note beat placement within a groove: In Unit 1, downbeats and upbeats were addressed, thus, the focus in this unit will be the "es" and "ah's" of every beat:
To create a realistic setting, you will play a four-bar phrase: three bars of time with a single 6th-note ensemble figure in bar 4. Each figure should be played in three different manners: 1) with one-handed 16th notes on the hi-hat; 2) with two-handed 16th notes on the hi-hat and 3) with eighths notes on the hi-hat
One-Handed 16ths: The"e" of Beat Ex 7.1
With one-handed sixteenth notes on the hi-hat at 75 bpm
Template
Ensemble Figure
Drum Set
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0945.PNG ---
Playing on the"e" (and/or the"ah") of a beat can make you feel off balance due to the syncopated nature of these beats Therefore; it is crucial that you develop solid feel for these beat placements. Once the ensemble figure is played (and before set-up fills are utilized), you must make sure that you recover from the 16th-note figure: In the example above (Ex 7.1), the area you must focus on is the ability to land precisely on beat 2 after the"e" of beat one. For example: Ex 7.2 Count the"e-&-ah" and wait for beat

One-Handed 16ths: The"ah" of Beat 1 An ensemble figure on the"ah"of a beat creates the illusion ofa rushed downbeat: Figures of this nature are normally paired with an anticipated chord change (that also feels as if it has arrived early). This motion is known asa"push"meaning the downbeat feels pushed ahead by one I6th note. Again, the key ~ to successful beat placement is to subdivide Ex 7.15 With one-handed sixteenth notes on the hi-hat at 75 bpm

Unit eight reading | techNIQUe perfOrmance Chapter 1: Basic Concepts Sixteenth note-based ensemble figures (short and/or long notes beginning on the"e" or"ah"ofa beat) need clear set-up fills in order for the rest ofan ensemble to execute the figures with precision In this unit we will look at the general target beats for setting up ensemble figures on the "e" and "ah" of all beats
The "e" of a Beat When setting up ensemble figures on the"e"of a beat; a clearly played downbeat must be present just prior to the ensemble figure. When reading music; everyone in the ensemble is reading ahead to prepare for the next sections, phrases and rhythms When a figure on the "e" of a beat arises, the natural tendency for the performer is to slightly rush this figure With a clear downbeat played just before the figure (on the"e"), the proper amount of space gets occupied, making all performers wait just long enough before executing the figure: In addition, a 16th-note-based fill will also help all performers subdivide 16th notes, again assisting in proper beat placement:
Setting Up the"e" of Beat 1 In this example there is a dotted eighth note on the "e" of beat 1. The target beat for this particular figure is the downbeat of beat 1. The following are a few set-up ideas: Ex 8.1
Template
Ensemble Figure
Drum Set
Set-up fill on the"&" of beat 4
Target beat: downbeat of beat 1
Once you are comfortable with the basic set-up, the fill can then be expanded by starting earlier in the measure: For example: Ex 8.2
Ensemble Figure
Drum Set
Two beat set-up fill starting on beat 3
Target beat: downbeat of beat 1

Setting Up the"e" of Beat 2 When setting up the"e" of beat 2, the target beat is the downbeat of beat 2. The snare drum backbeat on beat 2 takes care of the target beat; therefore; a fill on beat is needed to help project the upcoming ensemble figure on the"e" of beat 2,as follows: Ex 8.3

--- SOURCE: IMG_0963.PNG ---
The "ah" of a Beat When setting up ensemble figures on the "ah" of a beat; the target beat (or target area, in this case) is essentially the three 16th notes prior to the "ah" of the beat (the downbeat; the "e" and the "&"). Again, a figure on the "ah" of a beat creates a natural tendency for the performer to slightly rush this figure: As mentioned in Unit 7,a figure on the "ah" of the beat is regularly referred to as a "push" due to the fact that this action creates a feeling as if the next downbeat is occurring early:
Setting Up the"ah" of Beat 1 When setting up an ensemble figure on the"ah"of beat 1,there should be three 16th notes played, starting on the preceding downbeat; to act as the set-up fill, as follows: Ex 8.9
Template
Ensemble Figure
Drum Set
Set-up fill: 1-e-&
The fill may also start earlier; on beat 4. For example: Ex 8.10
Ensemble Figure
Drum Set
Two beat set-up fill starting on beat 4
Setting Up the"ah" of Beat 2 When setting up an ensemble figure on the"ah"of beat 2,there should be three I6th notes played, starting on the preceding downbeat; to act as the set-up fill, as follows: Ex 8.11

Unit 8 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Sticking Now that you have been presented with the general set-up rules regarding ensemble figures landing on the "e" and "ah"you must work on the execution of your set-up fills. In Unit 7 you learned how to incorporate these figures within your groove pattern When setting up I6th note figures, it is easy to get tied up making sticking choices. Now you will be presented with a series of exercises designed to show you common sticking patterns associated with isolated 16th note-based ensemble figures:
There are essentially two options when executing I6th note-based ensemble figures: 1) play everything hand-to-hand, or 2) incorporate double strokes when needed: You want to be comfortable with both options,in order to give yourselfthe most flexibility As a result; you will be able to design your fill with your destination in mind (thinking; for instance; what sound source would be best for a particular figure? Do you need to end your fiIl on the left hand in order to strike the china cymbal on your left side? Do you need to free up your right hand to get it in place to resume the groove following the figure? The "e" of Beat 1 Ex 8.17 Note: all written sticking patterns can be reversed

Letter € Letter € is a ten-bar phrase with many ensemble figures and articulation markings. After playing two bars of time; you must set up the first group of ensemble figures Each of the figures in this first phrase has a length of one and a half beats, spanning across bars 3 and 4. There is room in between each figure for two eighth notes Be sure to experiment with different combinations of eighth notes between the snare drum, toms and bass drum Ex 10.9 Each fiqure is worth one and a half beats.
In bars 5 and 6 there are more ensemble figures: Again, each figure has a duration lasting one and a half beats. In bar 7 there are four quarter notes marked staccato. These should be played as short as possible leading into bar 8. At bar 8 there is a break on beat 1, exaggerated with a strong quarter note (marked as martellato) on beat 1. On beat 4 of bar 8 there is an ensemble figure tied to a two-bar hold that you can fill through: Be sure to notice the decrescendo that occurs over bars 9 and 10. --- SOURCE: IMG_0987.PNG ---
About the Author
Stewart Jean was born and raised in Deptford, New Jersey, where he was introduced to music through his father's love of classical music and his friends' Led Zeppelin LPs. Stewart's passion for music grew as he studied drumset; timpani and mallets in high school. Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician:
lauug
At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree, Stewart became a sought-after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings, tours and shows.
After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles, maintaining steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the-point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists' lists.
Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years, Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves; Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards.
112 Drum Reading 3
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0992.PNG ---
UNit ONE reading | techNIQUe pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: 32nd Note Notation In Reading and Reading 2 you learned about basic notation and the division of note (and rest) values. To quickly review: Ex 1.1 One whole note equals two half notes: 0 = JJ 4=2+2
One half note equals two quarter notes:
J-J1 2=1+1
One quarter note equals two eighth notes:
J-$ $ 1 =1/2 + 1/2
One eighth note equals two sixteenth notes: JJj 1/2 =1/4+ 1/4
Dividing one sixteenth note in half produces two 32nd notes There are thirty-two 32nd notes in one measure of 4/4 or, eight 32nd notes to each beat: 32nd note require a triple beam or three flags in regards to notation. For example: Ex 1,.2 Triple flag One freestanding 32nd note:
Bundled as two groups of four Triple beam per each beat
One full bar of 32nd notes:
To count 32nd notes it is recommended to simply subdivide sixteenth notes knowing that each partial (1 - e-&-ah) is doubled. Remember that two 32nd notes equal one sixteenth note as follows: Ex 1,.3
VVMVV
Also, keep in mind that for every half beat (every eighth note in 4/4) there is space for four 32nd notes; as follows: Ex 1.4
&
2
&
3

32nd Notes in Use It is very common to see 32nd notes in slower pieces of music amongst larger note values When developing your ability to read 32nd notes it is best to visualize the I6th-note partials in order to assist with the proper placement of each 32nd note (or rest). Also, try to keep your focus on the larger subdivision of eighth notes, which house four 32nd notes for each downbeat and upbeat: The following exercises will assist you in training your eye to see where 32nd notes land among larger rhythmic values For example: Ex 1.10 Put your metronome on 45 bpm or 90 bpm

Unit twO reading | techNIQue pertOrMance Chapter 1: Phrase Interpretation (Phrasing) 32nd notes and alternate choices on how 32nd notes are notated. In addition, 32nd-note triplets will also be explained:
As mentioned throughout Reading 3,a drummer must develop an advanced ability to interpret figures and orchestrate them on the drum set. As you learned, drummers spend much of their time filling in and setting up figures in order to transform a stagnant figure into actual music Applying 32nd notes to ensemble figure interpretation will greatly open up your awareness of available space between figures In addition, adding 32nd notes to your reading skills will enhance your ability to playing creatively:
Rhythmic Abbreviations: 32nd Notes Another way to notate 32nd notes is to add a slash to individual stems under beamed I6th notes: These abbreviations help clean up triple beams (especially if you are writing by hand) and are commonly used to notate 32nd notes For example:
A string of 32nd notes notated with triple beams can also be written as I6th notes with a slash on each stem as follows: Ex 2.1
J
Ifyou indeed see 32nds notated with three slashes; you may have to decide if the composer intends for a roll (open or closed) or 32nd notes.
The above rhythms (Ex. 2.1,2.2) sound the same although the notation is different: As mentioned in Reading 3,arrangers have notational choices and preferences Therefore; it is your job,as the performer; to be familiar with these variances The following list can be used as a translation key for 32nd note notation: Ex 2.3 Fully notated Abbreviated notation

Chapter 3: Double Dotted Notes To continue our study of advanced rhythmic notation, we will now take a look at notes that are double dotted: Although double dotted notes are not very common, it is important that you are familiar with this concept A second dot adds half of the value of the first dot to the note: For example; in the example below (Ex 3.11),a half note is worth two beats Adding a single dot will add half of the value of the half note (one beat: half of two is one) making a dotted half note worth three beats. Adding second dot will add half of the value of the first dot to the equation The first dot was worth one beat making the second dot worth half beat: In total a double dotted half note is worth three and a half beats as follows: Ex 3.11 Dotted half note Value: three beats
Half note = two beats
Double dotted half note Value: three and a half beats
Half note = two beats
2
1/2
3 1/2
First dot = half of two beats or, beat (quarter note)

Unit OUN reading | technIque | pertOrMance Chapter 1: Cut Time As you learned in Reading 1 , Unit 1,the time signature of 4/4 can also be represented by a large "C"in place ofthe 4/4 symbol,referring to "common time" This is simply due to the fact that a large percentage of music is in 4/4 time:
Cut Time Cut time refers to the 4/4 time signature being "cut"in half, which is represented by a time signature of 2/2 or the symbol: € Cut time (or 2/2) would mean that there are two beats to a measure and the halfnote gets the beat: In the example below (Ex. 9.1) you can see the altered counting as follows: Ex 4.1 T
Of course the above passage could be written in 4/4. It would look and sound the same but the counting would be different: For example: Ex 4.2
3
47
The need for cut time comes into play when the tempo is very fast (say, 175 bpm and higher) When needing to count"1-2-3-4"at fast tempos it is not uncommon for a musician to tense up and feel nervous. Cut time allows for counting to pass by at a slower rate (half) allowing the reader to relax: Instead of counting"1-2- 3-4"at 1 8Obpm,one can count"1-2"at 9Obpm There are many jazz and Latin tunes that are at ZOObpm and higher; which is why you will commonly see charts in this ilk written in cut time. Let's look at the examples from above (Ex. 9.1 and 9.2) with tempo markings: Ex 4.3
=100
Counting two beats per measure in cut time LdJdJEJ J] 4| &  2 a   2
200
Counting four beats per measure in 4/4 d S F3 1  & 2   &   3   & 4   & 1  & &   3
38 Drum Reading 4
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1028.PNG ---
Unit 4 - Chapter 1
Another use for cut time is to minimize the use of smaller notation such as sixteenth notes Again; performers may tense up when seeing a flurry of sixteenth notes Ifthey see a series of eighth notes and quarter notes they may relax when reading (especially if sight reading a chart for the first time).
In the example below (Ex. 9.4) you see two rhythmic examples that will sound the same when played The difference is in the counting and the notation: Each example hasa single line staff below the written rhythm to display the downbeats and what note vales receives each beat: The example in cut time only contains eighth notes and quarter notes whereas the example in 2/4 may be perceived as more complicated de to the many sixteenth notes Ex 4.4 Example in cut time. Two half notes per bar: = 85
1 &E DE|43ED UJ
Example in 2/4 time: Two quarter notes per bar:
=85
73
In summary, cut time is simply a way to make faster tempos manageable for the reader A seasoned reader will be successful regardless of the format (cut time, 2/2,4/4,2/4) an arranger chooses to notate a chart:
39

Unit five reading | techNIQUe pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Odd and Asymmetrical Time Signatures  Through Reading 1,2 and 3 you have learned how to read basic rhythms in what are considered "regular" or "common" time signatures: 4/4, 2/4,3/4 and 6/8. These are standard time signatures for most popular music today (and previous decades) The term "odd meter" (while may technically refer to the top number ofa time signature) implies that the time signature is "uncommon" As you know, the 3/4 time signature has three beats to a measure (an odd amount) although it is not necessarily considered "odd" due to the fact that it is a widely used (and easily felt) time signature: Also, "common" time signatures are symmetrical; each measure can be evenly divided in half: For example:
A measure of 4/4 is divided in half by two half notes: Ex 5.1
2 beats each
beats total
A measure 3/4 (and/or 6/8) is divided in half by two dotted quarter notes: Ex 5.2 1/2 beats each
3 beats total
A measure of 2/4 is divided in half by two quarter notes. Ex 5.3
beat each
2 beats total
47
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1037.PNG ---
"Odd" time signatures are asymmetrical, meaning they cannot be evenly divided: In the above examples (Ex 5.1-5.3), each measure is evenly divided to produce two dominant beats (or rhythms) within one measure: Asymmetrical time signatures can have two (or three) dominant beats within one measure, yet these beats will not be equal in value:
For example; in the symmetrical time signature of 6/8, there are six eighth notes with the dominant beats falling on beats and 4 (with every dotted quarter note) as follows: Ex 5.4 3 beats each
beats total
Dominant beats
In the asymmetrical time signature of 5/8 there are still two dominant beats, although they are not of equal value: In 5/8 there are five eighth notes per measure (with each eighth note getting one beat) with the dominant beats usually on beats and 4or 1 and 3, depending on the rhythmic template For example: Ex 5.5
Dominant beats 3beats
2 beats
5 beats total
Dominant beats 2 beats
3 beats
5 beats total

Unit 5 - Chapter 1
This concept remains true regardless of the bottom number in a time signature Observe the above example (Ex 5.5) converted to the 5/4 time signature: In 5/4 there are five quarter notes to every measure, with each receiving one beat: The dominant beats will normally fall on beats and 4as follows: Ex 5.6 Dominant beats 3 beats 2 beats
5 beats total
Again; converting these examples (Ex. 5.5 and 5.6) to 5/16 creates an asymmetrical time signature: In 5/16 there are five beats to a measure with each I6th note receiving beat as follows: Ex 5.7 Dominant beats 3 beats 2 beats

--- SOURCE: IMG_1039.PNG ---
Chapter 2: Reading in 5/8 Now that you understand the concepts and construction of odd (or asymmetrical) time signatures, the next step is to apply counting and reading in 5/8.
When reading in 5/8, remember that the eighth note gets the beat; therefore; every eighth note is a downbeat as follows: Ex 5.8 )
5
With eighth notes receiving every downbeat; 16th notes are now counted as 1-&-2-&-3-&-4-&-5-& as follows: Ex 5.9 ) 1 & 2 & 3 & 4   & 5 &
As mentioned earlier; the time signature of 5/8 is normally broken up into smaller groups of two and three You essentially have a few choices in regards to how to count beats within these time signatures Counting is only verbalized in practice so personal choice (and comfort) can come into play when reading in asymmetrical time signatures For instance,a measure of 5/8 (or 5/4or 5/16 for that matter) can be counted as 1-2-3-4-5 or 1-2-1-2-3 (or 1-2-3-1-2).
With all of that being said it is recommended to use all numbers (1-2-3-4-5) when first learning how to read in odd meters, in order to solidify your ability to count each beat with the actual beat number: Once you are comfortable with reading in these meters, you can begin to alter your counting if you so choose: Keep in mind that tempo, instrumentation and notation may affect how you decide to count:

Unit Six reading | techNIQUe per{OrMaNce Chapter 1: 7/8,7/4 and 7/16 Time Signatures In the last unit (Reading 4, Unit 5) we looked at the asymmetrical time signature of 5/8. Remember that 5/8 normally has two dominant beats that are not of equal value. In 5/8 there are five eighth notes per measure (with each eighth note getting one beat) with the dominant beats usually on beats 1 and 4 or 1 and 3, depending on the rhythmic template:
7/8 Time Signature The time signature of 7/8 has seven beats per measure; with every eighth note receiving one beat: In 7/8 each measure can also be divided into smaller groups of two and three eighth notes to create dominant beats: The two most common 7/8 rhythmic phrasings are: 1) eight notes grouped as two groups of two and one group of three or 2) one group of three and two groups oftwo eighth notes, as follows: Ex. 6.1

Unit SeveN reading | techNIQUe pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Basic Concept of Mixed Meters Now thatyou have an understanding of common and odd time signatures, it is time to lookat mixed meters. Achart in mixed meters (also referred to as changing meters) has fluctuating time signatures There are many charts that may have a few bars that divert from the initial time signatures, but charts in mixed meters change time signatures very often-_so often that there is no dominant time signature per se. For example, you may see a chart that alternates between 4/4 and 3/4 every measure: In the example below (Ex 7.1), each measure is labeled with the appropriate time signature: Notice at the end of the first stanza a preparatory time signature (4/4) is provided. Ex 7.1 Alternating time signatures Preparatory time signature
A BB
13XA F
The above example (Ex. 7.1) may also be written with both time signatures written only at the beginning of the piece of music, which indicates that a particular pattern will be followed This method allows for clearer notation within each measure; for example: Ex 7.2 Multiple time signatures
3 FFF X MR EFAE X/A
The following is another example that alternates between 2/4 and 3/4.This example could be written in 5/4, although the bass line; chord movement and overall feel could determine whether this is written in 5/4 or 2/4+ 3/4.Remember that a composer (or arranger) has the freedom to choose their notational methods and it is the performer's job to execute the reading: Ex 7.3 Alternating time signatures

Unit 7 Chapter 3
Chapter 3: Advanced Mixed Meter Concepts In the previous examples within chapters and 2,only the top number of the time signatures changed: In other words,; the only thing that changed between the meters was the number of beats each measure received: Now that you understand this concept; the next step is to change the bottom number of a time signature: When the bottom number changes, the note that receives each downbeat will change. In the example below (Ex. 7.10), we have three measures of 4/4 followed by one measure of 5/8. In the measures of 4/4 there are four beats to every measure with quarter notes receiving one beat: In the measure of 5/8 there are five eighth notes with each receiving one beat: The eighth notes in the measures of 4/4 will move by at the same rate as the eighth notes in the measure of 5/8. Thus, the counting of each beat will change as follows: Ex 7.10 AIl eighth notes travel at the same rate

Unit eight reading | techNIQUe pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Subdivision Changes Drum Performance 3, Unit 7 presented a chart that went from an eighth-note triplet swing feel to a straight eighth-based bossa nova asa way to set up a new feel and subdivision In this unit; subdivision changes will be expanded upon and explained in more detail: Subdivision (and feel) changes are used in all genres of music Within an ensemble; these changes rely heavily on the drummer to help facilitate the transitions from one feel to another by means of projecting upcoming subdivision changes by use of setup fills. Drummers must develop a vocabulary of' "go to" setup fills that clearly set new feels.
As you know, many drum charts are merely lead sheets that do not provide written out fills and grooves When it comes to subdivision transitions most writers will leave it up to the drummer to choose how these transitions will be executed:
To get started; take a basic rock feel and transition it into a shuffle feel The tempo is 110 BPM for both feels, meaning the quarter-note pulse_wilLnot_change: What will change is the eighth-note subdivision: For example; the rock feel uses straight eighth notes, while the shuffle feel employs eighth-note triplets. Play eight bars of the rock feel (with a setup/transition fill in bar 8), then play eight bars of a shuffle feel that also transitions back to straight eighth notes Ex 8.1 J = 110 Tempo indicator

Lead Sheet Interpretation The following breakdown of this 16-bar exercise (Ex: 8.1) provides orchestration suggestions needed to make the transitions between subdivisions seamless and smooth:
Letter A In bars 1-6,a solid rock groove must be played with consistency and accuracy as follows: Ex 8.2
2 3
F 5
In bar 7 the rock groove continues On beat 4 of bar 7 the straight eighth notes should stop; replaced by a unison quarter note played between the snare drum and an open hi-hat The open hi-hat serves as an alert; disrupting the straight eighth notes, leading the ensemble (and listeners) into the next (transitional) bar: For example: Ex 8.3 3 7 3 3 3 3 3
5
6
8
Bar 8 serves as the transitional bar; setting up the eighth-note triplet feel  Physically you are making a lot of movement and mentally you are preoccupied with successfully performing the transition, so a simple fill should be played This is not the time or place to try out a new; complex fill Play the bass drum alone on the downbeat of beat in bar 8. Between this and the open hi-hat on beat 4 of bar 7, you are given time to get _ your arms in place to execute the eighth-note triplet double stops (between the snare drum and floor tom) for the remainder of bar 8. Each downbeat should be slightly accented in order to maintain the quarter- note pulse along with the bass drum playing all downbeats. For example: Ex 8.4 3 > 3 > 3 5 3
/ / /7+ 5 6
1383 8
3332
Bass drum alone on the downbeat of beat

Bar 16 serves as another transitional bar used to set up the straight eighth note feel. Again, there is a lot of physical movement involved going from bar 15 to bar 16. Thus, on beat of bar 16, play the bass drum alone on the downbeat: Starting on the"&"of beat 1, play double stops between the snare drum and floor tom until beat 4. On beat 4,play a flam on the downbeat and the bass drum on the"&"to provide an audible "4 &"leading the band back to the original rock feel. For example: Ex 8.7

Unit 8 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Simple Metric Modulations Overview The straight eighth note to swing eighth note subdivision change (or vice versa) is the most commonly used feel change in music As mentioned, within subdivision changes, the value of the downbeat does not change: However; feel changes other than straight to swing are very apparent in all musical genres These feel changes require a change in our counting system, thus creating a metric modulation: As you learned in Drum Performance 4, Unit 3, metric modulations may create changes in feel, meter or tempo derived from a note value encountered (within the chart) previous to the modulation:
In Drum Performance 4, Unit 3, the chart modulated from quarter notes at 165 BPM to eighth notes at 165 BPM: The chart used a value indicator showing that quarter notes are now eighth notes, creating a new tempo of 82.5 BPM (one half of 165) as follows: Ex 8.9 Value indicator On Cue: D E7 A7 E7 B7
Open vamp for Gtr: Solo
Open vamp for Gtr: Solo 5 3 On Cue: D S al Coda 6, 1  & 2 & 3 & 4   &
J-165
= 82.5
The metric modulations presented in Units 8 and 9 are commonly used modulations that allow a composer to change feels within a chart in the clearest and easiest possible manner These are practical modulations, which differ from more complex modulations, which will be explained in Unit 10 of this book
83
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1073.PNG ---
Chapter 3: The 4/4 to Cut Time Modulation 4/4 to Cut Time: Overview In this example; the chart will go from I6th-note funk feel in 4/4 to a samba in cut time: The funk section is written in 4/4 in order to display 16th-note patterns, which are idiomatic to the style The samba section is written in cut time to notate the ensemble figures as simply as possible; using larger note values. Also, the samba is known as a two-bar pattern; writing it in 4/4 would mistakenly notate the samba as a one- bar pattern:
The Chart The chart has two sections: letter A, which is twelve bars long (in 4/4) featuring a one-bar funk groove; and letter B, which is 16 bars long (in cut time), featuring a two-bar samba pattern: Both sections have ensemble figures that need to be set up and orchestrated: Ex 8.10 =84

There is a value indicator at bar alerting the reader that the quarter-note pulse from letter A is now equal to the value of half notes In other words, half notes in letter B move at the same tempo as quarter notes in letter A. There are ensemble figures in bars 4, 8 and 12 of letter B. Due to the modulation/value change, the ensemble figures in bar 15 and the four-beat fill in bar 16 are identical to the last two bars of letter A. You then D. Unit Ne reading | techNIQUe pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Modulation Techniques As you learned in Unit 8, metric modulations can be used to facilitate smooth (and musical) tempo and feels changes within a chart: In this unit; two more common modulations are presented to help you familiarize yourself with the focal points and techniques used to professionally execute modulations: Modulations: Stylistically Correct Notation Modulations not only allow for tempo and feel changes, but they also allow the performer to be presented with the most common (and practical) notation possibilities available. For instance; there are certain feels and styles that are always written a certain way, i.e: jazz is written in 4/4 with the hi-hat on beats 2 and 4 as follows: Ex 9.1 150 Tempo Swing feel Jazz ride cymbal pattern
Hi-hat on beats 2 and 4
Although the examples below (Ex. 9.2) will sound correct to the listener (they would most likely be unaware of the count off; listed tempo and how the chart is notated) it would feel strange to count (and read) jazz in this manner: Ex 9.2
J = 75 Swing feel
Tempo
Hi-hat on every upbeat
=75
Swing feel
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1079.PNG ---
As you can see, these examples (Ex. 9.2) place the hi-hat on every upbeat, which is not the correct way the hi-hat is felt in jazz Also, the notation for the jazz ride pattern is much more dense and complex than it needs to be: This stylistically incorrect notation will affect how The groove is interpreted, resulting in a groove that is stiff and flat Therefore; notation has a strong influence on how a groove is perceived by the performer: Another example of stylistically incorrect notation would be writing an up-tempo Latin chart in 4/4 as opposed to the preferred cut time Most Latin grooves are two-bar patterns and are felt in"2 "While it is not technically incorrect to notate an up-tempo Latin chart in 4/4,this is not the accepted standard, which can have an adverse effect on how the groove ultimately feels (and how ensemble figures are interpreted):
The example below displays stylistically correct notation (or preferred notation) for a traditional mambo pattern with ensemble figures. The pattern spans two bars, the bass drum pattern lands on the "ah"of beat 1 and the "&" of beat 2 (remember that cut time is 2/2, two beats per measure) and the ride pattern and ensemble figures are simply written with larger valued notes (eighths; quarters and dotted quarters). This is correct notation for a mambo as follows: Ex 9.3 = 116 Mambo Two-bar pattern
2 Again;, although the next example below (Ex 9.4) is not "technically" (or mathematically) incorrect; it is not the preferred notation to use when writing up-tempo Latin charts For example: Ex 9.4 J-116 Mambo One-bar pattern Z73

Unit ten reading | techNIQUe pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Advanced Metric Modulations As you learned in Drum Technique 4, Unit 9, metric modulations are used to create rhythmic illusions over an existing rhythmic template (for example; using eighth-note triplets grouped in sets of four (or two) to create the illusion of the tempo speeding up or slowing down). While creating rhythmic illusions are excellent for soloing ideas, these can also be used as literal modulations into an entirely new tempo and feel For example, an illusion can be created by grouping eighth-note triplets into packs of four: Ex 10.1 3 3 3 3 3 3 3 3
3
3
3 >
3 >
3 7/FaFJE)F3 F
Moving this idea (Ex 10.1) to the drum set exploits the illusion even further: Ex 10.2
3
3
When using a modulation to create a rhythmic illusion, the intention is to return back to the original idea that existed before the illusion: As mentioned in Drum Technique 4, Unit 9, it is imperative that you count through these illusions in order to not actually fall for your own superimposed rhythmic illusion:
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1089.PNG ---
When a modulation is used as a vehicle to actually changethe_tempo the illusion is first created before the actual modulation occurs, in order to set up the new tempo and feel. Using the example above (ex: 10.2) , let's see what a literal modulation looks like in regards to notation techniques Now, instead of the eighth- note triplets remaining constant; by use ofa value indicator they: will morph into I6th notes ata new tempo: Keep in mind that these examples contain abbreviated phrases that would normally last longer: For instance: Ex 10.3 J =88
3

J-66
Asyou can see above (Ex. 10.3),the eighth-note triplets, after being grouped in fours, modulate into four I6th notes per beat creating a slower tempo To reverse this (reverting groupings of four I6th notes back into eighth-note triplets), simply group 16th notes into groups of three to set up a triplet template once again. For example: Ex 10.4 J-88

About the Author
Stewart Jean was born and raised in Deptford, New Jersey, where he was introduced to music through his father's love of classical music and his friends' Led Zeppelin LPs. Stewart's passion for music grew as he studied drumset; timpani and mallets in high school. Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician:
lauug
At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree, Stewart became a sought-after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings, tours and shows.
After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles, maintaining steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the-point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists' lists.
Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years, Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves; Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards.
108 Drum Reading 4
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0510.PNG ---
Unit ONe reading | techNIQUe perfOrmance Chapter 1: Fulcrum & American Grip Fulcrum The fulcrum is the pivot point for the drumstick within your grip. The thumb and the forefinger should be across from each other (Ex 1.1), not wrapped (Ex 1.2). This fulcrum should be positioned about one-third up from the bottom of the stick_-this will be the"sweet spot" ofthe stick Ifthe fulcrum is too far up the stick, it will not be able to develop enough momentum: If the fulcrum is too far back, the stick will feel top-heavy and will have a very sluggish response: An easy way to discover the sweet spot is to use your hand to physically bounce the stick against the head while maintaining a loose fulcrum: Ex 1.1 Ex 1.2
The rest ofthe fingers should be gently wrapped around the stick, resting on the first crease (knuckle) of each respective finger (Ex: 1.3). This positions the stick for proper finger technique; which will be addressed in future units. Ex 1.3
Drum Technique
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0511.PNG ---
Unit
Chapter 1
If all of these positions are in place; there should be an open space in between your thumb and forefinger (Ex 1.4). Ex 1.4
American Grip American grip is the angle in which the hands are positioned in a resting posture The thumbs should be at about a 45" angle upward (Ex: 1.5). The sticks should be pointed at slight angle down toward the drum (Ex: 1.6); this will keep the hands in proper position for tap and/or up-stroke applications Ex 1.5 Ex 1.6
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0512.PNG ---
Chapter 2: Gladstone Technique/Free Stroke Technique is defined as: a The way in which the fundamentals, as of an artistic work, are handled: b Skill or command in handling such fundamentals
There is no one "right" technique in playing the drums. Technique serves two purposes: one; to allow you to freely express yourself without encumbrance, and two, avoiding unnecessary injury: However; the musicians and athletes that seem to display super-human physical ability have been able to tap into the natural physical properties that enable them to display such virtuosity; this is technique worth exploring:
The first technique we will be focusing on is the Gladstone technique, also known as the free stroke. This technique is vitally important,as it will serve as the foundation for most of the techniques you will use:
The main objective of this technique is to allow the stick to naturally bounce back to where the stroke began: Just as you would bounce a ball, you allow the sticks to bounce back into your hands, free of any restriction:
To become familiar with this concept; we are going to start from an up position (Ex: 1.7), and simply throw the stick down. Allow it to bounce back in your hand while leaving your fingers extended downward (Ex 1.8). This is an isolation exercise:
Ex 1.7
Ex 1.8
Just for this exercise; keep the fingers = down with a loose; open grip until the stick fully rebounds back to its starting position. This way,you will begin to fully appreciate the natural potential of this rebound stroke
Drum Technique
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0513.PNG ---
Unit
Chapter 2
Once this feels somewhat comfortable; begin to move your wrist with the natural motion of the stick After you throw the stick, bend the wrist back and follow the stick as it returns into your hand (Ex: 1.9). Don't lift the stick with your hand; allow the stick to naturally bounce back into your hand. Catch the stick with your hand, not your fingers. Ex, 1.9
Now that weve established the relationship between our wrist and natural motion of the stick, we are going to play continuous strokes with each hand individually Throughout the exercise the stick will not stop; it will stay in constant motion. The lateral movement of the stick should be very even and consistent There should be no accents. Allow your wrist to move in sync with the rebounding stick; but make sure it does not become the primary force that is enabling the continuously rebounding strokes: Ex 1.10
The following exercise will reveal how the height of the rebound will be determined by how fast the stroke is. To display this, we will play one bar of eighth notes, followed by one bar of sixteenth notes. For the eighth notes the stick height should be bouncing back to a full stroke position (Ex: 1.12), while the sixteenth notes should be at a half stroke position (Ex. 1.13). Ex 1.11 Full Strokes Half Strokes JEFF FES : [ W [ @ [ [ @ R R R R R R R R R R R R R R R R
Ex 1.12
Ex 1.13
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0514.PNG ---
Chapter 3: Control Stroke/Down Stroke A control stroke occurs when you deny the natural rebound of the stick It is very similar to the free stroke, but just after the stick strikes the drum you will use the back two fingers to stop it from rebounding (Ex 1.14). This will place the stick in the resting position of 1-2 inches above the head (Ex: 1.15). Ex 1.14 Ex 1.15
Remember to catch the stick low and after the impact Don't' catch and choke the stick before impact this will not only choke the open sound of the drum but will also transfer all of the energy back into your wrist: This is not ideal if you want to avoid injury:
Also, as general rule, keep your sticks in a down position when youre not using them. When in an up position, the sticks can only go down: But down position allows for more options; and this is generally preferred.
The following exercise will incorporate both free stokes and down strokes. The idea is to apply the down stokes only as a transition from each sequence of isolated free strokes. Concentrate on minimal effort and allow the sticks to do the work Breathe and be very relaxed The following exercise will incorporate both free stokes and down strokes The idea is to apply the down stokes only as a transition from each sequence of isolated free strokes. Concentrate on minimal effort and allow the sticks to do the work. Breathe and be very relaxed: If you feel any tension slow down or rest for a minute This will take time to feel completely comfortable; but daily practice will produce the desired results. Ex 1.16
Down Stroke
Down Stroke

Unit 1 Chapter 3
ON YOUR OWN
Homework Be able to explain what a fulcrum is and demonstrate American grip position: Be able to allow the stick to naturally bounce back to its starting position after the stroke (Ex: 1.7 and Ex 1.8). Be able to play continuous eighth notes using the Gladstone/free stroke method (Ex. 1.10). Incorporate down strokes within a series of free strokes (Ex 1.16). Memorize and play ex 1.17 by effectively transition from free strokes to down strokes
Practice Suggestions Read the entire lesson at least twice: Use a mirror or video monitor to check the different angles of your technique for consistency: Play - the Gladstone isolation exercise (Ex. 1.7 and Ex. 1.8) until you can allow the stick to bounce back naturally with no restriction from your hand:. Play Ex: 1.10 for five minutes per hand every day: Play - the free stroke/down stroke exercise (Ex: 1.16) for five minutes every day: Play Ex 1.17 for five minutes every day while monitoring the heights of the free strokes and down strokes:
==================================================

Unit twO reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: German Grip In German grip, the palms of the hands are parallel to the playing surface (Ex. 2.1). Apply the fulcrum you learned from Unit and lightly wrap the rest of your fingers around the stick (Ex 2.2). Ex 2.1 Ex 2.2
When you have both sticks in hand with each tip at the center of the drumhead, notice that the angle at which they meet is a 908 angle (Ex. 2.3).
Ex 2.3
The German grip primarily uses the wrist, which makes it great for power and utilizing the Moeller technique that we will be addressing in the next unit: Although this technique doesn't favor the use of fingers, it does place them in an effective position to stop the rebound for our down strokes; which we explored in the previous unit:
When beginning to explore this technique make sure your elbows don't flare out too far like a chicken wing; keep them relatively close to your sides. You can check this by seeing ifyour sticks have formed an angle greater than 908 (Ex. 2.4).
Ex 2.4
10 Drum Technique
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0517.PNG ---
Unit 2 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: French Grip
In French grip, the palms are facing directly towards each other in a vertical manner; the thumbs will be on top and aligned with the sticks (Ex. 2.5).
Ex 2.5
To position the sticks into French grip hold you hand out as ifyou were to shake another's hand (Ex. 2.6). Now place the stick in between the thumb and first crease of your forefinger and lightly wrap the rest of your fingers around the stick (Ex 2.7).
Ex 2.6
Ex 2.7
Because ofits position, French grip primarily uses fingers The sticks will have a much lighter feel and produce a more open sound: Since the stick moves more freely with this grip it has become a popular choice for ride cymbal work and faster playing at softer dynamics
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0518.PNG ---
Chapter 3: Traditional Grip
All of the grips we've discussed so far are considered matched grip, where both hands are identical. Traditional grip breaks this model by using two separate grips For a right-handed drummer; the right hand will incorporate an overhanded, matched grip technique; generally American or German grip. However; the left hand will incorporate an underhanded grip.
This technique descends from military marching drummers who played drum slung from their shoulder At that time it was difficult to march with a flat drum so they positioned the drum to ride closer to one hip and at an angle (Ex 2.8), which forced them to alter their grip.
Ex 2.8
To position the stick in this underhanded traditional grip, position your hand as if you were to use French grip. Place the stick on the webbing ofyour thumb and forefinger (Ex. 2.9).
Ex 2.9
12 Drum Technique
Nuaa
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0519.PNG ---
Unit 2 - Chapter 3
Curl the ring finger and pinkie underneath the stick so the stick is resting on the ring finger; close to the fingernail (Ex. 2.10) .
Ex 2.10
Lastly, curl your forefinger and middle finger over the top of the stick (Ex 2.11).
Ex. 2.11
Since traditional grip incorporates two separate grips, it becomes very difficult to achieve an equal sound and feel from both hands. This is not to say it's inferior to matched grip techniques; it will just require more time and practice to develop. A list of the greatest drummers of all time will prove this techniques validity:
13
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0520.PNG ---
Chapter 4: Second-Finger Fulcrum In Unit we developed a fulcrum using our thumb and forefinger: However; another popular position for the fulcrum is substituting the second finger for the forefinger: To establish this, we are going to drop the forefinger slightly down and allow the stick to rest in between the first and second crease of the second finger (Ex. 2.12).The thumb will remain on the stick; positioned slightlyin between the forefinger and second finger (Ex 2.13).
Ex 2.12
Ex 2.13
A second-finger fulcrum will coincide more with a wrist technique and allow power to be more accessible This fulcrum is also popular with heavier back-beat drumming; as the stick will naturally have a more open feel, and the impact of each stoke will be felt considerably less
Because both fulcrums have their advantages and disadvantages, a proper understanding of both is essential for a professional drummer: Depending on the dynamic; intensity and/or speed of the music, one of these fulcrums will allow you to express yourself more fully and be free of injury:
To become familiar with this position we will use the same eighth-to-I6th-note exercise (Ex: 1.17) from Unit 1. Use the American grip first; then experiment with the German grip: Ex 1.17

16 Drum Technique

--- SOURCE: IMG_0523.PNG ---
Unit 2 - Chapter 6
Chapter 6: The Drum Seat One of the most overlooked aspects of playing the drums is the seat and where to sit on it. The reason why it may be difficult to play the drumset comfortably and consistently is because it requires the use of all four limbs. Generally we use our feet to balance ourselves; but while playing the kit we need to have as much freedom with our feet as we do with our hands This means we can no longer dependon our feet for_balance while_plaving drums: Our balance needs to center on our seat:
To create this shift of balance from our feet to our seat we need to sit towards the backside of the drum throne (Ex. 2.28). Sitting at the front of the throne will transfer all of your weight directly down to your feet thus shifting your center of gravity (balance) to your feet (Ex 2.29).
Ex 2.28
Ex. 2.29
Not only will sitting on the backside of the throne free up your feet; but it will also relieve much unneeded stress from your lower back and hips. Can you play drums while sitting on the front side of the throne? Of course: But the purpose of studying technique is to understand how the body and the natural properties of physics coincide and how we can align both of them for our greatest gains
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0525.PNG ---
Unit three reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Moeller Technique The Moeller technique was formally introduced by Sanford A "Gus" Moeller; an influential drum teacher in the early 1900s. Since then; drum icons Jim Chapin and Joe Morello have championed and taught this technique to scores of today's top drummers. The main purpose of this technique is to provide speed and power with less energy and tension, thus allowing the ease of playing accented patterns free of injury:
Ex 3.1
The Moeller technique is based on wind-up movement from the arm followed by a whipping motion generated from the elbow: To get started, use American or German grip and focus more on a second finger fulcrum with a minimum amount of tension; this will allow the energy to flow through the stick easier Move the stick from the 4-oclock position to the 3-oclock position (Ex. 3.1). Now, lift your wrist up, with a loose grip, so the stick is pointed down directly to the center of the drum but not touching (Ex. 3.2). Make sure to keep your palm facing- towards yourself; don't turn it out and away: Finally; while keeping your wrist completely relaxed, quickly drop the elbow as you bring the stick back to the original 4-oclock position (Ex. 3.3).
Ex. 3.2
Ex 3.3
One ofthe most challenging aspects in executing this technique properly is not allowing the wrist to tense up. As drummers, we are conditioned to use our wrist for most of the strokes we play. However; any amount of tension from the wrist or forearm will reduce the power and speed this motion naturally generates.
19
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0526.PNG ---
The following exercises are designed to be played at a slow tempo (50-60 bpm): Use this opportunity to watch the natural flow of your shoulder-elbow-wrist whipping motion: Ex 3.4
Ex 3.5
1 ]
|
As you begin to play these exercises faster you will notice the motion will start to minimize and involve less shoulder and elbow: Using this technique at a fast tempo will mostly utilize the wrist to produce the whipping motion; this is referred to as "low Moeller"
The low Moeller is accomplished by allowing the wrist to come up while the hand stays down (Ex 3.6). As you keep the hand relaxed and the fulcrum slightly squeezing the stick, snap the wrist down (Ex. 3.7). The tip of the stick will come up as the butt end of the stick goes down: Allow the natural flow of energy to engage the stick and bring the stroke to completion (Ex. 3.8).
Ex 3.6
Ex. 3.7
Ex 3.8

Unit 3 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Accents, Taps and Up Strokes One of the greatest advantages of learning the Moeller technique is its ability to offer the drummer an effective way to play accents within a series of notes Accents and taps are crucial to a drummer's vocabulary, as they are one of the few ways we can allow expression and color to contribute within our performance
An accented note is played louder than the other notes; this will make it stand apart from the rest: For the sake of this study, taps will be unaccented notes, generally played one to two inches above the drumhead (Ex. 3.8).
Ex 3.8
A very common and widely overlooked stroke is the "Moeller Up Stroke:" This is essentially a tap stroke; but this technique allows you to play a tap if it precedes any accented Moeller stroke:
To effectively utilize this technique, start with the stick in a tap position and with a loose grip (Ex. 3.9). As you begin to raise your forearm allow the tip ofthe stick to drop and tapthe drumhead (Ex. 3.10). Make sure you don't raise your forearm first and then drop the stick; the motion should be simultaneous
Ex 3.9
Ex 3.10
In conjunction with the low Moeller technique; this stroke will prove to be very effective in playing faster eighth and I6th note riding patterns, which we will explore in future units.
21
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0528.PNG ---
Chapter 3: Technique Control Exercises The following exercises will demonstrate how various accents, taps and up strokes can be utilized within the Moeller technique: Begin each exercise at a slow enough tempo where you can focus on each individual stroke; whether it be an accent; tap or up.
Remember ifit'snot an accent; it is a tap, and the Moeller up stroke (tap) always precedes an accented note: Also, if a tap follows an accent; that accented note will essentially utilize the same control/down stroke technique we developed in Unit 1. --- SOURCE: IMG_0529.PNG ---
Unit 3 - Chapter 4
Chapter 4: Bass Drum Technique Heel Up & Heel Down Bass drum technique is one of the most widely overlooked aspects in effectively playing and studying the drums Just as we explore and develop various stick techniques to gain the freedom that allow us to express ourselves, we should also have a fundamental understanding of how to control our feet.
Ex 3.16
Basic bass drum technique generally falls into two categories: "heel down" and "heel up" The heel-down position is just what the name implies; drop your heel to the ground (or base ofthe pedal plate) and then allow your foot to rest on the remaining surface of the pedal (Ex 3.16). This position solely relies on the ankle to be the pivot point in transferring the energy from the foot to the pedal:
The advantages of this technique that it allows you to play very soft and controlled,and it will help maintain your overall balance (even though we learned in the previous chapter our balance should be centered on out seat) The disadvantage of heel-down technique is it isolates the shin muscle   (tibialis anterior). Whenever you press down on the bass drum pedal this muscle is doing all the work, and it can quickly become fatigued ifyoure playing fast and/or loud passages of music
Ex 3.17
Heel-up position is somewhat the same as heel-down; but the heel is slightly raised off of the pedal board about one to two inches (Ex: 3.17) . Heel-up offers more options in terms of dynamics and speed The heel-up technique is also less fatiguing on the shin muscle: Since we have raised our heel, the shin is no longer isolated; others muscles in our leg can now take some of the responsibility: This position allows us to use some of our upper leg for energy and power; however weare not relying on our_upper leg_to_provide_theenergy for thebass drum stroke The focus is still on the pivoting of the ankle to provide the stroke; just as we did with the heel-down position:
Think of heel-up as a "heel-down motion with a heel-up position" The pivoting of the ankle should be identical in both positions A good way to gauge if you're using too much upper leg in heel-up position is to watch your knee as youre playing: Ifyour knee is "bobbing"up and down like a piston, youre using too much upper leg: Of course this will change when you have to play accents or loud sections of a song, but generally the knee should only be moving sympathetically within the stroke:
23
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0530.PNG ---
To bury or not to bury . that is the question. Another; and often highly debated, aspect of bass drum technique is whether or not to bury the beater. Burying the beater is leaving the beater pressed against the bass drum head after it has been played. Not burying the beater is allowing the beater to naturally rebound off the head after it has been played:
This is primarily based on personal sonic preference. Just like any drum, pressing something against the head after it has been struck will quickly stop the natural resonance: If you want that quick and/or muted sound, then bury the beater; if you want the drum to resonate; don't bury the beater: However; if you do decide to bury the beater; pay attention to any residual "dribble"that the beater may softly play against the head if there is not enough pressure holding it to the surface: This is rarely a desirable sound, and one you should be able to control:
Dynamic Bass Drum Exercises The following exercises are designed to help you explore the heel-down and heel-up positions. One ofthe most effective methods to develop complete control is through dynamic exercises; dynamic meaning being able to play at various levels of soft and loud. You will play these exercises with both heel-down and heel- up positions but you will quickly realize that the accented patterns will be much easier to play with a heel-up position:
Finally,asyou begin to get comfortable with these patterns, allow the beater to come backas far as possible after each stroke; this will allow you to take advantage of the full range of motion the pedal is naturally providing: Being able to control this natural motion will prove to be very beneficial when louder bass drum playing is required: You will be able to conserve more energy as you allow the pedal to do the work foryou: Ex 3.18
pp
pp
Ex 3.19
E
In this last bass drum technique exercise, challenge yourself to create the biggest dynamic separation between the unaccented and accented notes When playing the unaccented notes the beater should only come back one to three inches from the head, while with the accented notes the beater should come back as far as possible: Ex 3.20

Unit 3 - Chapter 5
Chapter 5: Seat Height Seat height is subject to the length of your legs: To prescribe the same measurement from the floor to the top of the stool for every drummer would be very ineffective. Rather; base the height of your seat to the relationship of your hips to your knees:
Generally, your hips should be slightly higher than your knees (Ex 3.21). If your hips drop below your knees, your center of gravity will shift and apply much unneeded pressure to your lower back and hips (Ex 3.22). If a seat is too high you may run the risk of only being able to use a full-leg bass drum technique; thus leaving the desirable ankle technique aside (Ex. 3.23). Ex. 3.21 Ex 3.22
Ex. 3.23
Since we all come in many shapes and sizes, this will require some fine tuning: Nevertheless, this offers a solid starting place:

Unit fOUr reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Rudimental Drumming A rudiment is defined as: fundamental element; principle; or skill, as of a field of learning: To create and/or perform anything of significant value requires a certain amount of technique: In playing drums, studying the rudiments is a very effective way to develop technique. Just as a pianist or guitarist will diligently practice scales to gain the freedom to which they can effortlessly express themselves; the practicing of rudiments to the drummer will offer the same results
Every time you pickup a pair of sticks to play any percussion instrument; you will inevitably be playing some kind of established rudiment Rudiments organize; in the most basic forms, the various stickings and rhythms we use to percussively express ourselves. Therefore, any serious student who desires to play to the best of their ability cannot overlook the importance of studying the rudiments:
All of the drum rudiments fall within one of the five rudimental "families" or groups They are:
1 _
Ruffs (3-Stroke, 4-Stroke; etc) 2 Rolls (Long; 5,6,7, etc) 3. Diddles (Paraddidle; Double Paradiddle; etc) 4. Flams (Flam Accent; Flam Tap; = Swiss Triplet; etc ) 5_ Drags (Single; Double; Ratamacue; etc)
All of the rudimental families will be covered in the following units In this unit; the ruff family will be introduced and developed: A ruff is a short single-stroke roll that is generally played either before or after an accent The 3-,4-, 5-,and 7-stroke ruff will be the rudiments focused on for this unit.
One ofthe main"understood" concepts while analytically studying any technique is to focus while you practice but forgetitwhen you perform. The performance should be where all ofthe focused practice naturally surfaces without you thinking about it: Drum rudiments are just that; they require focused, diligent study and, when developed, offer an endless amount of possibilities to your rhythmic vocabulary:
27

--- SOURCE: IMG_0534.PNG ---

Chapter 6: Hi-Hat Technique There are two popular approaches in hi-hat stepping technique: The heel/toe method and the heel-up method. Both serve valuable purposes and are worthy of investigation.
The heel/toe method is primarily used for stepping the hi-hat on beats 2 and 4. The natural motion of this method allows this rhythm to be played without having to focus on it. To utilize this technique begin with your foot resting flat on the pedal (Ex. 4.16), this is beat 1. Next; roll the weight ofyour foot up to the ball/toe area of your foot (Ex 4.17); this is beat 2. As the weight shifts up to the toes; the pressure on the pedal will pull the top hi-hat cymbal down to the bottom cymbal creating a "chick" sound. Aim for a tight; articulate "chick"; weak or "fluffy" sounds are not the goal at this point: Roll back to your heel for beat 3, then repeat for beat 4.
Ex 4.16
Ex. 4.17
One of the reasons the heel/toe method is popular is its ability to anchor oneself Some drummers will subconsciously use this method because they haven't developed a foundation of balance on their seat, which we explored in Unit 2. Remember; when you do use this technique; don't rely on your foot for balance, just allow the motion to do what it is meant to do: play the hi-hat:
The heel-up method is the most widely used hi-hat stepping technique: It offers the ability to play faster and with a bigger dynamic range: Unlike the bass drum technique we explored in the previous unit; this is primarily an upper leg technique We need to access the weight of our leg to help produce a solid, consistent "chick" from the hi-hat; thus the primary motion is now coming from the hip:
32 Drum Technique
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_0539.PNG ---
Unit 4 - Chapter 6
Begin with your heel up a few inches from the pedal plate with your toes/ball of foot resting towards the top ofthe pedal (Ex.4.18). Slightly raise your toes to separate the two hi-hat cymbals, then, using the weight ofyou leg; play down through your heel and toes to produce a solid "chick" sound.
Ex 4.18
To achieve a tight, articulate "chick" sound in both methods, the two hi-hat cymbals have to have enough space to generate the sound: A minimum of one inch between the cymbals in necessary to produce this sound (Ex. 4.19);anything less will be a compromise (Ex. 4.20).
Ex 4.19
Ex. 4.20

Second, press down on the pedal with the ball of your foot, not your toes Toes have a tendency to absorb some ofthe energy to the pedal, thus making it more challenging to effectively play the dynamic spectrum:

--- SOURCE: IMG_0543.PNG ---
Unit Six reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Eighth-Note Triplet Study Even though we touched upon eighth-note triplets with the four-note ruff study, we are now going to explore the various stickings and accent patterns available within this rhythm:
Triplets have a rounder feel than eighth and/or I6th notes. The three evenly divided notes per beat have a softer; flowing - feel  Even though some of the stickings and accents we will be using may seem square and rigid, the idea is to maintain a flowing; round feel.
One of the great benefits of practicing hand-to-hand triplets is the natural alternating sticking that occurs on every beat This insures that both hands will be receiving the same amount of attention, which makes for a very balanced study: Even though the following exercises are not all hand-to-hand, each hand will be focused on equally: Ex 6.1

Chapter 3: Bass Drum and Hi-Hat Coordination Part 1 This chapter will focus on the development of bass drum and hi-hat coordination The following exercises are designed to build a foundation for playing linear and layered rhythms with your feet: Generally, in drumset performance; the hi-hat will be stepping out an onstinato while various bass drum rhythms will played against it: This is where focusing your balance on your seat will prove to be essential We are now involving both feet; which means we can no longer depend on them for balance:
As you play these exercises, allow your hands to rest at your sides or hold your sticks in the resting position one to two inches above the snare head. Donot hold on tothedrum or anything else_toassist your balance: This will defeat the purpose in developing proper balance:

Unit SeveN reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Double Stroke Technique The ability to play clean and even double-stroke rolls is essential for all drummers, although initially; it can be deceptively difficult: By slowing down the required motion and focusing on the basic mechanics, we will make the development of this technique much easier: Ex 7.1
To begin, play one free stroke; remember to allow the stick to bounce back naturally (Ex 7.1). Next; play a down stroke; using your back fingers to stop the rebound (Ex 7.2). Both strokes should have the same dynamic integrity:
Typically, drummers will accent the first note of the double; which is not desirable: The goal in producing an open, clean and evenly subdivided roll is focusing on both notes sounding equal in tone and dynamic This is accomplished by even stick heights and a relaxed grip for both strokes. Allow the sticks to breathe within the hand, applying just enough pressure in the fulcrum to control the natural motion of the stick while not micro-managing it: Ex 7.2
As you begin to increase the speed, you will notice the free stroke will begin to get smaller; and the natural rebound of the stick will be controlled primarily with the fingers The fingers will be "catching and releasing" the second stroke, but don't focus too much on this It will eventually be a natural reaction:
To get familiar with the double-stroke roll we are going to isolate each hand and focus on both individual strokes Use four-part counting as you play these 16th- note partials to help maintain even subdivisions within the notes.
Ex 7.3
Now; put them together to create a double stroke roll: Remember; do not accent the first note of each double and focus on even stick heights. Ex 7.4
Check patterns are used to ensure accuracy in note placement: This is a basic check for doubles: Ex 7.5
R L   R L  R L   R L   R L R L   R L   R L
R   R L L  R   R L L   R R L L   R   R

Chapter 3: Six-Stroke Roll One of the most widely used rolls for fills and soloing concepts is the six-stroke roll. It is comprised of one accent followed by two sets of doubles; and then finishing with another accent: Once you master the following variations, you will begin to benefit from the many useful applications this roll offers.

Chapter 5: Ten-Stroke Roll One of the lesser-known rolls, but an exciting one to explore; is the ten-stroke roll. This roll incorporates a different rhythmic architecture than the previous rolls studied Traditionally,the roll begins with a double on the upbeat followed by three sets of doubles, and then ending with two single accents:

Unit eight reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Single Paradiddle The paradiddle just may be the most popular rudiment for drummers and even non-drummers One of the many beneficial attributes this rudiment offers is its natural ability to alternate the leading hand while playing an even-note phrase: When applied to the drumset; this option will prove to be very useful in facilitating ideas that necessitate various sound sources:
The paradiddle is a combination of singles and doubles, depending on which paradiddle is being used: There are virtually hundreds of various sticking and accent patterns for the paradiddle The objective for this unit is to introduce the four basic paradiddles and their common variations You should be able to play all the various paradiddles with and without their respective accents Single Paraddidle Ex 8.1 ) R
