Chapter 4: Paradiddle-Diddle The most common paradiddle outside ofthe single; double and triple family is the paradiddle-diddle Much like the double paradiddle; it is comprised of six notes but incorporates a different sticking: Just as its name suggests, it is a single paradiddle followed with another diddle; which would create the sticking RLRRLL Unlike the previous paradiddles we've studied, this paradiddle does not naturally alternate, so we will also focus on developing this rudiment with the left-hand-lead sticking as well: LRLRLL Paradiddle-Diddle Ex 8.17

Unit ten reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Hairta The hairta is a modern rudiment that has gained great prominence within contemporary drumset vocabulary Whether played with your hands or your feet; this rudiment offers an effective and simple way of creating excitement within a groove or, more commonly,a fill or solo concept: Even though this is a fairly simple rudiment; the coordination involved can easily get sloppy if youre not intentional with the sticking: This is an alternating-stroke rudiment; although one hand will be playing doubles while the other will be playing singles Since we've been diligently studying both singles and doubles; this rudiment should come together fairly quickly:
The following harita exercises will be presented in both triplet and 16th-note forms and will also be incorporating check patterns to establish and maintain a consistent; even time feel:

Unit ONE reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Flam Technique The main purpose of a flam is to add color; texture or"thickness"to a single note: Flams can be very effective in showcasing a specific note without changing the dynamic quality. Flams can take on various shapes and sizes, especially when applied to the drumset: In this unit we will explore the basic components of the flam and its corresponding rudiments:
The flam is comprised of a grace note tied to a primary note: To begin, start off with your left hand at the down-stroke position (one to two inches above the head) and your right hand at the up position (Ex 1.1). At the same time; allow both sticks to drop down to the head This will create the desired "fLAm" sound; hence its onomatopoeic title Ex 1.1
Hand-to-hand flams can be very challenging but also very rewarding in terms of technique awareness because of the various strokes they ` incorporate: Each hand will be a playing an accent followed by a grace note; this will utilize the Moeller down and up stroke: As each hand alternates, focus on the position of each corresponding down-stroke and up-stroke position, as they should look the same. Keeping the down strokes consistently low will allow you to develop more speed and agility: Ex 1,.2
DownShroke
DounSudke
DowuSucke
DownStrok c
UpStroke
UpStoke
UpStoke
UpStroke
As you play these alternating hand-to-hand flams, pay attention to the "openness" and/or the tightness of the flam. Don't play them too tight,as they will have a tendency to flatten out; thus becoming "double stops" (which are not flams) The goal is to maintain the same amount of openness as each hand alternates:

Chapter 3: Drumset Mobility: Foundational Motion One of the more challenging aspects in playing the drumset is the dynamic range of motion that is required: All drumsets will vary in certain shapes and setups, and the ability to access all the voices comfortably will be determined by how conditioned your mind and body is to those necessary motions:
In the following units, we will be exploring the various ways to move around the kit by utilizing the rudiments and various concepts that have been presented up to this point: Be sure to go back and review the basic components of the rudiments from the previous units before you attempt to apply them to the kit.
The main goal (as always) is control,not speed Practicing drumset mobility should be slow and methodical; this way you will be able to identify your strengths and weaknesses more accurately and make the proper adjustments in a timely manner: Focusing on sound is paramount: The drums should speak openly and evenly,not choked and imbalanced; this is achieved by playing with a loose grip and maintaining even stick heights as you move from voice to voice: Use your eyes to see if one hand looks different than the other: Use your ears to listen for any dynamic inconsistencies. Focus on every motion, don't just "go through the motions" (pun intended):
In the following exercises you will be presented with three different motion concepts: clockwise; counter- clockwise and home base. Alternating single strokes with various note values will be used to explore each concept: Remember to begin slowly and focus on consistency of sound and note placement Moving from voice to voice may hinder the consistent feel ofthe note value being used;this is another reason to practice slowly and effectively:
Clockwise Motion
Quarter notes: one note per drum Ex 1.10
Eighth notes: two notes per drum Ex 1.11
I6th notes: four notes per drum Ex 1.12

Unit twO reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Drag Technique Drags are two grace notes that are tied to a primary note Traditionally, drags do not have a specific note value associated with them, and they can be interpreted as a tight buzz or an open diddle. For our purposes in studying drumset; we will play drags as open diddles with a corresponding note value: Much like the flam technique of alternating the hands with up-stroke and down-stroke positioning; alternating drags apply the same sequential motion (Ex 2.1). Rather than playing one grace note in an up- stroke position, you will now be playing a low diddle: Use the"catch and release" method discussed in Drum Technique 1, Unit 7 to execute the low diddles Once the diddle has been played, raise the hand into a down-stroke position: Following the diddle will be the primary note played by the opposite hand coming from its down-stroke position: Ex 2.1
Much like the flams; hand-to-hand drags can be very challenging because of the various strokes they incorporate: Each hand will be playing an accent followed by a low diddle; this will utilize the Moeller down and up stroke as well as the"catch and release" method for the diddles As each hand alternates; focus on the position of each corresponding down-stroke and up-stroke position, as they: should look the same Keep the diddles low and the primary notes high. The following exercise introduces the first drag rudiment; named the single drag: Ex 2.2
DownStrake
DounStok €
DownStrok e
DounStok e
CalchReleke UpStcke
Calch Relea-e UpStroke
CatchRelea € UpStrdk
Catch Release UpStroke

TrL
Because we are metering the drags with actual note value, this would be another way of writing this rhythm: The drags become the last two 32nd notes of the previous 16th note. Ex 2.3

To thoroughly understand the rhythmic architecture of each exercise, first play the rhythm on the snare only: Once you become comfortable with the rhythmic transitions to and from each note value; begin orchestrating the exercise to the kit as written. Unit three reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Charley Wilcoxon Solo No.1 One of the most effective ways to thoroughly learn something is through variety: Exploring various applications; perspectives and combinations will enable you to build a stronger sense of what this new information can be capable of. Rudimental snare drum solos offer opportunities to further enhance and grow our understanding of the snare drum rudiments we've learned thus far
For these reasons, we will be exploring many of the rudimental snare drum solos from Charley Wilcoxons iconic book, The All-American Drummer 150 Rudimental Solos. These solos will reveal how the ability to combine various rudiments is not only essential to a drummer's vocabulary, but is also an extremely useful musical tool. Try to look at each solo as a piece of music not an exercise. Paying attention to the dynamic markings will allow these solos to have color and life Strive for musical expression don't just play a combination of rudiments: that is not art Use these solos as opportunities to stretch your musical capabilities and reap the rewards of what honest expression offers
For this unit we will be exploring Solo No.1. This 16-bar solo, in the time signature of 2/4,incorporates a handful of rudiments we've studied individually: Here, we get to combine these rudiments to create a piece of music. The rudimental breakdown is as follows:
Bar 1: Flam Taps (as eighth notes) Bar 2: Flam Paradiddles Bar 5: Flamacue with a left-hand Flam Tap ' (as I6th notes) on the"&" of beat 2 Bar 6: Flam Taps' (as eighth notes) Bar 7: Flam Paradiddles Bar 8: Alternating Flams Bar 10: Single Paradiddles Bars 11 & 12: Triple Paradiddles Bar 14: Single Paradiddles Bar 16: Alternating Flams (ending on beat 2)
As with learning any new idea or concept on the drums, the first priority is developing the coordination (assuming the reading is not an issue): Once the mechanics have been worked out; then and only then do you begin to address the dynamic markings Dynamics require a heightened sense of control, which makes learning something new too much for the brain/body to handle all at once: Develop the coordination first, then slowly apply the dynamics This will greatly speed up the learning process, and the outcome will be of higher quality as well:

Six-Stroke Roll Variations The six-stroke roll is one of the most widely used and popular rudiments applied to the drumset: Here, we will explore how the standard and inverted six-stroke rolls, along with a couple of inspired stickings, can be used to further your drumset mobility and vocabulary:
All of the following six-stroke-roll-inspired stickings will be written and played as 16th-note triplets. Here are the four sticking patterns that we will be utilizing: Standard Six-Stroke Roll Ex 3.6

Six-Stroke Roll Drumset Mobility In the following mobility exercises all of the doubles will be played on the snare Restricting the doubles to the snare and orchestrating the singles between snare and toms will create a consistent flow of motion while allowing the singles to effectively "shape" the rhythmic structure of the idea:
Once the general idea has been developed, begin to approach each exercise with a dynamic intent This is achieved by accenting all of the single strokes while maintaining a low dynamic with all of the double strokes on the snare

Unit fOUN reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Charley Wilcoxon Solo No.5 The Wilcoxon solo we will be developing this week is Solo No. 5. Where Solo No. focused on flams and paradiddles; the main focus of Solo No. 5 is drag applications

Solo No. 5 is a 16-bar solo in the time signature of 2/4.Various rudiments are used throughout; but drags are the primary focus The rudimental breakdown is as follows:
Bar I: Single Drags Bar 2: Single Drag and Flam Bar 3: Alternating Flams (as eighth notes) Bar 4: Flam Paradiddles Bar 5: Flam and Single Drag Bar 6: Single Drags Bar 8: Single Drags (as eighth notes) Bar 11: Lesson 25 (starting on beat 2) Bar 12: Lesson 25 Bar 15: Lesson 25 Bar 16: Single Drags (as eighth notes)
Remember to focus on the rhythms and mechanics first: Once these have been established; begin to address the dynamic markings to make this solo come to lifel

Unit 4 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Drumset Mobility: Paradiddles The paradiddle is one of the most popular rudiments because it is comprised of the two main strokes of drumming: the single stroke and the double stroke. Combining these two strokes offers endless application and functionality in terms of drumset mobility: Also, the paradiddles natural ability to alternate the leading hand will offer you more options for orchestrations and rhythmic composition:
In the following exercises; we will explore how the paradiddle can be applied to the kit by using various voicing options and note values. One of the goals of applying rudiments to the drumset is to no make them sound like rudiments; we want to create music, not perform exercises This can be difficult to achieve every time you apply a rudiment; however; the following exercises are designed to get you thinking outside of the basic approach:
Stemming from Unit 9 of Technique 1_ we are going to take various accented paradiddle patterns and use them as a guide to orchestrate the paradiddle around the kit: To streamline this introduction in paradiddle application, all of the diddles will be played on the snare; while the single strokes will be moving to toms and accented/unaccented snare strokes. Once you become comfortable with these concepts, you can develop your own variations by orchestrating the diddles anywhere you please:

Chapter 2: Drumset Mobility: Flams Flams are one of the most effective rudiments to apply to the drumset when a specific note needs to be enhanced and/or featured. By utilizing a few of the previously-studied flam rudiments and applying them to various voicings, you will quickly understand the virtually unlimited possibilities available to you:
All of the following exercises with a flam being split between a tom and snare will always be played in that order: tom, snare. Playing the snare before the tom is an option but will not articulate the relationship ofthe flam as clearly. Also, accents and grace notes are subjective within the following ideas  As you move around the kit some accents will feel natural for you to play, others will not The main objective is to focus on the rhythmic relationship between the two notes ofthe flam,not the dynamic relationship Therefore,no accents will be written:
Non-Alternating Flams Ex 7.5
'Ud # #   #   #  #   # # # # # # # # # # # # #

Unit 8 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Linear Hand/Foot Development Having a strong foundation of hand and foot coordination and/or combinations are paramount in developing a complete drumset understanding and vocabulary: Being able to combine various hand/foot combinations are not only essential to fill and solo development; but more importantly they: give each limb an opportunity to feel and understand time in a greater capacity. Since these are linear (i.e: no two or more limbs/voices are playing at the same time) exercises, each limb cannot depend on another limb to "guide"it: Each limb is now solely responsible for subdividing the time evenly; that is what will make all of these hand/foot combinations sound intentional and applicable
Each of the following exercises will be presented as a basic idea, first as eighth notes, then applied to 16th notes The same coordination is required, only the note value has changed. Count each respective note value out loud,
Following the basic idea will be two separate ways to orchestrate the basic idea Once you become comfortable with these orchestrations; explore and find your unique way of applying these concepts to your individual style: Hand/Foot Combinations

Unit 9 - Chapter 3
Chapter 3: Four-Way Coordination Paraddidles with Bass Drum Permutations
As you've already discovered, paradiddles offer an endless amount of rhythmic options for you to explore and create. Continuing with the bass drum permutation method we've been using with the previous four- way coordination exercises, paradiddles will now be applied to this method.
We will use all ofthe single paradiddles variations from Unit 8 of Drum Technique I: standard, inward, reverse and outward. Refer back to that unit if you need a refresher The exercises can be challenging; so take your time and thoroughly examine the specific coordination required for each permutation The potential these concepts have for building interesting grooves and advanced fills has no bounds These are the essential building blocks for much of contemporary drumming:
Standard Paradiddle Ex 9.16
R L   R R L R L L   R L   R   R L   R L L
R L   R   R L   R L L   R L   R   R L   R L
R L  R   R L  R L
R
R   R L  R L  L R L  R   R L  R L L  R L  R   R L  R L
R
R
R
R
R
Inward Paradiddle Ex 9.17
L   R   R L R

Unit ten reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Linear Hand/Foot Development Pt: 3 By taking the same approach of developing I6th-note linear hand/foot combinations from Unit 8 and eighth-note-triplet linear hand/foot combination from Unit 9,we will now combine the two note values to deepen our understanding of not only coordination but also rhythmic relationships Transitioning from one note value to another takes special focus in subdividing each rhythm according to its note value: Subdividing beat into four equal parts (16th notes) feels much different than subdividing it into three equal parts (eighth-note triplets). Combining these two within a measure provides a great opportunity to understand how these rhythms relate,as well as providing interesting rhythmic possibilities for your hand/foot vocabulary: Hand/Foot Combinations Ex 10.1

Unit ONE reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Further Development of the Upstroke In Drum Technique 1, Unit 3, Chapter you began the study of upstrokes You will now further develop this important technique using I6th notes and eighth-note triplets with alternating sticking: The ability to conserve energy by creating two or more notes with one relaxed motion is an integral part of playing the drums A gentle bend in your wrist willyield the tap stroke. Essentially you are creating two sounds with one motion, i.e. one note on the upstroke and one on the accent: Importantly, lead with your "weaker" hand. A good rule of thumb is to lead with your weaker hand 70% of the time:
Metronome marking for these exercises: BPM = 40...54. 60
Both feet with
metronome always!
Downstroke Exercises 16th Notes To begin with; you will be accenting on the downbeat: We will call this the "downstroke" In Ex 1.1, if the downbeat is accented with the right hand, then the "&"ofthe beat (the other right hand within each group of four I6th notes), will be the upstroke or up motion of the downbeat: By keeping the stick low after striking the accent on the downbeat in Ex. 1.1 and then slightly bending the wrist forward, you will then be able to lightly strike the pad or drum: This needs to be a fluid and natural motion with a minimal amount of squeezing or releasing within the grip. Ex 1.1
R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L
The left hand will now accent the "e" of the group of 16ths. This means that the"ah" will be the upstroke with the left hand: Ex 1,.2
R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L

Chapter 2: The Double-Stroke Roll For a drummer; one of the most important roll strokes is the long roll performed as an open roll. In an open roll, each stroke ofthe stick is clearly audible; producing a crisp sound. (This is in comparison to another very important rudiment which will be studied in later units, the closed roll:) One must remain relaxed throughout this important rudiment; relying on the energy created by the sticks on the drum or practice pad: With enough concentrated practice and effort every drummer can approach perfection with the long; open roll:
The following exercises focus on alternating between one measure of various basic stickings and one measure of double strokes as rebounds: A rebound is one wrist motion followed by a bounce: These exercises are to be performed at 120 BPM, tapping both feet with the metronome: Strive for a clean and distinct sound of all strokes, with no difference between one measure and the next. Ex 1.8
R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R R L L R R L L R R L L R R L L
L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L L R R L L R R L L R R L L R R

--- SOURCE: IMG_1300.PNG ---
Chapter 3: Basic Independence The study of coordinated independence is essential in order to gain command and control of the drumset: The method presented in this (and upcoming units) introduces you to a "tried and true" method to begin developing the control needed to perform at a high level on your instrument To acquire these skills, you must develop disciplined and meticulous practice method There is no substitute for practicing slowly and in a controlled manner
In these exercises, the ride cymbal and hi-hat will play a continuous pattern while the snare drum and bass drum are placed in various downbeat and upbeat groupings The cymbal pattern is a common pattern used in all genres of music which is a quarter note on beats 1 and 3 and two eighth notes on beats 2 and 4. In addition, the hi-hat is played with the foot on beats 2 and 4 as follows: Ex 1.9 Ride cymbal
Hi-hat with foot
When played as straight eighth notes this pattern can be used in Latin, funk; rock, R&B, metal, etc: This common pattern can also be interpreted as swung eighth notes for swung styles such as jazz and hip-hop: As presented in Drum Performance 3, swung eighth notes may be indicated at the top of a chart with a symbol indicating all written eighth notes are swung unless otherwise indicated. For example: Ex 1.10 J-r;_ S Swung eighth notes indicator
Equals
General Rules There are a few general rules to keep in mind when beginning independence exercises 1 . When the snare drum (or bass drum) lands on a downbeat; it will always be paired with the ride cymbal.
Ride cymbal and snare drum together on every downbeat Ex 1.11
Drum Technique 3

2 When the snare drum (or bass drum) lands on beats 2 and 4it will always be paired with the ride cymbal and the hi-hat:

Unit twO reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Double-Stroke Rolls In Drum Technique 1, Unit 7, the double-stroke technique was introduced: Four roll strokes were introduced: the five-, six-, seven- and ten-stroke rolls Utilizing the techniques studied thus far; you will now be introduced to a further study of roll strokes:
Metronome marking for these exercises: BPM = 104...120...132
Both feet with
metronome always!
Traditionally,the eight-stroke roll is comprised of three sets of doubles followed by two singlestroke accents. It is an alternating roll stroke; in that when you terminate one roll stroke; the next roll stroke begins with the alternate hand. The following will introduce you to the various note values and common stickings used by the eight-stroke roll. One way to count the eightstroke roll is to count "1-2-3-4-5" as there are five hand motions Ex 2.1
7 33 FEEF` R R L L R R L R L L R R L L R L R R L L R R L R L L

The nine-stroke roll is an essential rudiment; found throughout the percussion repertoire: It is an alternating roll stroke It is comprised of four sets of doubles followed by one single-stroke accent: One way to count the nine-stroke roll is to count"1-2-3-4-5,"as there are five hand motions Ex 2.2

The eleven-stroke roll is yet another important addition to the double stroke roll family of rudiments This roll stroke does not naturally alternate and will first be practiced "one way" then the "other way" and finally alternating or "both ways" One way to count the nine-stroke roll is to count "1-2-3-4-5-6, as there are six hand motions Ex 2.3
L LRRL LRRLL RL L R R LLRRLL RL L RRL L R R L L RL L R R L L R RL L R

The thirteen-stroke roll is an alternating roll stroke. An easy way to count this roll stroke is"1-2-3-4-5-6-7,"a5 there are seven hand motions: Ex 2.6

Finally, the fifteen-stroke roll is introduced. Count this roll stroke is"1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8,as there are eight hand motions This roll stroke does not naturally alternate; and we will use the above-mentioned method of studying such a roll stroke (i.e: "one way" then the "other way" and finally alternating or"both ways"). Chapter 2: Accented Eighth-Note Triplets Drum Technique 2 introduced accented 16th notes and eighth-note triplets The following exercises build on your knowledge and practice Certain popular music styles (mainly those in more of a jazz idiom) require you to be able to phrase in a swing manner These exercises are designed to reinforce the swing feel This is also an opportunity to practice the up motion, which has been instructed throughout the Technique series: The note before each accented note can be articulated asan up motion. Single-handed practice (especially when concentrating on the weak hand) will improve your technique; speed and endurance It is imperative that your articulation of all subdivisions is clean and precise, and that all notes (snare drum; bass drum and hi-hat) all line up with no flamming between sound surfaces Presented is a simple yet classic bass drum and hi-hat ostinato. Do not accent the bass drum when you accent the snare drum:
Metronome marking for these exercises: BPM =60...80...104
These exercises should be played in four ways: 1 . Right hand lead: R-L-R-L 2. Left hand lead: L-R-L-R 3. Exclusively with the right hand: R-R-R-R 4 Exclusively with the left hand: L-L-L-L

Unit three reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Rudimental Recall Single Paradiddle and Inversions The single paradiddle is an essential rudiment for technique development It is very important for a drummer to be conversant with the many inversions ofthe single paradiddle Presented is an exercise demonstrating the single paradiddle followed by its inversions As with all of the previous exercises; tap both feet with the metronome; playing quarter notes on all downbeats Note: this exercise reverses sticking upon the repeat:

Chapter 3: Single-Stroke Rolls Performing a smooth single-stroke roll of any length and at any tempo is a most important and challenging drumming skill: In the following exercises each roll stroke begins and ends with the metronome as noted. You are adding one note to each successive roll stroke:
Maintaining equal pressure throughout all motions and strokes is ofthe utmost importance: Tension should be kept to a minimum as you (a) increase the number of notes per beat and (b) increase tempo: If you feel tension in your hands, do not raise the tempo of the exercise: Conversely, lower the tempo in order to feel the relaxation in your hands; wrists and forearms:
Metronome marking for these exercises: BPM = 40...48
Ex 3.10 We will begin with the five-stroke roll Learn to count"3"for the lead hand instead of counting all the notes (i.e "1,2,3,4, 5"), as this will help when the tempo increases Remember to alternate sticking as noted.

Some very important concepts and things to remember as you are performing these exercises are: Feet with metronome always! Relax and just turn your wrists. No squeezing and releasing as you perform the exercises Keep pressures constant throughout all the strokes Just turn your wrists. Each stroke begins and ends one inch above the pad and parallel to the floor of the stroke: Make sure youre relaxed Your shoulders, neck, arms should be relaxed throughout the exercises: Begin and end each roll stroke with the metronome:

Chapter 3: Coordinated Independence Exercises Utilizing the same ride cymbal pattern from previous units, introduced now are rhythmic patterns played between snare drum, bass drum and hi-hat: These exercises will enhance your command and control of the drumset by accentuating the intercommunication between all the limbs Your complete attention to detail is required in order to perform all levels ofthese exercises Sound balance and articulation are essential in performing these patterns correctly: The first set of exercises will be performed using a jazz phrasing; where the written even eighth notes are interpreted as swung eighths: Exercises 4.18a, b, € and d are all written out for you: For Exercise 4.18a you are playing the jazzride cymbal; with hi-hat on beats 2 and 4. You will play the written phrase between snare drum and bass drum:
Exercise 4.18b has you performing the jazz ride cymbal pattern: You will play the written phrase between snare drum and hi-hat;
Exercise 4.18c has you performing the written phrase between snare drum and bass drum with the hi-hat on beats 2 and 4
Exercise 4.18d has you performing the written phrase between snare drum and hi hat:
All exercises will be performed in this manner; i.e. with four distinct steps Ex 4.19

Unit SeveN reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Wrist Turn Exercise Throughout the Drum Technique curriculum you have studied singles and doubles with various stickings Now we can investigate an interesting exercise that incorporates these two stickings using wrist turns only, no rebounds Stay on each level for as long as you can This length of time could be one minute per level: It could be twenty minutes per level: Stay relaxed, with a very relaxed and comfortable grip When possible; please lead this exercise (and others within this curriculum) with the "weak" hand.
Since we'll be making transitions between different note groupings, here are some important things - to remember: Keep the grip constant and relaxed no matter the speed. No accents are notated and none should be played during this exercise. When you make the transitions between the different note groups and/or different stickings, maintain your gripl Also, when you make the transitions (i.e. between groups of six notes and groups of five notes), make them smoothly. Metronome will be set at 40 beats per minute (BPM):
Feet with metronome throughout the exercise

Chapter 2: Jazz Style Eighth-Note Triplet Study Continuing your study of coordinated independence; we will now venture into eighth-note triplet phrasing between all limbs. As with our study with eighth-note and 16th-note phrases, you will be performing these exercises using four levels, which are written for you. The fifth and very important level is your contribution as a creative individual This fifth level will be examined in your final exam, so it is most important that you develop a high skill of notation, as this will be graded as well.
Your precise articulation of the eighth-note triplets is essential for these exercises This concentration on articulation will help every level of your drumset performance: It will also help immensely with your jazz phrasing: Ex 7.2

Unit eight reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Wrist Turn Exercises Maintaining relaxed and comfortable grip is very important as you study the technique exercises in all levels and units of this series The ability to maintain your grip as you change subdivisions is the goal of this next singles exercise The tempo will begin at a very slow metronome marking (16th note = 40 BPM) and will only increase when you do not feel any change in tension moving between one level of the exercise (32nd notes) and the next (64th notes).
Level one of the exercise; I6th notes, shall be used as a warm up. The I6th note is equal to the metronome marking: Caution: This is very slow: Each metronome strike is one I6th note Concentrate on your time: As per usual: both feet with the metronome:
Level two of the exercise has you performing one measure of 32nd notes followed by one measure of 64th notes. There needs to be no change in pressure (i.e no squeezing and releasing) moving from one subdivision to the next: There is one measure per subdivision. Five minutes at this level could be considered minimum amount of time Concentrate on relaxing and letting the stick work for you:
Remember to begin most technique exercises with your"weak"hand  In order to switch lead hands; go back to the I6th-note portion of the exercise: At this subdivision, change to the alternate lead hand:
Metronome BPM: 40_ 42...44...48

Uni 7e reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Singles Exercise (Wrist Turns) The ability - to comfortably play odd-numbered note groupings is a relatively advanced and useful musical concept The following exercise has you playing different groups ofnotes all at the same metronome speed: Your wrists, in a relaxed manner; turn faster or slower depending on the note groupings. Goals for this exercise include:
Play each line many times: Utilize a relaxed and controlled playing manner: Don't squeeze or release the grip as you move between the different note groups. Develop the ability to smoothly transition between all note groups: Tap both feet with the metronome at all times. Have the ability to lead with either hand:
Use this technique for playing the odd note groupings: When playing five notes per beat; count the lead hand as"3,"with the alternation of"3"being "1" of the next note group: Ex 9.1

Unit ten reading | techNIque perfOrmance Chapter 1: Rudimental Recall The flammed mill, sometimes called the windmill; is a very useful rudiment both to develop your snare drum technique and also as an application to the drumset The flammed mill is a bounced rudiment; with the Swiss army triplet portion of the rudiment (the two consecutive snare strokes, followed by a single stroke) played as rebounds. Bounced
1R TL
L R
R
Your concentration should be on a very fluid interpretation of this rudiment: Presented here are applications of the flammed mill using groups of five notes In Ex 9.1, there are two phrases of five notes followed by a phrase of six notes Each of the five note groups begins with a flammed mill: You are also playing a common bass drum and hi-hat ostinato as an accompaniment to this musical phrase

Ghuek (filverman 1952 - 2014 Teacher; Mentor; Friend and Percussion Ambassador
Internationally renowned as a specialist in applying Afro- Caribbean rhythms to the drum set, Chuck Silverman brought over 40 years of drumming and percussion experience to our Drum Program: As an author; his books have been included in Modern Drummer's list of "Best Drum Books of All Time"
As an educator Chuck championed the importance of reading and developing a relaxed, purposeful playing style; and incorporated the techniques developed through his extensive study in Brazil and Cuba into his teaching:

Unit One reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Applying Accents to the Six-Stroke Roll Accents are the nuances needed to make patterns take shape and propel rhythms. Just like being able to smoothly mix and transition from singles to doubles,a drummer must be comfortable with applying accents to an array of sticking and patterns. As you learned in Drum Technique 1, Unit 7 and Drum Technique 3, Unit 7, the six-stroke roll traditionally has accents on the two single strokes within the roll (traditional right- hand lead sticking) as follows: Ex 1.1 )= LL R   R L L R   R L L   R   R L L   R   R 1f 11
Single Strokes
The six-stroke roll can also be played as sextuplets as follows: Ex 1.2
R L L   R   R L R
L
L R   R L   R L L   R   R L R L L  R   R
These two versions of the six-stroke roll (Ex: 1.1 and 1.2) can be applied to the drum set The two single strokes can easily be moved to tom toms and cymbals, like so: Ex 1,.3
R L L  R   R L   R L L  R   R L   R L L  R   R L   R L L R   R L
Ex 1,.4
R L L  R   R L   R L L  R   R L  R L L  R   R L   R L L  R   R L
Ex 1,.5

Ex 1.6
R L L  R   R L   R L L  R   R L   R L L  R   R L   R L L  R   R L

Applying Accents to the Double Strokes within a Six-Stroke Roll A great way to develop your ability to add accents to any pattern at will is to apply accents to the double strokes within a six-stroke roll This technique takes you out of your comfort zone with six-stroke rolls, allowing you to create numerous combinations of sticking and accents.
To start you will play an unaccented six-stroke roll in order to not rely on the accented single strokes. Next, you will move one accent through every partial of a six-stroke roll as follows: Ex 1,.7
Be sure not to accent any note: ZEEZZEE3EEE= R R R R R
Add an accent to every down beat making sure not to accent the single left hand on the "ah" of every beat:
R L L  R R L R
L L  R   R L   R L L  R   R L R L L  R R L
Add an accent to the first note of the left hand double stroke: You must throw the left stick downward to produce the accent but must immediately ease up to allow the rebound to fall gently after the accented note: ]X R L L R   R L R L L   R   R L R L L  R   R L R L L   R   R L
Add an accent to the second note of the left hand double stroke: You must snap your wrist after the first stroke of the double in order to produce the accent:

Unit 1 Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Over-the-Bar Groove Technique As you have learned from the Drum Performance series, drummer must be able to interact and communicate (musically and visually) with the rest of an ensemble. While technique does not necessarily tellyou what to play (how to interact appropriately with developed musical ideas), technique does provide you with the facility to be creative and play what comes to your mind in an instant:
Common genres that allow for expansive interaction and creativity are jazz, funk, gospel, Latin and jam bands (Phish, John Scofield, String Cheese Incident and Widespread Panic) While a drummer must maintain steady groove, they must also be able to interact without harming the integrity of the groove
Over-the-Bar Patterns To play"over the bar" means to play a repeated musical phrase (usually grouped in an odd amount of beats) against the grain of a groove template. Playing small groupings of ideas that do not land on beat 1 of a bar is a great technique to help you develop creative freedom:
The dotted eighth note is a very useful rhythm to play in 4/4 that will help you begin to feel over-the-bar concepts. Play quarter notes with both feet while your hands play unison dotted eighth notes (or tied eighths and 16ths) between the floor tom and snare drum: The dotted eighth note is worth one and a half beats (or three I6th notes) Therefore; when repeated this pattern creates a three beat phrase which lands on downbeat every three beats. To start; we will use a two bar phrase as follows: Ex 1.9 Three beats Three beats
Hands
Feet
In the above example; the three-beat phrase repeats on beat 4 of the first measure, creating an over-the- bar phrase To take this concept a step further; lets apply the same three-beat phrase to a four-bar phrase; as follows: Ex 1.10
Hands
Feet
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1371.PNG ---
One importantrule to remember when developing over-the-bar ideas is to count outloud By counting out loud you will maintain where beat is in every bar an essential key when improvising The last thing you want to have happen is to proceed into a hip; " over-the-bar phrase only to get lost within the phrase: Lets now play the three-beat; dotted-eighth pattern over 12 bars, where the entire sequence comes full circle: Count quarter notes out loud. Ex 1.11
Grooving Over the Bar As stated earlier; you must be able to interact creatively with the band while maintaining a steady groove: One way to develop this ability is to apply over-the-bar concepts to bass drum, snare drum, and hi-hat (or ride cymbal) combinations, thus creating small germs of groove ideas to use in improvisational settings
Now that you understand the dotted eighth note over-the-bar concept; this idea can be applied to the bass drum with the snare drum filling in the holes with 16th notes and the hi-hat playing eighth notes, maintaining a 4/4 feel The basic idea is as follows: Ex 1.12
Be sure to play the bass drum with a consistent volume; the snare drum as ghost notes and the hi-hat evenly with no accents The tendency is to feel this in 3/4,accenting every other group of two snare drum notes It is essential that you do not fall into this trap and play all notes evenly while counting quarter notes out loud: Let's play a two-bar phrase utilizing this groove concept: Ex 1.13 Three beats Three beats

Phrasing As stated earlier; you must count to keep from getting lost within an over-the-bar pattern. To avoid getting locked into an over-the-bar pattern, it is good practice to play a clear fiIl at the end of a phrase. The fill will help you (and the band) to reset and recover from an over-the-bar pattern:
In the following exercises you will play the repeated three-note grouping followed by a fill on beat 4 to help you feel how to exit over-the-bar patterns: Two-bar phrases Ex 1.16

Chapter 2: Over-the-Bar Groove Technique Part 2 In Unit you learned how to play over the bar by using a three-I6th-note grouping (a value of a beat and half or a dotted eighth) between the bass drum and snare drum, with the hi-hat playing a steady eighth- note pattern on top. In this unit; we will now apply varying hi-hat patterns over the three-note grouping in order to solidify your ability to feel over-the-bar patterns:
In this first set of exercises you will add an accent on every downbeat (to pair , up with your verbal counting) on the hi-hat: Remember to maintain a consistent bass drum sound The snare drum notes need to be played as ghost notes The addition of the accents on the hi-hat will create a desire to fluctuate the bass drum and snare drum sounds It is important that you avoid allowing the bass drum and snare drum to be sympathetic to the hi-hat pattern:
To begin, you will play a two-bar phrase with the three-note grouping ending on beat 4 of bar 2. Remember to keep the hi-hat moving and exaggerate the accents Also,accent the snare drum on the downbeat of beat 4 in bar 2_ Ex 2.7 Hi-hat pattern:
Exercise:
Next; accent the upbeats on the hi-hat: This will help you to develop more independence in addition to solidifying your ability to play over the bar: Ex 2.8
Exercise:

Unit 2 - Chapter 2
Layered Patterns The next step in developing over the bar patterns is to begin to layer more intricate hi-hat patterns over the snare drum pattern. This technique will give you the ability to create interesting modular groupings between varying playing surfaces with precision To begin;, let's isolate the hands and look at the combinations
The hi-hat pattern will be an eighth note on every downbeat followed by two I6th notes on the "&"and "ah" of every beat, as follows: Ex 2.9
The snare drum pattern will continue to be two I6th notes moving over by a I6th note every three notes, for example: Ex 2.10
Before putting these two patterns together (Ex. 2.9 and 2.10), we will isolate each grouping of two snare drum notes and play them under the hi-hat pattern. This will help you to feel the pairings between the hands. For example: Ex 2.11
Snare drum on the downbeat and the "e" of every beat:
Hi-hat
Snare
Snare drum on the "e" and the "&" of every beat:
Hi-hat
Snare
Snare drum on the "& and the "ah" of every beat:
Hi-hat
Snare
Snare drum on the "ah" and the downbeat of every beat:
Hi-hat
Snare
19
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1383.PNG ---
Once you are comfortable with the pairings from above (Ex. 2.11), you can then apply the over-the-bar pattern Note: the pattern repeats every three bars, but you must try to feel four-bar phrases The complete pattern resets after 12 bars, as follows: Ex 2.12
The next step is to work the bass drum into the mix First, isolate the bass drum over-the-bar pattern under the hi-hat pattern as follows: Ex 2.13

Unit 3 Chapter 4
Unit three reading | techNIque perfOrMance Chapter 1: Developing a Closed Roll The closed roll consists of consecutive and alternating multiple bounce strokes Each stroke has an undefined number of notes that are produced by pushing the stick into the drumhead (or practice pad) using careful amount of fulcrum pressure: The number of bounces is generated by the pressure you use on your fulcrum- the more pressure you use; the less amount of bounces Please note that"pressure" does not mean "rigidity" or"tension"Therefore,it is important that you focus on maintaining even pressure within the hands when developing closed rolls. The closed roll is another rudiment that once mastered-_will open up your ability to be very creative and expressive in your drumming: There are many names for the closed roll: multiple bounce roll; buzz roll and press roll are a few common terms:
Note: closed rolls are notated in different styles For our purposes, the closed roll will be indicated with a"z" attached to the stem as follows: Ex 3.1
To begin developing a smooth closed roll; play one bar of quarter notes with alternating sticking; followed by a bar of closed quarter-note rolls also with alternating sticking: The goal is to try and make each rolled quarter note "bleed" into the next one by producing as many rebounds as possible with each roll stroke: Play at 60 BPM: Ex 3.2
}
One very important factor is to maintain your single stoke "primary pulse" ! when playing a closed roll Try the same idea using eighth notes When playing the second measure, you must maintain the eighth-note movement in the hands: By doing this you will realize that the finger pressure is what produces the "buzz" Ex 3.3 B= R R L R R
Another way to conceptualize the production of multiple bounces is to try and create 32nd-note sextuplets with each stroke. When attempting this you must remember to stay relaxed and not tense up. If the rebounds die out before the next stroke; do not let this discourage you. Be patient; in time the roll will become seamless Start at 60 BPM: Ex 3.4
23
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1387.PNG ---
The next step in developing a smooth closed roll is to apply the multiple bounce technique to 16th notes Again; play relaxed at 60 BPM Ex 3.5
#eEX333 L  R L R L R L R L R LR L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L
Remember that the goal is to produce as many rebounds as possible. To help with the development ofthis technique; start by producing 32nd-note triplets with each stroke: Ex 3.6
At this point you should notice that if you open your hand up a tad and apply less pressure; the stick produces a "wider" series of rebounds Ifyou crush the stick into the head applying more pressure; the result is a tighter rebound response While playing a continuous closed roll you should experiment with varying pressure level within the fingers This technique is helping you to create more finesse and control:

Unit 3 - Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Fill Development Many students often feel as though they do not have a large arsenal of fills at their disposal Students go from teacher to teacher looking for that magic fill to help them get to "the next level" What these students are lacking is creativity: As sensitive musicians; we always want to impress an audience or the other band members when playing (especially when soloing) There is a fear among young musicians that people listening will find that performers dont have a deep vocabulary ifthey repeat themselves This is not the case: Ifyou play something someone really likes, they probably want to hear more of it The trick is to repackage your ideas Think of a fill idea as a seed You play the initial idea and then develop the idea until it transforms into something new:
Compression A great way to develop this way of thinking is to take one simple lick and think of all the ways You can transform it This is a very musical and creative way to enhance your filling and soloing ability To get started we will take a common I6th-note hand/foot idea as follows: Ex 3.8
To ensure proper beat placement of every note, isolate the bass drum and snare voices in each version of this idea: Using this method will give you larger rhythms to focus on during execution: For example, when playing this idea, the isolated bass drum and snare drum notes produce the following pattern (bottom staff): Ex 3.9
As a result we have a pattern of 1-"ah"-2-"ah"-3-"ah"-4-"ah"between the snare drum and bass drum, respectively:
Next; compress this idea (Ex. 3.8) into I6th-note triplets, as follows: Ex 3.10
29
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1393.PNG ---
By isolating the bass drum and snare drum we see that the pattern between the two produces alternating eighth notes. For example: Ex 3.11
Now play two beats of each idea repeatedly: Focus your attention on the snare drum landing on every downbeat: Ex 3.12
Ifyou are struggling to keep a steady tempo (or maintaining a smooth transition between the two patterns) think about the snare drum and bass drum movement; as follows: Ex 3.13
Another example of compressing an idea would be to take the original idea (16th note-based; Ex. 3.8) and transform it into sextuplets as follows: Ex 3.14
The idea above (Ex 3.14) can easily become very sloppy if you do not take special care regarding note placement: A good way to help lock this idea into place is to isolate the right hand movement (and rhythm): The result is alternating eighth-notes triplet (in the right hand) between the snare drum and floor tom, respectively: Ex 3.15
30 Drum Technique 4
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1394.PNG ---
Unit 3 - Chapter 2
Or; you can simply focus on the snare drum movement; which produces a quarter-note triplet; as follows: Ex 3.16
Expansion Ideas can also be expanded,or stretched,to create the illusion that the original idea had slowed down. Let's take our I6th note-based idea (Ex. 3.8) and slow it down by playing it as eighth notes, as follows: Ex 3.17
There is nothing too difficult with this transformation, but it will create a dramatic effect when paired with the original idea. This technique will also help you to develop your ability to control momentum: Play two beats of both ideas (Ex 3.8 and 3.17) at 90 BPM: Ex 3.18
Another great example is to take the original idea and play it as eighth-note triplets, as follows: Ex 3.19
Isolating the right hand reveals a quarter-note triplet pivoting between the snare drum and the floor tom as follows: Ex 3.20
3
These examples are merely the tip of the iceberg in regards to idea development: Using these initial ideas in this unit should open your mind to a wealth of possibilities To create your own unique style and sound you need to explore these concepts on your own: Be sure to write out your ideas when you find some magical combinations:

Unit fOUN reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Velocity and Endurance Exercises Two important aspects of developing proper drumming technique are velocity and endurance: In drumming; velocity relates to the speed and direction an object is moving: Endurance is the ability to maintain velocity. These two areas are developed through muscle control and strengthening: Endurance is diminished when muscles tense up. Thus, if you want to play fast and loud, you must stay loose and relaxed. The following exercises are designed to help you develop both velocity and endurance while maintaining control.
Play . each exercise three ways: 1. Entirely with the right hand: 2. Entirely with the left hand: 3.With both hands together
In addition: 1. Start at 65 BPM and gradually work your way to 80 BPM: 2. Tap quarter notes with your feet in unison: 3. Stay relaxed at all times: Only play at tempos where you can stay relaxed:. A Ex 4.1
B]
5]
4442 1 G4L 58ZZE
3)
35
37]
==================================================

--- SOURCE: IMG_1399.PNG ---
B Ex 4.2
5
8
One big reason why you must have endurance is simply So you can stay relaxed while playing grooves Many young players do not realize the stamina (and control) required to maintain even the simplest of grooves. Also, while you may be able to get through a tune with plenty of gas left in the tank, what about 90-minute set going from song to song without a chance to rest? Over the course of a gig, you must have the endurance to pace yourself and maintain your stamina. Young drummers often break from the groove to play a fill merely because they: are struggling to physically maintain a groove. The following exercises will help you develop control and stamina by playing a repeated pattern (while adding some varying nuances).
The right hand should play at a medium volume with the left hand ghost notes played at pianissimo (very soft). The accents should be exaggerated, played at fortissimo (very loud) Start at 60 BPM and gradually work your way to 80 BPM C Ex 4.3

Unit Six reading | techNIque perfOrMance Chapter 1: Closed Triplet Rolls In Unit 3,closed rolls were presented as a way to develop your touch and finger  control In this unit, you will apply the same closed roll concepts to triplet-based rolls and rhythms This technique will help you to become expressive when playing jazz and other triplet-based styles:
To developa smooth triplet-based roll start by playing alternating (hand-to-hand) eighth note triplets at 110 BPM Do not accent any notes and play in a relaxed, yet controlled manner; as follows: Ex 6.1

Chapter 2: Hybrid Rudiments Hybrid rudiments (or "compound" rudiments) are defined as two or more rudiments combined to produce a new rudiment (i.e "flam" + "paradiddle" ="flam paradiddle" or"flamadiddle") This new breed of rudiments, developed in the drum corps scene; have seeped into modern drum set playing through use by drummers such as Vinnie Colaiuta, Tommy Igoe; Jason Sutter and Glen Sobel. Creatively speaking; these hybrids are challenging; but they - will also expand your ability to play expressively:
In Drum Technique 2, Unit you learned the single flam paradiddle,a standard rudiment--yet also a hybrid rudiment due to the blending of the flam and the single paradiddle: Ex 6.9
5= LR L   R  R   RL   R
LR
L
R  R   RL   R
The Flam Dragadiddle This hybrid rudiment places a drag on the second single stroke on the flam paradiddle: A key item to remember is the hand that plays the grace note plays 32nd notes (diddle) immediately following the grace note: Ex 6.10
] LR  LL   R  R   RL RR
LR  LL   R  R   RL
RR
There are many options available when applying hybrid rudiments to the drum set: One simple application is to place the primary stroke on the cymbals (with bass drum support), as follows: Ex 6.11
LR   LL   R   R   RL   RR
LR  LL   R  R RL RR
The Fliddle-adiddle This hybrid rudiment places a drag on the first single stroke on the flam paradiddle creating a nice cluster of notes on every downbeat; as follows: Ex 6.12
34= LRR L   R  R   RLL   R L L LRR L   R  R   RLL  R L

Chapter 3: The King Kong Groove The King Kong groove is a drum beat which utilizes a distinct rhythm in the hi-hat or ride cymbal It is further enhanced by either basic snare drum patterns (.e "backbeat") or different rudimental sticks between the ride surface and snare drum: This beat is immensely popular and will also give you the opportunity to develop your coordination and command of the drum set:
This sticking exercise uses the Swiss army triplet Make sure that the I6th notes are performed articulately and in a relaxed manner: Ex. 6.16

Unit 0 Chapter
Unit SEVEN reading | techNIque perfOrMance Chapter 1: Basic Linear Concepts The definition of linear drumming is that no two (or more) voices sound at the same time: Linear drumming concepts are great ways to expand your technique and creativity. In fact;you most likely already have played linear ideas without even knowing it To display this concept in the simplest form; take a look at a simple rock beat for example: Ex 7.1
In the above example (Ex. 7.1) the hi-hat and bass drum play at the same time on beats and 3.On beats 2 and 4 the hi-hat and the snare drum play at the same time
Ifyou remove the hi-hat from the downbeats of beats 1,2,3,and 4 (leaving the hi-hat playing solely on the upbeats), a simple linear groove is created, as follows: Ex 7.2
Asyou can see in the example above (Ex. 7.2), no two voices are sounding (playing) at the same time. This is a very simple example of a linear groove:
What happens when linear grooves are played is the template of subdivision (normally coming from the hi- hat) no longer exists You do not have a steady stream of eighth notes present to help other voices"line up" with one another Therefore; your ability to mentally subdivide must be very strong: One great exercise to get you started is to play the following exercise, which exploits the"es"and "ahs" of each beat:
I6th-Note Trainer Ex 7.3
60 Drum Technique 4

Unit 7 Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Linear 16th Note Concept Now that you have a basic understanding of what linear means, the next step is to apply these concepts to 16th-note groove ideas  Most linear grooves are fairly complex with heavy syncopated elements which, again, will require a strong ability to mentally subdivide:
Basic Linear Trainer System The following exercises are designed to help you feel a repeated linear pattern of three I6th notes that travel over the bar: By doing this exercise, you will be able to feel syncopated linear patterns in all possible 16th note subdivisions
Start by playing the following four-bar pattern between the hi-hat, snare drum and bass drum: Play = allnotes at a mezzo-forte dynamic; avoiding accents any notes Take notice of the "turnaround" bass drum double between the last bar of the pattern (the "ah" of beat 4) and the downbeat of bar 1. You must subdivide 16th notes verbally (out loud) while playing this pattern. Start at 60 BPM: Ex 7.4
Next, take notice of what voice (hi-hat; snare drum or bass drum) lands on each downbeat: Switch your counting from I6th-note subdivision to quarter-note subdivision (count out loud on every downbeat: "1-2-3-4". Ex 7.5

Chapter 2: Hand-Foot Patterns The second part of this system is playing patterns between the hands and bass drum: In order to develop relaxed, smooth flow of notes using these three limbs, you must be very careful of two main aspects: 1) balance of sound between the hands and foot,and 2) precise note placement: The patterns are organized by note grouping in the following manner:
Two-note patterns one on the hands and one on the foot: Ex 8.13
Three-note patterns two on the hands and one on the foot; or one in the hands and two on the foot. Ex 8.14
Four-note patterns two on the hands and two on the foot Ex 8.15
Five-note patterns three on the hands and two on the foot; Ex 8.16 T 3

Unit & Chapter
UnIt Ne reading | techNIque perfOrMance Chapter 1: Displaced Grooves The ability to play a specific sequence of notes and shift the pattern by one eighth note or 16th note before or after the beat will offer exciting possibilities in your rhythmic vocabulary. Initially, these ideas will challenge your mind or"ear" more than the physical component Becoming comfortable hearing certain phrases that begin and end in unfamiliar places within the time will unlock certain rhythmic plateaus and give you a fresh understanding of what is possible with beat placement and its affects on music By displacing beats, we can create the illusion that the time has temporarily shifted to an "odd-meter" However; by knowing where you are within the time and being able to resolve the displaced beat will guarantee the meter has not changed; this why counting is essential when practicing and performing this concept: KNOW WHERE YOU AREI
All of the following exercises are four-bar phrases The first two bars will establish the basic groove sequence with the last two bars displacing it Pay attention to set-ups as you transition in and out of the displaced grooves:
Grooves displaced "after" one eighth note: Ex 9.1
Grooves displaced "before" one eighth note: Ex 9.2
7
Grooves displaced "after" one I6th note: Ex 9.3

Chapter 2: Implied Metric Modulation The concept of grouping a specific note value into odd-note phrases and then applying those phrases to grooves is referred to as"implied"metric modulation It is "implied"because we have not changed the original note value; although it may sound like we have: For example; grouping I6th notes in phrases of three may give the impression that eighth-note triplets are being played when, in reality; it's merely a repeating phrase of three I6th notes:
3/16 Implied Metric Modulation: Warm-Ups The following exercises are designed to prepare your ear to hear how the three-note phrases resolve after three bars; these would be considered "over-the-bar" phrases It is beyond imperative that you count out loud while playing through these exercises Counting will reinforce the unique nature of these rhythmic concepts Ex 9.6

Chapter 3: Metric Modulation Similar to implied metric modulation_but very different in terms of rhythmic composition_metric modulation establishes a set pattern in one note value and then applies that same pattern to a different note value: This is a very effective technique in creating an illusion of time speeding up or slowing down. Just as we have learned with the previous two concepts, counting is imperative here.

Uhitt'3- Chapter ]
Unit ten reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Developing an Ostinato-Based Drum Solo As drummers, we play ostinatos all of the time, primarily with our hi-hat stepping pattern and our ride cymbal pattern. The main reason for playing an ostinato is to establish a constant: something to which the listener can quickly understand This is imperative because once the drummer begins adding variations and improvises over (or along with) the ostinato, the listener will have a"home base" they can relate to. From Max Roach to John Bonham; solo-based ostinatos have been a very effective and exciting way to make a solo have a unique sound and/or characteristic But it wasn't until- Terry ' Bozzio began using this tool as a primary agent to compose highly sophisticated drum solos that this concept reached its full potential. The ostinatos that Bozzio was incorporating were very specialized and specific to his original drum compositions and his large setup. This paved the way to exploring the drum set as a solo instrument like never before and has influenced countless drummers to create without boundaries
Just as you've practiced groove-based ostinatos (.e. ride and hi-hat stepping patterns) we will use the same systematic methodology to develop improvisational freedom with this units solo-based ostinato.
The Ostinato To begin, we will voice the ostinato with the bass drum and the small tom: The bass drum is playing a standard samba-esque rhythm and the left hand is "filling in" the"e" and"&"on the small tom. Ex 10.1
Right Hand Development Exercises with Ostinato The following are all ofthe standard 16th-note variations your right hand will be playing against the ostinato. For now, we will use the floor tom to play these patterns Ex 10.2 Basic Rhythm Combined with Ostinato
3 XIE

--- SOURCE: IMG_1451.PNG ---
Chapter 2: Drum Soloing Concepts Drum solos are a chance for the drummer to shine; be creative and add excitement to a piece of music: When taking a drum solo it is very easy to get overwhelmed with ideas, therefore you must think ahead and have a seed of an idea to start your solo. You can then develop your idea in similar fashion to the concepts presented in this book (compression/expansion; over-the-bar ideas, etc ).
One way to build your own repertoire of"go to"ideas is to research past drummers""go to"ideas. Drummers such as Philly Joe Jones, Steve Gadd, Tony Williams, John Bonham, Neil Peart and Steve Jordan have provided the drumming community with classic concepts that are essential to the development of great drum soloing:
Drum solos can be categorized as follows: The "open" solo: An extended, unaccompanied drum solo that typically changes tempos and has rubato (free-form, out-of-time) sections Soloing over the form: Common in the jazz idiom, this style of solo follows the form (AABA, ABAB, Blues; etc) of the tune just as a melodic instrumentalist would: Soloing over a rhythmic vamp: A drum solo over a set of ensemble figures, usually in a two or four-bar pattern: Groove-based solo: This drum solo is primarily a groove with variations and fill ideas either unaccompanied or over a bass line:
Examples of Each Solo Style
Open Solos Open solos were very popular in the classic rock era of the 1970s and 1980s. Drummers such as John Bonham; lan Paice; Carl Palmer and Neil Peart are commonly associated with this style of soloing: Below are few brief highlights from this era. John Bonham uses nice phrasing in this 32nd-note lick featuring doubles on a single bass drum, as follows: Ex 10.10
Bonham then plays a triplet-based idea that was originally played by Elvin Jones For example: Ex 10.11
F
E2
Neil Peart also pays homage to the early jazz masters by "borrowing" this idea from Art Blakey: Note: Billy Cobham got a lot of mileage out ofthis idea as well: Ex 10.12

Unit 10 - Chapter 2
Soloing over the Form Soling over the form of a tune is a staple for jazz music. In this concept; the drummer must be aware of the form at all times Many drummers in the jazz era such as Art Blakey; Max Roach and Elvin Jones would use the melody of the tune for rhythmic ideas, masterfully incorporating them into their solos.
Philly Joe Jones would use a variety of approaches when trading fours with the other ensemble members For example; these are all ideas from just a few choruses of the same tune: Ex 10.13
3
3 7 3
3
3
3
4
Soloing over a Rhythmic Vamp Soloing over vamp was made popular by jazz crossover guitarists such as John Scofield and Alan Holdsworth: Drummers like Dennis Chambers, Steve Gadd, Tony Williams and Steve Jordan have recorded timeless solos over vamps These solos are especially challenging due to the fact that ensemble figures are present: A drummer must play the figures while soloing in between, around and over the figures:
Steve Gadd provides solid ideas that work in all genres. For example; here are a few ideas he uses to set up the"&"of beat 4 Ex 10.14
7 L L   R L R L L   R L   R
L L   R L   R

Tony Williams uses a doubles stop between the right hand on the snare drum and the right foot on the bass drum to create a thick attack (Neil Peart also "borrowed"this from Tony) , as follows: Ex 10.15
F FFEEF 3| =ZEE3F R R L R L R R L R L R L R R R L R L R R R R R L R L R R
3 R L R L R R R L R L R R R R L R L R R  R R
Tony would also play melodically by using the right hand to travel around the toms while the left hand on the snare drum filled in all other notes: For example: Ex 10.16
FEEEEEE 1EE EEEEEXEE R L L R L L R L R L L R L L R L R L L R L L R L R R L L R L R L
JAESTAZZEZDZEEEEEEE R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R L R R L L R L L R L R L L R L L R
FZEZEDZZZEDZEZEZ ZEZ L R L L R L L R L R L L R L L R L R L L R L L R L R L L R L L R

Groove-Based Solos Groove solos are great to use in rock and funk genres where the groove just cannot be interrupted by drumming calisthenics Drummers such as Steve Jordan use a minimalistic approach and usually limit their orchestration to just bass drum; snare drum and cymbals Ex 10.17

About the Authors
Stewart Jean was born and raised in Deptford, New Jersey, where he was introduced to music through his father's love of classical music and his friends' Led Zeppelin LPs. Stewart's passion for music grew as he studied drumset; timpani and mallets in high school. Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician:
lnuug
At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree; Stewart became a sought-after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings, tours and shows.
After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles; maintaining steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the- point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists' lists.
Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years, Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves, Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards.
92 Drum Technique 4

Jeff Bowders began his journey of drumming in 1987. Since then; he has been blessed with the opportunity to perform and/or teach in over 50 countries Whether it is backing artist such as Paul Gilbert; Puddle of Mudd, Shakira and host of  others, or performing as a solo artist at drum festivals and camps, Jeff's passion for drums and drumming is undeniable: An in-demand educator at Musicians Institute in Los Angeles since 2003, Jeff's critically acclaimed MI Press Hal Leonard Publications include Essential Rock Drumming Concepts book/CD; Double Bass Drumming-The  Mirrored Groove System book/CD; Essential Drumset Fills-The Component Rhythm System book/CD and Double BassDrumming Workshop DVD. Many of the concepts from Jeff's publications, including those in this book; can be heard on Jeff's solo album The Pilgrimage of Thingamuhjig:
Jeff is honored to be endorsed by these amazing companies: Tama Drums and Hardware; Sabian Cymbals, Vater Drumsticks and Evans Drumheads: wwWieffbowderscom

Since moving to LA. in 2003, Kevin Stevens has played with the house band (Cleto and the Cletones) for "Jimmy Kimmel Live" performed with Shakira on The Ellen DeGeneres Show" toured the globe with the likes of Chilean pop superstar Beto Cuevas (La Ley), the critically acclaimed Neon Neon, and actress/singer Minnie Driver Kevin was a member of the original cast for the hit Broadway musical/movie Rock   of   Ages and is also the leader/founder of the internationally renowned funk/jazz combo Brass Monkey Brass Band. Kevin is graduate of the acclaimed University of Miami jazz department with a BA in Studio Music and Jazz Performance. Since arriving at Musicians Institute in 2009, Kevin has been known as one of the instructors to go to in order to get your groove together: Kevin teaches Private Lessons, Drumset Performance, Music Minus One,   along with New Orleans Drumming a class which he created for Ml which covers the history ofthe drum set and the role of the instrument in jazz, R&B, rock & roll, funk and beyondl
